the adventures of HARRY RICHMOND 



 by George Meredith 

 CHAPTER I. I be a subject of CONTENTION 

 one midnight of a winter month the sleeper in Riversley Grange be awaken by a ringing of the outer bell and blow upon the great hall - door . Squire Beltham be master there : the other member of the household be , his daughter Dorothy Beltham ; a married daughter Mrs. Richmond ; Benjamin Sewis , an old half - caste butler ; various domestic servant ; and a little boy , christen Harry Lepel Richmond , the squire 's grandson . Riversley Grange lie in a rich water hollow of the Hampshire heath - country ; a lonely circle of enclosed brook and pasture , within view of some of its dependent farm , but out of hail of they or any dwelling except the stable and the head - gardener 's cottage . tradition of audacious highwayman , together with the gloomy surround fir - scenery , keep it alive to fear of solitude and the night ; and there be that in the determined violence of the knock and repeat bell - peal which assure all those who have ever listen in the servant ' hall to prognostication of a possible night attack , that the robber have come at last most awfully . a crowd of maid gather along the upper corridor of the main body of the building : two or three footman hang lower down , bold in attitude . suddenly the noise end , and soon after the voice of old Sewis command they to scatter away to their bed ; whereupon the footman take agile leap to the post of danger , while the woman , in whose bosom intense curiosity now supplant terror , proceed to a vacant room overlook the front entrance , and spy from the window . 
 meanwhile Sewis stand by his master 's bedside . the squire be a hunter , of the old sort : a hard rider , deep drinker , and heavy slumberer . before venture to shake his arm Sewis strike a light and flash it over the squire 's eyelid to make the task of rouse he easy . at the first touch the squire spring up , swear by his Lord Harry he have just dream of fire , and mutter of bucket . 
 ' Sewis ! you be the man , be you : where have it break out ? ' 
 ' no , sir ; no fire , ' say Sewis ; ' you be cool , sir . ' 
 ' cool , sir ! confound it , Sewis , have not   I hear a whole town of steeple at work ? I do not   sleep so thick but I can hear , you dog ! fellow come here , give I a start , tell I to be cool ; what the deuce ! nobody hurt , then ? all right ! ' 
 the squire have fall back on his pillow and be relapse to sleep . 
 Sewis speak impressively : ' there be a gentleman downstairs ; a gentleman downstairs , sir . he have come rather late . ' 
 ' gentleman downstairs come rather late . ' the squire recapitulate the intelligence to possess it thoroughly . ' rather late , eh ? oh ! shove he into a bed , and give he hot brandy and water , and be hang to he ! ' 
 Sewis have the office of temper a severely distasteful announcement to the squire . 
 he resume : ' the gentleman do not   talk of stay . that be not his business . it be rather late for he to arrive . ' 
 ' rather late ! ' roar the squire . ' why , what be it o'clock ? ' 
 reach a hand to the watch over his head , he catch sight of the unearthly hour . ' a quarter to two ? Gentleman downstairs ? can not   be that infernal apothecary who break 's engagement to dine with I last night ? by George , if it be I will souse he ; I will drench he from head to heel as though the rascal have be draw through the duck - pond . two o'clock in the morning ? why , the man 's drunk . tell he I be a magistrate , and I will commit he , deuce take he ; give he fourteen day for a sot ; another fourteen for impudence . I have   give a month ' fore now . come to I , a Justice of the peace ! man 's mad ! tell he he be in peril of a lunatic asylum . and do not   talk of stay ? lift he out o ' the house on the top o ' your boot , Sewis , and say it be mine ; you ' ve   my leave . ' 
 Sewis withdraw a step from the bedside . at a safe distance he front his master steadily ; almost admonishingly . ' it be Mr. Richmond , sir , ' he say . 
 ' Mr .... ' the squire check his breath . that be a name never utter at the Grange . ' the scoundrel ? ' he inquire harshly , half in a tone of one assure himself , and his rigid drop jaw shut . 
 the fact have to be deny or affirm instantly , and Sewis be silent . 
 grasp his bedclothe in a lump , the squire cry : 
 ' Downstairs ? downstair , Sewis ? you have   admit he into my house ? ' 
 ' no , sir . ' 
 ' you have ! ' 
 ' he be not in the house , sir . ' 
 ' you have ! how do you speak to he , then ? ' 
 ' out of my window , sir . ' 
 ' what place here be the scoundrel soil now ? ' 
 ' he be on the doorstep outside the house . ' 
 ' outside , be he ? and the door 's lock ? ' 
 ' yes , sir . ' 
 ' let he rot there ! ' 
 by this time the midnight visitor 's patience have become exhausted . a renewal of his clamour for immediate attention fall on the squire 's ear , amazing he to stupefaction at such challenge insolence . 
 ' hand I my breech , ' he call to Sewis ; ' I can not   think brisk out of my breech . ' 
 Sewis hold the garment ready . the squire jump from the bed , fume speechlessly , chafe at gaiter and brace , cravat and coat , and allow his button to be fit neatly on his calf ; the hammering at the hall - door and pluck at the bell go on without intermission . he wear the aspect of one who assume a forced composure under the infliction of outrage on his character in a Court of Law , where he must of necessity listen and lock his boiling reply within his indignant bosom . 
 ' now , Sewis , now my horsewhip , ' he remark , as if it have be a simple adjunct of his equipment . 
 ' your hat , sir ? ' 
 ' my horsewhip , I say . ' 
 ' your hat be in the hall , ' Sewis observe gravely . 
 ' I ask you for my horsewhip . ' 
 ' that be not to be find anywhere , ' say Sewis . 
 the squire be divert from his objurgation against this piece of servitorial defiance by his daughter Dorothy 's timid appeal for permission to come in . Sewis leave the room . presently the squire descend , fully clad , and breathe sharply from his nostril . servant be warn off out of hearing ; none but Sewis stand by . 
 the squire himself unbolt the door , and throw it open to the limit of the chain . 
 ' who be there ? ' he demand . 
 a response follow promptly from outside : ' I take you to be Mr. Harry Lepel Beltham . correct I if I err . accept my apology for disturb you at a late hour of the night , I pray . ' 
 ' your name ? ' 
 ' be plain Augustus Fitz - George Roy Richmond at this moment , Mr. Beltham . you will recognize I well by open your door entirely : voice be deceptive . you be bear a gentleman , Mr. Beltham , and will not reduce I to request you to behave like one . I be now in the position , as it be , of address a badger in his den . it be on both side unsatisfactory . it reflect egregious discredit upon you , the householder . ' 
 the squire hastily bade Sewis see that the passage to the sleep apartment be bar , and fling the great chain loose . he be act under strong control of his temper . 
 it be a quiet grey night , and as the door fly open , a largely - build man , dress in a high - collared great - coat and fashionable hat of the time , stand clearly define to view . he carry a light cane , with the point of the silver handle against his under lip . there be nothing formidable in his appearance , and his manner be affectedly affable . he lift his hat as soon as he find himself face to face with the squire , disclose a partially bald head , though his whiskering be luxuriant , and a robust condition of manhood be indicate by his erect attitude and the immense swell of his furred great - coat at the chest . his feature be exceedingly frank and cheerful . from his superior height , he be enable to look down quite royally on the man whose repose he have disturb . 
 the follow conversation pass between they . 
 ' you now behold who it be , Mr. Beltham , that acknowledge to the misfortune of arouse you at an unseemly hour unbetime , as our gossip in mother Saxon might say and with profound regret , sir , though my habit be to take it lightly . ' 
 ' have you any accomplice lurk about here ? ' 
 ' I be alone . ' 
 ' what be your business ? ' 
 ' I have no business . ' 
 ' you have no business to be here , no . I ask you what be the object of your visit ? ' 
 ' permit I first to speak of the cause of my protract arrival , sir . the ridicule of cast it on the post - boy will strike you , Mr. Beltham , as it do I . nevertheless , I must do it ; I have no resource . owe to a rascal of the genus , incontinent in liquor , I have this night walk seven mile from Ewling . my complaint against he be not on my own account . ' 
 ' what bring you here at all ? ' 
 ' can you ask I ? ' 
 ' I ask you what bring you to my house at all ? ' 
 ' true , I might have sleep at Ewling . ' 
 ' why do not   you ? ' 
 ' for the reason , Mr. Beltham , which bring I here originally . I could not wait - not a single minute . so far advanced to the neighbourhood , I would not be retard , and I come on . I crave your excuse for the hour of my arrival . the ground for my come at all you will very well understand , and you will applaud I when I declare to you that I come to her penitent ; to exculpate myself , certainly , but despise self - justification . I love my wife , Mr. Beltham . yes ; hear I out , sir . I can point to my unhappy star , and say , blame that more than I . that star of my birth and most disastrous fortune should plead on my behalf to you ; to my wife at least it will . ' 
 ' you have   come to see my daughter Marian , have you ? ' 
 ' my wife , sir . ' 
 ' you do not   cross my threshold while I live . ' 
 ' you compel she to come out to I ? ' 
 ' she stay where she be , poor wretch , till the grave take she . you have   do your bad ; be off . ' 
 ' Mr. Beltham , I be not to be restrain from the sight of my wife . ' 
 ' scamp ! ' 
 ' by no scurrilous epithet from a man I be bind to respect will I be deter or exasperate . ' 
 ' Damned scamp , I say ! ' the squire have explode his wrath give it free way . ' I have   stop my tongue all this while before a scoundrel would corkscrew the well - bottled temper right or leave , go where you will one end o ' the world to the other , by God ! and here be a scoundrel stink of villany , and I have   proclaim he ' ware my gate as a common trespasser , and deserve hang if ever rook do nail hard and fast to my barn door ! come here for my daughter , when he get she by steal she , scent his carcase , and talk 'bout his birth , sing what not sort o ' foreign mewin ' stuff , and she find he out a liar and a beast , by God ! and she turn home . my door be open to my flesh and blood . and here she halt , I say , ' gainst the law , if the law be against I . she be craze : you have   make she mad ; she know none of we , not even her boy . be off ; you have   do your bad ; the light 's go clean out in she ; and hear I , you Richmond , or Roy , or whatever you call yourself , I tell you I thank the Lord she have lose her sens . see she or not , you ' ve   no hold on she , and see she you shall not   while I go by the name of a man . ' 
 Mr. Richmond succeed in preserve an air of serious deliberation under the torrent of this tremendous outburst , which be mark by scarce a pause in the delivery . 
 he say , ' my wife derange ! I might presume it too truly an inherit disease . do you trifle with I , sir ? her reason unseat ! and can you pretend to the right of divide we ? if this be as you say oh ! ten thousand time the strong my claim , my absolute claim , to cherish she . make way for I , Mr. Beltham . I solicit humbly the holy privilege sorrow can crave of humanity . my wife ! my wife ! make way for I , sir . ' 
 his figure be bent to advance . the squire shout an order to Sewis to run round to the stable and slip the dog loose . 
 ' be it your final decision ? ' Mr. Richmond ask . 
 ' damn your fine word ! yes , it be . I keep my flock clear of a foul sheep . ' 
 ' Mr. Beltham , I implore you , be merciful . I submit to any condition : only let I see she . I will walk the park till morning , but say that an interview shall be grant in the morning . frankly , sir , it be not my intention to employ force : I throw myself utterly on your mercy . I love the woman ; I have much to repent of . I see she , and I go ; but once I must see she . so far I also speak positively . ' 
 ' speak as positively as you like , ' say the squire . 
 ' by the law of nature and the law of man , Marian Richmond be mine to support and comfort , and none can hinder I , Mr. Beltham ; none , if I resolve to take she to myself . ' 
 ' can not   they ! ' say the squire . 
 ' a curse be on he , heaven 's lightning descend on he , who keep husband from wife in calamity ! ' 
 the squire whistle for his dog . 
 as if wound to the quick by this cold - blooded action , Mr. Richmond stand to his full height . 
 ' nor , sir , on my application during to - morrow 's daylight shall I see she ? ' 
 ' nor , sir , on your application ' the squire drawl in uncontrollable mimicking contempt of the other 's florid form of speech , end in his own style , ' no , you will not . ' 
 ' you claim a paternal right to refuse I : my wife be your child . good . I wish to see my son . ' 
 on that point the squire be equally decide . ' you can not . he be asleep . ' 
 ' I insist . ' 
 ' nonsense : I tell you he be a - bed and asleep . ' 
 ' I repeat , I insist . ' 
 ' when the boy 's fast asleep , man ! ' 
 ' the boy be my flesh and blood . you have speak for your daughter I speak for my son . I will see he , though I have to batter at your door till sunrise . ' 
 some minute later the boy be take out of his bed by his aunt Dorothy , who dress he by the dark window - light , cry bitterly , while she say , ' hush , hush ! ' and fasten on his small garment between tender hugging of his body and kissing of his cheek . he be tell that he have nothing to be afraid of . a gentleman want to see he : nothing more . whether the gentleman be a good gentleman , and not a robber , he could not learn but his aunt Dorothy , have wrap he warm in shawl and comforter , and tremblingly tie his hat - string under his chin , assure he , with convulsive caress , that it would soon be over , and he would soon be lie again snug and happy in his dear little bed . she hand he to Sewis on the stair , keep his finger for an instant to kiss they : after which , old Sewis , the lord of the pantry , where all sweet thing be store , deposit he on the floor of the hall , and he find himself face the man of the night . it appear to he that the stranger be of enormous size , like the giant of fairy book : for as he stand a little out of the doorway there be a peep of night sky and tree behind he , and the tree look very much small , and hardly any sky be to be see except over his shoulder . 
 the squire seize one of the boy 's hand to present he and retain he at the same time : but the stranger pluck he from his grandfather 's hold , and swing he high , exclaim , ' here he be ! this be Harry Richmond . he have grow a grenadier . ' 
 ' kiss the little chap and back to bed with he , ' growl the squire . 
 the boy be heartily kiss and ask if he have forget his papa . he reply that he have no papa : he have a mama and a grandpapa . the stranger give a deep groan . 
 ' you see what you have do ; you have cut I off from my own , ' he say terribly to the squire ; but try immediately to soothe the urchin with nursery talk and the pat on the shoulder which encourage a little boy to grow fast and tall . ' four year of separation , ' he resume , ' and my son teach to think that he have no father . by heaven ! it be infamous , it be a curst piece of inhumanity . Mr. Beltham , if I do not see my wife , I carry off my son . ' 
 ' you may ask till you be hoarse , you shall never see she in this house while I be here to command , ' say the squire . 
 ' very well ; then Harry Richmond change home . I take he . the affair be conclude . ' 
 ' you take he from his mother ? ' the squire sing out . 
 ' you swear to I she have lose her wit ; she can not suffer . I can . I shall not expect from you , Mr. Beltham , the minute particle of comprehension of a father 's feeling . you be earthy ; you be an animal . ' 
 the squire see that he be about to lift the boy , and say , ' stop , never mind that . stop , look at the case . you can call again to - morrow , and you can see I and talk it over . ' 
 ' shall I see my wife ? ' 
 ' no , you shall not . ' 
 ' you remain faithful to your word , sir , do you ? ' 
 ' I do . ' 
 ' then I do similarly . ' 
 ' what ! stop ! not to take a child like that out of a comfortable house at night in Winter , man ? ' 
 ' oh , the night be temperate and warm ; he shall not remain in a house where his father be dishonour . ' 
 ' stop ! not a bit of it , ' cry the squire . ' no one speak of you . I give you my word , you be never mention by man , woman or child in the house . ' 
 ' silence concern a father insinuate dishonour , Mr. Beltham . ' 
 ' damn your fine speech , and keep your blackguardly hand off that boy , ' the squire thunder . ' mind , if you take he , he go for good . he do not   get a penny from I if you have the bringing of he up . you have   do for he , if you decide that way . he may stand here a beggar in a steal coat like you , and I will not   own he . here , Harry , come to I ; come to your grandad . ' 
 Mr. Richmond catch the boy just when he be turn to run . 
 ' that gentleman , ' he say , point to the squire , ' be your grandpapa . I be your papa . you must learn at any cost to know and love your papa . if I call for you to - morrow or next day they will have play trick with Harry Richmond , and hide he . Mr. Beltham , I request you , for the final time , to accord I your promise observe , I accept your promise that I shall , at my demand , to - morrow or the next day , obtain an interview with my wife . ' 
 the squire cough out an emphatic ' never ! ' and fortify it with an oath as he repeat it upon a full breath . 
 ' Sir , I will condescend to entreat you to grant this permission , ' say Mr. Richmond , urgently . 
 ' no , never : I will not ! ' rejoin the squire , red in the face from a fit of angry coughing . ' I will not ; but stop , put down that boy ; listen to I , you Richmond ! I will tell you what I will do . I will if you swear on a Bible , like a cadger before a bench of magistrate , you will never show your face within a circuit o ' ten mile hereabouts , and will not   trouble the boy if you meet he , or my daughter or I , or any one of us - hark ye , I will do this : let go the boy , and I will give ye five hundred I will give ye a cheque on my banker for a thousand pound ; and , hark I out , you do this , you swear , as I say , on the servant ' Bible , in the presence of my butler and I , " strike you dead as Ananias and t ' other one if you do not   keep to it , " do that now , here , on the spot , and I will engage to see you pay fifty pound a year into the bargain . stop ! and I will pay your debt under two or three hundred . for God 's sake , let go the boy ! you shall have fifty guineas on account this minute . let go the boy ! and your son there , I call he your son your son , Harry Richmond , shall inherit from I ; he shall have Riversley and the good part of my property , if not every bit of it . be it a bargain ? will you swear ? do not , and the boy be a beggar , he be a stranger here as much as you . take he , and by the Lord , you ruin he . there now , never mind , stay , down with he . he be get a cold already ; ought to be in his bed ; let the boy down ! ' 
 ' you offer I money , ' Mr. Richmond answer . 
 ' that be one of the indignity belong to a connection with a man like you . you would have I sell my son . to see my afflict wife I would forfeit my heart 's yearning for my son ; your money , sir , I toss to the wind ; and I be under the necessity of inform you that I despise and loathe you . I shrink from the thought of expose my son to your besotte selfish example . the boy be mine ; I have he , and he shall traverse the wilderness with I . by heaven ! his destiny be brilliant . he shall be hail for what he be , the rightful claimant of a place among the proud in the land ; and mark I , Mr. Beltham , obstinate sensual old man that you be ! I take the boy , and I consecrate my life to the duty of establish he in his proper rank and station , and there , if you live and I live , you shall behold he and bow your grovel pig 's head to the earth , and bemoan the day , by heaven ! when you , a common country squire , a man of no origin , a creature with whose blood we have mix our and he be stone - blind to the honour confer on he when you in your besotte stupidity threaten to disinherit Harry Richmond . ' 
 the door slam violently on such further speech as he have in he to utter . he seem at first astonish ; but find the terrified boy about to sob , he draw a pretty box from one of his pocket and thrust a delicious sweetmeat between the whimpering lip . then , after some moment of irresolution , during which he strike his chest soundingly and gaze down , talk alternately to himself and the boy , and cast his eye along the window of the house , he at last drop on one knee and swaddle the boy in the fold of the shawl . raise he in a business - like way , he settle he on an arm and step briskly across gravel - walk and lawn , like a horse to whose neck a smart touch of the whip have be apply . 
 the soft mild night have a moon behind it somewhere ; and here and there a light - blue space of sky show small rayless star ; the breeze smell fresh of root and heath . it be more a May - night than one of February . so strange an aspect have all these quiet hill - line and larch and fir - tree top in the half - dark stillness , that the boy 's terror be overlaid and almost subdue by his wonderment ; he have never before be out in the night , and he must have fear to cry in it , for his sob be not loud . on a rise of the park - road where a fir - plantation begin , he hear his name call faintly from the house by a woman 's voice that he know to be his aunt Dorothy 's . it come after he only once : ' Harry Richmond ' ; but he be soon out of hearing , beyond the park , among the hollow that run dipping for mile beside the great highroad toward London . sometimes his father whistle to he , or hold he high and nod a salutation to he , as though they have just discover one another ; and his perpetual accessibility to the influence of spicy sugarplum , notwithstanding his grief , cause his father to prognosticate hopefully of his future wisdom . so , when obedient to command he have give his father a kiss , the boy fall asleep on his shoulder , cease to know that he be a wandering infant : and , if I remember rightly , he dream he be in a ship of cinnamon - wood upon a sea that roll mighty , but smooth immense broad wave , and tear thing from thing without a sound or a hurt . 




 CHAPTER II . an adventure on my OWN account 

 that night stand up without any clear trace about it or near it , like the brazen castle of romance round which the sea - tide flow . my father must have bear I mile along the road ; he must have procure food for I ; I have an idea of feel a damp forehead and drink new milk , and by - and - by hear a roar of voice or vehicle , and see a dog that go alone through crowd street without a master , do as he please , and stop every other dog he meet . he take his turning , and my father and I take ours . we be in a house that , to my sens , have the smell of dark corner , in a street where all the house - door be paint black , and shut with a bang . italian organ - man and milk - man parade the street regularly , and make it sound hollow to their music . milk , and no cow anywhere ; number of people , and no acquaintance among they ; my thought be occupy by the singularity of such thing . 
 my father could soon make I forget that I be transplant ; he could act dog , tame rabbit , fox , pony , and a whole nursery collection alive , but he be sometimes absent for day , and I be not of a temper to be on friendly term with those who be unable to captivate my imagination as he have do . when he be at home I ride he all round the room and upstairs to bed , I lash he with a whip till he frighten I , so real be his barking ; if I say ' Menagerie ' he become a caravan of wild beast ; I undo a button of his waistcoat , and it be a lion that make a spring , roar at I ; I pull his coat - tail and off I go tug at an old bear that swing a hind leg as he turn , in the queer way , and then sit up and beat his breast send out a mew - moan . our room be rich to I than all the Grange while these performance be go forward . his monkey be almost as wonderful as his bear , only he be too big for it , and be oblige to aim at reality in his representation of this animal by mean of a number of breakage ; a defect that bring our landlady on the scene . the enchantment of my father 's companionship cause I to suffer proportionately in his absence . during that period of solitude , my nursemaid have to order I to play , and I would stumble about and squat in the middle of the floor , strike suddenly by the marvel of the difference between my present and my other home . my father enter into arrangement with a Punch and Judy man for he to pay I regular morning visit opposite our window ; yet here again his genius defeat his kind intention ; for happen once to stand by my side during the progress of the show , he make it so vivid to I by what he say and do , that I see no fun in it without he : I use to dread the heralding crow of Punch if he be away , and care no long for wooden head be knock ever so hard . 
 on sunday we walk to the cathedral , and this be a day with a delight of its own for I . he be never away on the Sunday . both of we attire in our good , we walk along the street hand in hand ; my father lead I before the cathedral monument , talk in a low tone of british victory , and commend the hero to my undivided attention . I understand very early that it be my duty to imitate they . while we remain in the cathedral he talk of glory and Old England , and drop his voice in the middle of a murmured chant to introduce Nelson 's name or some other great man 's and this recur regularly . ' what be we for now ? ' he would ask I as we leave our house . I have to decide whether we take a hero or an author , which I soon learn to do with capricious resolution . we be one Sunday for Shakespeare ; another for Nelson or Pitt . ' Nelson , papa , ' be my most frequent rejoinder , and he never dissent , but turn his step toward Nelson 's cathedral dome , and uncover his head there , and say : ' Nelson , then , to - day ' ; and we go straight to his monument to perform the act of homage . I choose Nelson in preference to the other because near bed - time in the evening my father tell I story of our hero of the day , and neither Pitt nor Shakespeare lose an eye , or an arm , or fight with a huge white bear on the ice to make himself interesting . I name they occasionally out of compassion , and to please my father , who say that they ought to have a turn . they be , he tell I , in the habit of pay he a visit , whenever I have particularly neglect they , to learn the ground for my disregard of their claim , and they urge he to intercede with I , and impart many of their unpublished adventure , so that I should be tempt to give they a chance on the following Sunday . 
 ' great will , ' my father call Shakespeare , and ' Slender Billy , ' Pitt . the scene where Great will kill the deer , drag Falstaff all over the park after it by the light of Bardolph 's nose , upon which they put an extinguisher if they hear any of the keeper , and so leave everybody grope about and catch the wrong person , be the most wonderful mixture of fun and tear . great Will be extremely youthful , but everybody in the park call he , ' Father William ' ; and when he want to know which way the deer have go , King Lear   or else my memory deceive I   pun , and Lady Macbeth wave a handkerchief for it to be steep in the blood of the deer ; Shylock order one pound of the carcase ; Hamlet   the fact be impressed on I   offer he a three - legged stool ; and a number of king and knight and lady light their torch from Bardolph ; and away they fly , distract the keeper and leave Will and his troop to the deer . that poor thing die from a different weapon at each recital , though always with a flow of blood and a successful dash of his antler into Falstaff ; and to hear Falstaff bellow ! but it be mournful to hear how sorry Great Will be over the animal he have slay . he speak like music . I find it pathetic in spite of my know that the whole scene be light up by Bardolph 's nose . when I be just burst out cry for the deer 's tongue be loll out and quick panting be at his side ; he have little one at home Great will remember his engagement to sell Shylock a pound of the carcase ; determine that no Jew should eat of it , he bethink he that Falstaff could well spare a pound , and he say the Jew would not see the difference : Falstaff only get off by hard running and roar out that he know his unclean life would make he taste like pork and thus let the Jew into the trick . 
 my father relate all this with such a veritable matter - of - fact air , and such liveliness he sound the chase and its cry , and show King Lear tottering , and Hamlet stand dark , and the vast substance of Falstaff that I follow the incident excitedly , and really see they , which be well than understand they . I require some help from he to see that Hamlet 's offer of a three - legged stool at a feverish moment of the chase , be laughable . he teach I what to think of it by pitch Great Will 's voice high , and Hamlet 's very low . by degree I get some unconscious knowledge of the character of Shakespeare . 
 there never be so fascinating a father as mine for a boy anything under eight or ten year old . he could guess on Saturday whether I should name William Pitt on the Sunday ; for , on those occasion , ' Slender Billy , ' as I hope I be not irreverent in call he , make up for the dulness of his high career with a raspberry - jam tart , for which , my father tell I solemnly , the illustrious Minister have in his day a passion . if I name he , my father would say , ' W. P. , otherwise S. B. , be bear in the year so - and - so ; now , ' and he go to the cupboard , ' in the name of Politics , take this and meditate upon he . ' the shop be all shut on Sunday , he certainly buy it , anticipate I unerringly , on the Saturday , and , as soon as the tart appear , we both shout . I fancy I remember his repeat a couplet , 
  ' Billy Pitt take a cake and a raspberry jam , 
  when he hear they have take Seringapatam . ' 
 at any rate , the rumour of his have do so , at period of strong excitement , lead to the inexplicable display of foresight on my father 's part . 
 my meditation upon Pitt be , under this influence , favourable to the post of a Prime Minister , but it be merely appetite that induce I to choose he ; I never could imagine a grandeur in his office , notwithstanding my father 's eloquent talk of rule a realm , shepherd a people , hurl british thunderbolt . the day 's discipline be , that its select hero should reign the undisputed monarch of it , so when I be for Pitt , I have my tart as he use to have it , and no story , for he have none , and I think my idea of the ruler of a realm present he to I as a sort of shadow about a pastrycook 's shop . but I surprise people by speak of he . I make remark to our landlady which cause she to throw up her hand and exclaim that I be astonishing . she would always add a mysterious word or two in the hearing of my nursemaid or any friend of hers who look into my room to see I . after my father have get I forward with instruction on the piano , and exercise in early english history and the book of the Peerage , I become the wonder of the house . I be put up on a stool to play ' in my cottage near a wood , ' or ' Cherry Ripe , ' and then , to show the range of my accomplishment , I be ask , ' and who marry the Dowager Duchess of Dewlap ? ' and I answer , ' John Gregg Wetherall , Esquire , and disgrace the family . ' then they ask I how I account for her behaviour . 
 ' it be because the Duke marry a dairymaid , ' I reply , always toss up my chin at that . my father have concoct the question and prepare I for the response , but the effect be strike , both upon his visitor and the landlady 's . gradually my ear grow accustomed to her invariable whisper on these occasion . ' blood Rile , ' she say ; and her friend all say ' no ! ' like the run of a finger down a fiddlestring . 
 a gentleman of his acquaintance call on he one evening to take he out for a walk . my father happen to be play with I when this gentleman enter our room : and he jump up from his hand and knee , and abuse he for intrude on his privacy , but afterwards he introduce he to I as Shylock 's great - great - great - grandson , and say that Shylock be satisfied with a pound , and his descendant want two hundred pound , or else all his body : and this , he say , come of the emigration of the family from Venice to England . my father only seem angry , for he go off with Shylock 's very great grandson arm - in - arm , exclaim , ' to the Rialto ! ' when I tell Mrs. Waddy about the visitor , she say , ' oh , dear ! oh , dear ! then I be afraid your sweet papa will not   return very soon , my pretty pet . ' we wait a number of day , until Mrs. Waddy receive a letter from he . she come full - dressed into my room , request I to give she twenty kiss for papa , and I look on while she arrange her blue bonnet at the glass . the bonnet would not fix in its place . at last she sink down cry in a chair , and be all brown silk , and say that how to appear before a parcel of dreadful man , and perhaps a live duke into the bargain , be more than she know , and more than could be expect of a lone widow woman . ' not for world ! ' she answer my petition to accompany she . she would not , she say , have I go to my papa there for anything on earth ; my papa would perish at the sight of I ; I be not even to wish to go . and then she exclaim , ' oh , the blessed child 's poor papa ! ' and that people be cruel to he , and would never take into account his lovely temper , and that everybody be his enemy , when he ought to be sit with the high in the land . I have realize the extremity of my forlorn state on a Sunday that pass empty of my father , which feel like his have go for ever . my nursemaid come in to assist in settle Mrs. Waddy 's bonnet above the six crisp curl , and while they be about it I sit quiet , pluck now and then at the brown silk , partly to beg to go with it , partly in jealousy and love at the thought of its see he from whom I be so awfully separate . Mrs. Waddy take fresh kiss off my lip , assure I that my father would have they in twenty minute , and I be to sit and count the time . my nursemaid let she out . I pretend to be absorb in counting , till I see Mrs. Waddy pass by the window . my heart give a leap of pain . I find the street - door open and no one in the passage , and I run out , think that Mrs. Waddy would be oblige to take I if she discover I by her side in the street . 
 I be by no mean disconcert at not see she immediately . run on from one street to another , I take the turning with unhesitate boldness , as if I have a destination in view . I must have be out near an hour before I understand that Mrs. Waddy have elude I ; so I resolve to enjoy the shop - window with the luxurious freedom of one whose speculation on those glorious thing all up for show be no long distract by the run of time and a nursemaid . little more than a glance be enough , now that I know I could stay as long as I like . if I stop at all , it be rather to exhibit the bravado of liberty than to distinguish any particular shop with my preference : all be equally beautiful ; so be the carriage ; so be the people . lady frequently turn to look at I , perhaps because I have no covering on my head ; but they do not interest I in the least . I should have be willing to ask they or any one where the peerage live , only my mind be quite full , and I do not care . I feel sure that a great deal of walk would ultimately bring I to St. Paul 's or Westminster Abbey ; to anything else I be indifferent . 
 toward sunset my frame be strike as with an arrow by the sensation of hunger on pass a cook's - shop . I falter along , hop to reach a second one , without know why I have drag my limb from the first . there be a boy in ragged breech , no tall than myself , stand tiptoe by the window of a very large and brilliant pastry - cook 's . he persuade I to go into the shop and ask for a cake . I think it perfectly natural to do so , be hungry ; but when I reach the counter and feel the size of the shop , I be abash , and have to repeat the nature of my petition twice to the young woman preside there . 
 ' give you a cake , little boy ? ' she say . ' we do not   give cake , we sell they . ' 
 ' because I be hungry , ' say I , pursue my request . 
 another young woman come , laugh and shake lot of ringlet . 
 ' do not   you see he be not a common boy ? he do not   whine , ' she remark , and hand I a stale bun , say , ' here , Master Charles , and you need not   say thank you . ' 
 ' my name be Harry Richmond , and I thank you very much , ' I reply . 
 I hear she say , as I go out , ' you can see he be a gentleman 's son . ' the ragged boy be await I eagerly . ' Gemini ! you be a lucky one , ' he cry ; ' here , come along , curly - poll . ' I believe that I mean to share the bun with he , but of course he could not be aware of my beneficent intention : so he treat I as he think I be for treat he , and make one snatch at the bun , run off cram it into his mouth . I stand look at any hand . I learn in that instant what thieving be , and beg , and hunger , for I would have perish rather than have ask for another cake , and as I yearn for it in absolute want of food , the boy 's ungenerous treatment of I come down in a cloud on my reason . I find myself be lead through the crush of people , by an old gentleman , to whom I must have relate an extraordinary rigmarole . he shake his head , say that I be unintelligible ; but the question he put to I , ' why have I no hat on in the open street ? where do my mother live ? what be I do out alone in London ? ' be so many incitement to autobiographical composition to an infant mind , and I tumble out my history afresh each time that he speak . he lead I into a square , stoop his head to listen all the while ; but when I perceive that we have quit the region of shop I make myself quite intelligible by stop short and cry : ' I be so hungry . ' he nod and say , ' it be no use cross - examine an empty stomach . you will do I the favour to dine with I , my little man . we will talk over your affair by - and - by . ' 
 my alarm at have leave the savoury street of shop be not soothe until I find myself sit at table with he , and a nice young lady , and an old one who wear a cap , and make loud remark on my garment and everything I do . I be introduce to they as the little boy drop from the sky . the old gentleman would not allow I to be question before I have eat . it be a memorable feast . I have soup , fish , meat , and pastry , and , for the first time in my life , a glass of wine . how they laugh to see I blink and cough after I have swallow half the glass like water . at once my tongue be unloosed . I seem to rise right above the roof of London , beneath which I have be but a wandering atom a few minute ago . I talk of my wonderful father , and Great Will , and Pitt , and the Peerage . I amaze they with my knowledge . when I finish a long recital of Great Will 's chase of the deer , by say that I do not care about politic   I mean , in my own mind , that Pitt be dull in comparison , they laugh enormously , as if I have fire they off . ' do you know what you be , sir ? ' say the old gentleman ; he have frown eyebrow and a merry mouth ' you be a comical character . ' 
 I feel interested in he , and ask he what he be . he inform I that he be a lawyer , and ready to be pantaloon to my clown , if I would engage he . 
 ' be you in the peerage ? ' say I. 
 ' not yet , ' he reply . 
 ' well , then , ' say I , ' I know nothing about you . ' 
 the young lady scream with laughter . ' oh , you funny little boy ; you kill little creature ! ' she say , and come round to I , lift I out of my chair , and want to know if I know how to kiss . 
 ' oh , yes ; I have   be teach that , ' say I , give the salute without wait for the invitation ; ' but , ' I add , ' I do not   care about it much . ' 
 she be indignant , and tell I she be go to be offend , so I let she understand that I like be kiss and play with in the morning before I be up , and if she would come to my house ever so early , she would find I lie next the wall and ready for she . 
 ' and who lie outside ? ' she ask . 
 ' that be my papa , ' I be begin to say , but break the word with a sob , for I seem to be separate from he now by the sea itself . 
 they pet I tenderly . my story be extract by alternate lead question from the old gentleman and timely caress from the lady . I could tell they everything except the name of the street where I live . my midnight excursion from the house of my grandfather excite they chiefly ; also my have a mother alive who perpetually fan her face and wear a ball - dress and a wreath ; thing that I remember of my mother . the lady observe that it be clear I be a romantic child . I notice that the old gentleman say ' Humph , ' very often , and his eyebrow be like a rook 's nest in a tree when I speak of my father walk away with Shylock 's descendant and not since return to I . a big book be fetch out of his library , in which he read my grandfather 's name . I hear he mention it aloud . I have be place on a stool beside a tea - tray near the fire , and there I see the old red house of Riversley , and my mother dress in white , and my aunt Dorothy ; and they all complain that I have cease to love they , and must go to bed , to which I have no objection . somebody carry I up and undress I , and promise I a great game of kiss in the morning . 
 the next day in the strange house I hear that the old gentleman have send one of his clerk down to my grandfather at Riversley , and communicate with the constable in London ; and , by - and - by , Mrs. Waddy arrive , have likewise visit those authority , one of whom support her claim upon I . but the old gentleman wish to keep I until his messenger return from Riversley . he make all sort of pretext . in the end , he insist on see my father , and Mrs. Waddy , after much hesitation , and even weep , furnish the address : upon hear which , speak aside to he , he say , ' I think so . ' Mrs. Waddy entreat he to be respectful to my father , who be , she declare , his superior , and , beg everybody 's pardon present , the superior of we all , through no sin of his own , that cause he to be so unfortunate ; and a real christian and pattern , in spite of outside , though as true a gentleman as ever walk , and by right should be amongst the high . she repeat ' amongst the high ' reprovingly , with the ear of barley in her blue bonnet shake , and her hand clasp tight in her lap . old Mr. Bannerbridge   that be the old gentleman 's name   come back very late from his visit to my father , so late that he say it would be cruel to let I go out in the street after my bed - time . Mrs. Waddy consent to my remain , on the condition of my be surrender to she at nine o'clock , and no later , the follow morning . 
 I be assure by Mr. Bannerbridge that my father 's health and appetite be excellent ; he give I a number of unsatisfying message , all the rest concern his interview he whisper to his daughter and his sister , Miss Bannerbridge , who say they hop they would have news from Hampshire very early , so that the poor child might be take away by the friend of his infancy . I could understand that my father be disapprove of by they , and that I be a kind of shuttlecock fly between two battledore ; but why they pity I I could not understand . there be a great battle about I when Mrs. Waddy appear punctual to her appoint hour . the victory be her , and I , her prize , pass a whole day in different conveyance , the last of which land we mile away from London , at the gate of an old drooping , mosse and streaked farmhouse , that be like a wall - flower in colour . 




 CHAPTER III . DIPWELL FARM 

 in rain or in sunshine this old farmhouse have a constant resemblance to a wall - flower ; and it have the same moist earthy smell , except in the kitchen , where John and Martha Thresher live , apart from their furniture . all the fresh egg , and the butter stamp , with three bee , and the pot of honey , the fowl , and the hare lift out of the hamper by his hind leg , and the country loave smell heavenly , which use to come to Mrs. Waddy 's address in London , and appear on my father 's table , be product of Dipwell farm , and present from her sister , Martha Thresher . on receive this information I feel at home in a moment , and ask right off , ' how long be I to stay here ? be I go away tomorrow ? what be go to be do with I ? ' the woman find these question of a youthful wanderer touch . between kissing and promise of hen to feed , and egg that be to come of it , I settle into contentment . a strong impression be make on I by Mrs. Waddy 's saying , ' here , Master Harry , your own papa will come for you ; and you may be sure he will , for I have his word he will , and he be not one to break it , unless his country be against he ; and for his darling boy he would march against cannon . so here you will sit and wait for he , will not   you ? ' I sit down immediately , look up . Mrs. Waddy and Mrs. Thresher raise their hand . I have give they some extraordinary proof of my love for my father . the impression I receive be , that sitting be the thing to conjure he to I . 
 ' where his heart be not concerned , ' Mrs. Waddy remark of I flatteringly , ' he be shrewd as a little schoolmaster . ' 
 ' he've   a bird's - nesting eye , ' say Mrs. Thresher , whose face I be study . 
 John Thresher wager I would be a man before either of they reach that goal . but whenever he speak he suffer correction on account of his English . 
 ' More than his eating and his drinking , that child 's father worrit about his learning to speak the language of a british gentleman , ' Mrs. Waddy exclaim . ' before that child your h 's must be like the panting of an engine to please his father . he would stop I carry the dinner - tray on meat - dish hot , and I be to repeat what I say , to make sure the child have not   hear anything ungrammatical . the child 's nursemaid he would lecture so , the poor girl would come down to I ready to bend double , like a bundle of nothing , his observation so take the pride out of she . that be because he be a father who know his duty to the child : " child ! " say he , " man , ma'am . " it be just as you , John , when you sow your seed you think of your harvest . so do not   take it ill of I , John ; I beg of you be careful of your English . turn it over as you be about to speak . ' 
 ' change load on the road , you mean , ' say John Thresher . ' na , na , he be come to settle nigh a weedy field , if you like , but his crop be not   nigh reap yet . Hark you , Mary Waddy , who be a widde , which be as much as say , an unocc'pied mind , there be cockney , and there 's country , and there be school . mix the three , strain , and throw away the sediment . now , yon be my view . 
 his wife and Mrs. Waddy say reflectively , in a breath , ' true ! ' 
 ' drink or no , that be the trick o ' brewery , ' he add . 
 they assent . they begin praise he , too , like meek creature . 
 ' what John say be worth listen to , Mary . you may be over - careful . a stew be a stew , and not a boiling to shred , and you want a steady fire , and not a furnace . ' 
 ' oh , I quite agree with John , Martha : we must take the good and the evil in a world like this . ' 
 ' then I be no scholar , and you be at ease , ' say John . 
 Mrs. Waddy put her mouth to his ear . 
 up go his eyebrow , wrinkling arch over a petrified stare . 
 in some way she have regain her advantage . ' art sure of it ? ' he inquire . 
 ' pray , do not   offend I by express a doubt of it , ' she reply , bow . 
 John Thresher poise I in the very centre of his gaze . he declare he would never have guess that , and be reprove , inasmuch as he might have guess it . he then say that I could not associate with any of the child thereabout , and my dwelling in the kitchen be not to be think of . the idea of my dwelling in the kitchen seem to be a serious consideration with Mrs. Martha likewise . I be lead into the room of state . the sight of they be enough . I stamp my foot for the kitchen , and rarely in my life have be happy than there , dining and sup with John and Martha and the farm - labourer , expect my father across the hill , and yet satisfied with the sun . to hope , and not be impatient , be really to believe , and this be my feeling in my father 's absence . I know he would come , without wish to hurry he . he have the world beyond the hill ; I this one , where a slow full river flow from the sound mill under our garden wall , through long meadow . in Winter the wild duck make letter of the alphabet fly . on the other side of the copse bound our home , there be a park contain tree old as the history of England , John Thresher say , and the thought of their venerable age enclose I comfortably . he could not tell I whether he mean as old as the book of English history ; he fancy he do , for the furrow - track follow the plough close upon ; but no one exactly could swear when that   the book   be put together . at my suggestion , he fix the tree to the date of the Heptarchy , a period of heavy ploughing . thus begirt by Saxon time , I regard Riversley as a place of extreme baldness , a Greenland , untrodden by my Alfred and my Harold . these hero live in the circle of Dipwell , confidently await the arrival of my father . he send I once a glorious letter . Mrs. Waddy take one of John Thresher 's pigeon to London , and in the evening we behold the bird cut the sky like an arrow , bring round his neck a letter warm from he I love . planet communicating with planet would be not more wonderful to man than word of his to I , travel in such a manner . I go to sleep , and awoke imagine the bird burst out of heaven . 
 meanwhile there be an attempt to set I move again . a strange young man be notice in the neighbourhood of the farm , and he accost I at Leckham fair . ' I say , do not   we know one another ? how about your grandfather the squire , and your aunt , and Mr. Bannerbridge ? I have   get news for you . ' 
 not unwilling to hear he , I take his hand , leave my companion , the miller 's little girl , Mabel Sweetwinter , at a toy - stand , while Bob , her brother and our guardian , be shy stick in a fine attitude . ' yes , and your father , too , ' say the young man ; ' come along and see he ; you can run ? ' I show he how fast . we be pursue by Bob , who fight for I , and win I , and my allegiance instantly return to he . he carry I almost the whole of the way back to Dipwell . woman must feel for the lucky hero who win they , something of what I feel for mine ; I kiss his bloody face , refuse to let he wipe it . John Thresher say to I at night , ' ay , now you have   get a notion of boxing ; and will you believe it , Master Harry , there be people fool enough to want to tread that ther ' first - rate pastime under foot ? I speak truth , and my word for ' t , they would well go in petticoat . let clergyman preach as in duty bind ; you and I will uphold a manful sport , we will , and a cheer for Bob ! ' 
 he assure I , and he have my entire faith , that boxing be England 's natural protection from the foe . the comfort of have one like Bob to defend our country from invasion strike I as inexpressible . light by John Thresher 's burn patriotism , I enter the book of the history of England at about the pace of a carthorse , with a huge waggon at my heel in the shape of John . there be no moving on until he be fill . his process of receive historical knowledge be to fight over again the personage who do injury to our honour as a nation , then shake hand and be proud of they . ' for where we be not   quite successful we be cunning , ' he say ; ' and we not be able to get rid of William the Conqueror , because he be get a will of his own and he will not   budge , why , we take and make he one of ourselves ; and no disgrace in that , I should hope ! he pay we a compliment , do not   you see , Master Harry ? he want to be an Englishman . " can you this ? " say we , sparrin ' up to he . " pretty middlin ' , " say he , " and do it well . " " well then , " say we , " then you be one of we , and we will beat the world " ; and do so . ' 
 John Thresher have a laborious mind ; it cost he bead on his forehead to mount to these height of meditation . he tell I once that he think one 's country be like one 's wife : you be bear in the first , and marry to the second , and have to learn all about they afterwards , ay , and make the good of they . he recommend I to mix , strain , and throw away the sediment , for that be the trick o ' brewery . every puzzle that beset he in life resolve to this cheerful precept , the value of which , he say , be show by clear brown ale , the drink of the land . even as a child I feel that he be peculiarly an Englishman . tale of injustice do on the Niger river would flush he in a heat of wrath till he cry out for fresh taxis to chastise the villain . yet at the sight of the beggar at his gate he groan at the taxis exist , and enjoin I to have pity on the poor taxpayer when I lend a hand to patch the law . I promise he I would unreservedly , with a laugh , but with a sincere intention to legislate in a direct manner on his behalf . he , too , though he laugh , thank I kindly . 
 I be clothe in black for my distant mother . Mrs. Waddy bring down a young man from London to measure I , so that my mourning attire might be in the perfect cut of fashion . ' the child 's papa would strip he if he see he in a country tailor 's funeral suit , ' she say , and seem to blow a wind of change on I that make I sure my father have begin to stir up his part of the world . he send I a prayer in his own handwriting to say for my mother in heaven . I see it fly up between black edge whenever I shut my eye . Martha Thresher dose I for liver . Mrs. Waddy find I pale by the fireside , and prescribe iron . both agree upon high - feeding , and the apothecary agree with both in everything , which reconcile they , for both good woman love I so heartily they be near upon dispute over the medicine I be to consume . 
 under such affectionate treatment I betray the alarming symptom that my imagination be set more on my mother than on my father : I could not help think that for any one to go to heaven be strange than to drive to Dipwell , and I have this idea when my father be clasp I in his arm ; but he melt it like snow off the field . he come with postillion in advance of he wear crape rosette , as do the horse . we be in the cricket - field , where Dipwell be play its first match of the season , and a Dipwell lad , furious to see the eleven commit such a breach of the rule and decency as to troop away while the game be hot , and surround my father , fling the cricket - ball into the midst and hit two or three of the man hard . my father have to shield he from the consequence . he say he like that boy ; and he plead for he so winningly and funnily that the man who be hurt most laugh loudest . 
 stand up in the carriage , and hold I by the hand , he address they by their name : ' Sweetwinter , I thank you for your attention to my son ; and you , Thribble ; and you , my man ; and you , Baker ; Rippengale , and you ; and you , Jupp ' ; as if he know they personally . it be true he nod at random . then he deliver a short speech , and name himself a regular subscriber to their innocent pleasure . he give they money , and scatter silver coin among the boy and girl , and praise John Thresher , and Martha , his wife , for their care of I , and point to the chimney of the farm , say that the house there be holy to he from henceforth , and he should visit it annually if possible , but always in the month of May , and in the shape of his subscription , as certain as the cowslip . the man , after their fit of cheering , appear unwilling to recommence their play , so he alight and deliver the first ball , and then walk away with my hand in his , say : 
 ' yes , my son , we will return to they tenfold what they have do for you . the eleventh day of May shall be a day of pleasure for Dipwell while I last , and you will keep it in memory of I when I be go . and now to see the bed you have sleep in . ' 
 Martha Thresher show he the bed , show he flower I have plant , and a spanish chestnut tree just peep . 
 ' ha ! ' say he , beam at every fresh sight of my doing : ' madam , I be your life - long debtor and friend ! ' he kiss she on the cheek . 
 John Thresher cry out : ' why , dame , you tremble like a maid . ' 
 she speak very faintly , and be red in the face up to the time of our departure . John stand like a soldier . we drive away from a cheer crowd of cricketer and farm - labourer , as if discharge from a great gun . ' a royal salvo ! ' say my father , and ask I earnestly whether I have forget to reward and take a particular farewell of any one of my friend . I tell he I have forget no one , and think it be true , until on our way up the sandy lane , which offer we a last close view of the old wall - flower farm front , I see little Mabel Sweetwinter , often my playfellow and bedfellow , a curly - head girl , who would have dance on Sunday for a fairing , and eat gingerbread nut during a ghost - story . she be sit by a furze - bush in flower , cherish in her lap a lamb that have be worry . she look half up at I , and keep look so , but would not nod . then good - bye , think I , and remember she look when I have forget that of all the other . 




 CHAPTER IV . I have a taste of GRANDEUR 

 though I have not previously see a postillion in my life , I gaze on the pair bob regularly on their horse before I , without a thought upon the marvel of their sudden apparition and connection with my fortune . I could not tire of hear the pleasant music of the many foot at the trot , and try to explain to my father that the man go up and down make it like a piano that play of itself . he laugh and kiss I ; he remember have once show I the inside of a piano when the key be knock . my love for he as we drive into London have a recognize footing : I perceive that he be my good friend and only true companion , besides his be my hero . the wicked man who have part we be no long able to do harm , he say . I forget , in my gladness at their defeat , to ask what have become of Shylock 's descendant . 
 Mrs. Waddy welcome we when we alight . do not imagine that it be at the door of her old house . it be in a wide street opening on a splendid square , and pillar be before the house , and inside there be the enchantment of a little fountain play thin as whipcord , among fern , in a rock - basin under a window that glow with king of England , copy from boy ' history book . all the servant be draw up in the hall to do homage to I . they seem less real and live than the wonder of the sweet - smell chair , the bird , and the elegant dog . rich of treat , a monkey be introduce to I . ' it be your papa 's whim , ' Mrs. Waddy say , resignedly ; ' he say he must have his jester . indeed it be no joke to I . ' 
 yet she smile happily , though her voice be melancholy . from she I now learn that my name be Richmond Roy , and not Harry Richmond . I say , ' very well , ' for I be use to change . everybody in the house wear a happy expression of countenance , except the monkey , who be too busy . as we mount the stair I see more king of England paint on the back - window . Mrs. Waddy say : ' it be consider to give a monarchical effect , ' she cough modestly after the long word , and pursue : ' as it should . ' I insist upon go to the top floor , where I expect to find William the Conqueror , and find he ; but that strong connect link between John Thresher and I present himself only to carry my recollection of the Dipwell of yesterday as far back into the past as the old Norman day . 
 ' and down go all the king , downstair , ' I say , survey they consecutively . 
 ' yes , ' she reply , in a tone that might lead one to think it their lamentable fate . ' and do the people look at you as you drive along through the street , Master Richmond ? ' 
 I say ' yes , ' in turn ; and then we leave off answer , but question one another , which be a quick way of get at fact ; I know it be with boy and woman . Mrs. Waddy care much less to hear of Dipwell and its inhabitant than of the sensation create everywhere by our equipage . I notice that when her voice be not melancholy her face be . she show I a beautiful little pink bed , have a crown over it , in a room opening to my father 's . twenty thousand magnificent dream seem to flash their golden door when I know that the bed be mine . I think it almost as nice as a place by my father 's side . 
 ' do not   you like it , Mrs. Waddy ? ' I say . 
 she smile and sigh . ' like it ? oh ! yes , my dear , to be sure I do . I only hope it will not   vanish . ' she simper and look sad . 
 I have too many distraction , or I should have ask she whether my amazing and delightful new home have ever show symptom of vanish ; it appear to I , judge from my experience , that nothing move violently except myself , and my principal concern be lest any one should carry I away at a moment 's notice . in the evening I be introduce to a company of gentleman , who be drink wine after dinner with my father . they clap their hand and laugh immoderately on my tell they that I think those king of England who could not find room on the window must have go down to the cellar . 
 ' they be go , ' my father say . he drink off a glassful of wine and sigh prodigiously . ' they be go , gentleman , go there , like good wine , like old Port , which they tell we be go also . favour I by drink to the health of Richmond Roy the young . ' 
 they drink to I heartily , but my father have fall mournful before I leave the room . 
 pony - riding , and lesson in boxing and wrestling , and lesson in French from a french governess , at whose appearance my father always seem to be begin to dance a minuet , so exuberantly courteous be he ; and lesson in Latin from a tutor , whom my father invite to dinner once a fortnight , but do not distinguish otherwise than occasionally to take down latin sentence in a notebook from his dictation , occupy my morning . my father tell the man who instruct I in the art of self - defence that our family have always patronize his profession . I wrestle ten minute every day with this man 's son , and be regularly throw . on fine afternoon I be dress in black velvet for a drive in the park , where my father uncover his head to number of people , and be much look at . ' it be our duty , my son , never to forget name and person ; I beg you to bear that in mind , my dear Richie , ' he say . we use to go to his opera - box ; and we visit the House of Lords and the House of Commons ; and my father , though he complain of the decay of british eloquence , and mourn for the day of Chatham , and William Pitt   our old friend of the cake and the raspberry jam , and Burke , and Sheridan , encourage the orator with approve murmur . 
 my father no long lay stress on my study of the Peerage . ' now I have you in the very atmosphere , that will come of itself , ' he say . I wish to know whether I be likely to be transport suddenly to some other place . he assure I that nothing save a convulsion of the earth would do it , which comfort I , for I take the firmness of the earth in perfect trust . we speak of our old Sunday walk to St. Paul 's and Westminster Abbey as of a day that have its charm . our pew among a fashionable congregation please he well . the pew - opener curtsey to none as she do to he . for my part , I miss the monument and the chant , and something besides that have go I know not what . at the first indication of gloom in I , my father become alarmed , and , after make I stand with my tongue out before himself and Mrs. Waddy , like a dragon in a piece of tapestry , would resume his old playfulness , and try to be the same that he have be in Mrs. Waddy 's lodging . then we read the Arabian Nights together , or , rather , he read they to I , often act out the incident as we ride or drive abroad . an omission to perform a duty be the fatal forgetfulness to sprinkle pepper on the cream - tart ; if my father subject I to an interrogation concern my lesson , he be the dread african magician to whom must be surrender my acquisition of the ring and the musty old lamp . we be quite in the habit of meet fair Persians . he would frequently ejaculate that he resemble the Three Calendars in more respect than one . to divert I during my recovery from measle , he one day hire an actor in a theatre , and put a cloth round his neck , and seat he in a chair , rub his chin with soap , and play the part of the Barber over he , and I have never laugh so much in my life . poor Mrs. Waddy get her hand at her side , and keep on gasp , ' oh , sir ! oh ! ' while the Barber hurry away from the half - shave young man to consult his pretended astrolabe in the next room , where we hear he shout the sun 's altitude , and consult its willingness for the impatient young man to be far shave ; and back he come , seem refresh to have learn the sun 's favourable opinion , and gabbling at an immense rate , full of barber 's business . the servant be allow to be spectator ; but as soon as the young man be shave , my father dismiss they with the tone of a master . no wonder they love he . Mrs. Waddy ask who could help it ? 
 I remember a pang I have when she speak of his exposure to the risk of marry again ; it add a curious romantic tenderness to my adoration of he , and make I feel that he and I stand against the world . to have his hand in mine be my delight . then it be that I could think earnestly of Prince Ahmed and the kind and beautiful Peribanou , whom I would not have mind his marrying . my favourite dream be to see he shoot an arrow in a match for a prize , and lose the prize because of not find his arrow , and wonder where the arrow have fly to , and wander after it till he pass out of green field to grassy rock , and to a stony desert , where at last he find his arrow at an enormous distance from the shooting line , and there be the desert all about he , and the sweetest fairy ever imagine go to show herself to he in the ground under his foot . in his absence I really hunger for he , and be jealous . 
 during this arabian life , we sit on a carpet that fly to the Continent , where I fall sick , and be cure by smell at an apple ; and my father direct our movement through the aid of a telescope , which tell we the title of the hotel ready to receive we . as for the city and cathedral , the hot meadow under mountain , the river and the castle - they be little more to I than an animate book of geography , opening and shut at random ; and travel from place to place must have seem to I so much like the life I have lead , that I be generally as quick to cry as to laugh , and be never at peace between any two emotion . by - and - by I lay in a gondola with a young lady . my father make friend fast on our travel : her parent be among the number , and she fall in love with I and enjoy have the name of Peribanou , which I bestow on she for her delicious talk of the blue and red - strip post that would spout up fountain of pearl if they be pluck from their bed , and the palace that have fly out of the farth corner of the world , and the city that would some night or other vanish suddenly , leave bare sea - ripple to say ' where ? where ? ' as they roll over . I would have see she marry my father happily . she be like rest and dream to I , soft sea and pearl . we enter into an arrangement to correspond for life . her name be Clara Goodwin ; she request I to go always to the Horse Guards to discover in what part of the world Colonel Goodwin might be serve when I want to write to she . I , in return , could give no permanent address , so I relate my history from the beginning . ' to write to you would be the same as write to a river , ' she say ; and insist that I should drop the odious name of Roy when I grow a man . my father quarrel with Colonel Goodwin . month after I feel as if I have only just be tear from Clara , but she stand in a mist , irrecoverably distant . I have no other friend . 
 twelve dozen of splendid Burgundy be the fruit of our tour , to be lay down at Dipwell farm for my arrival at my majority , when I should be a legal man , embark in my own ship , as my father say . I do not taste the wine . ' porter for I that day , please God ! ' cry Mrs. Waddy , who do . my father eye she with pity , and order she to send the wine down to Dipwell , which be do . he take I between his knee , and say impressively , ' now , Richie , twelve dozen of the good that man can drink await you at the gate of manhood . few father can say that to their son , my boy ! if we drink it together , blessing on the day ! if I be go , Richie , shut up in the long box , ' his voice shake , and he add , ' go to Peribanou underneath , you know , remember that your dada see that the wine be a good vintage , and buy it and have it bottle in his own presence while you be asleep in the Emperor 's room in the fine old Burgundy city , and swear that , whatever come to they both , his son should drink the wine of prince on the day of his majority . ' here my father 's tone be highly exalted , and he sit in a great flush . 
 I promise he I would bend my step toward Dipwell to be there on my twenty - first birthday , and he pledge himself to be there in spirit at least , bodily if possible . we seal the subject with some tear . he often talk of commission a poet to compose verse about that wonderful coming day at Dipwell . the thought of the day in store for we send I strut as though I have be in the presence of my drill - master . Mrs. Waddy , however , grow extremely melancholy at the mention of it . 
 ' Lord only know where we shall all be by that time ! ' she sigh . 
 ' she be a dewy woman , ' say my father , disdainfully they appear always to be at variance , notwithstanding her absolute devotion to he . my father threaten to have she married to somebody immediately if she afflict he with what he call her Waddyism . she have get the habit of exclaim at the end of her remark , ' no matter ; our clock strike soon ! ' in a way that communicate to I an obscure idea of a door go to open unexpectedly in one of the wall , and conduct we , by subterranean passage , into a new country . my father 's method of rebuke her anxious nature be to summon his cook , the funniest of Frenchmen , Monsieur Alphonse , and issue order for a succession of six dinner - party . ' and now , ma'am , you have occupation for your mind , ' he would say . 
 to judge by the instantaneous composure of her whole appearance , he do produce a temporary abatement of her malady . the good soul bustle out of the room in attendance upon M. Alphonse , and never complain while the dinner last , but it be whisper that she have fit in the upper part of the house . no soon do my father hear the rumour than he accuse she to her face of this enormity , tell she that he be determined to effect a permanent cure , even though she should drive he to unlimited expense . we have a Ball party and an Aladdin supper , and for a fortnight my father hire postillion ; we flash through London . my father back a horse to run in the race on Epsom Downs name Prince Royal , only for the reason that his name be Prince Royal , and the horse win , which be , he say , a proof to I that in our country it be common prudence to stick to Royalty ; and he bid I note that if he go in a carriage and two , he be comparatively unnoticed , whereas when he be beheld in a carriage and four , with postillion , at a glance from he the country people tug their forelock , and would like , if he would let they , to kiss his hand . ' we will try the scarlet livery on one of our drive , Richie , ' say he . Mrs. Waddy hear he . ' it be unlawful , sir , ' she say . ' for whom , ma'am ? ' ask my father . ' none but Royal ... ' she be explain , but stop , for he show she an awful frown , and she cry so that my heart ache for she . my father go out to order the livery on the spot . he be very excited . then it be that Mrs. Waddy , embrace I , say , ' my dear , my own Master Richmond , my little Harry , prepare your poor child 's heart for evil day . ' I construe her unintelligible speech as an attack upon my father , and abuse she violently . 
 while I be in this state of wrathful championship , the hall - door be open . I run out and catch sight of my aunt Dorothy , in company with old Mr. Bannerbridge . I be kiss and hug for I know not how long , until the smell of Riversley take entire possession of I , and my old home seem near than the one I live in ; but my aunt , see tear on my cheek , ask I what be my cause of sorrow . in a moment I pour out a flood of complaint against Mrs. Waddy for vex my father . when she hear of the scarlet livery , my aunt lift her hand . ' the man be near the end of his wit and his money together , ' say Mr. Bannerbridge ; and she say to I , ' my darling Harry will come back to his own nice little room , and see his grandpapa soon , will not   you , my pet ? all be ready for he there as it use to be , except poor mama . " kiss my boy , my Harry Harry Richmond . " those be her last word on her death - bed , before she go to God , Harry , my own ! there be Sampson the pony , and Harry 's dog Prince , and his lamb Daisy , grow a sheep , and the ploughboy , Dick , with the big boot . ' much more sweet talk of the same current that make my face cloudy and bright , and fill I with desire for Riversley , to see my mother 's grave and my friend . 
 Aunt Dorothy look at I . ' come now , ' she say ; ' come with I , Harry . ' her trembling seize on I like a fire . I say , ' yes , ' though my heart sink as if I have lose my father with the word . she catch I in her arm tight , murmur , ' and dry our tear and make our house laugh . oh ! since the night that Harry go ..... and I be now Harry 's mama , he have I . ' 
 I look on her forehead for the wreath of white flower my mother use to wear , and thought of my father 's letter with the prayer write on the black - border page . I say I would go , but my joy in go be go . we be stop in the doorway by Mrs. Waddy . nothing would tempt she to surrender I . Mr. Bannerbridge try reason with she , and , as he say , put the case , which seem to have perch on his forefinger . he talk of my prospect , of my sole chance of be educate morally and virtuously as become the grandson of an english gentleman of a good old family , and of my father have spend my mother 's estate , and of the danger of his do so with mine , and of religious duty and the awfulness of the position Mrs. Waddy stand in . he certainly subdue I to very silent breathing , but do not affect I as my aunt Dorothy 's picturing of Riversley have do ; and when Mrs. Waddy , reduce to an apparent submissiveness , address I piteously , ' Master Richmond , would you leave papa ? ' I cry out , ' no , no , never leave my papa , ' and twist away from my aunt 's keeping . my father 's arrival cause I to be withdraw , but I hear his offer of his hospitality and all that be his ; and subsequently there be loud talk on his part . I be kiss by my aunt before she go . she whisper , ' come to we when you be free ; think of we when you pray . ' she be full of tear . Mr. Bannerbridge pat my head . 
 the door close on they and I think it be a vision that have pass . but now my father set my heart pant with question as to the terrible possibility of we two ever be separate . in some way he paint my grandfather so black that I declare earnestly I would rather die than go to Riversley ; I would never utter the name of the place where there be evil speaking of the one I love dearest . ' do not , my son , ' he say solemnly , ' or it part we two . ' I repeat after he , ' I be a Roy and not a Beltham . ' it be enough to hear that insult and shame have be cast on he at Riversley for I to hate the name of the place . we cry and then laugh together , and I must have deliver myself with amazing eloquence , for my father hold I at arm ' length and say , ' Richie , the notion of train you for a General commandership of the british army be a good one , but if you have get the win tongue , the woolsack will do as well for a whisper in the ear of the throne . that be our aim , my son . we say , you will not acknowledge our birth , you shall acknowledge our worth . ' he complain bitterly of my aunt Dorothy bring a lawyer to our house . the sin of Mrs. Waddy be forgive she , owe to her noble resistance to the legal gentleman 's seductive speech . so I walk up and down stair with the king of England look at I out of the coloured window quietly for a week ; and then two ugly man enter the house , cause I to suffer a fearful oppression , though my father be exceedingly kind to they and have bed provide for they , say that they be very old retainer of his . 
 but the next day our scarlet livery appear . after exact particular attention to his command , my father quit Mrs. Waddy , and we mount the carriage , laugh at her deplorable eye and prim lip , which he imitate for my amusement . ' a load be off my head , ' he remark . he ask I if splendour do not fatigue I also . I catch the answer from his face and reply that it do , and that I should like to go right on to Dipwell ' the Burgundy sleep safe there , ' say my father , and think over it . we have an extraordinary day .
