People stand fast to gaze at we ; in the country some pull off their hat and set up a cheer . the landlord of the inn where we bait remain bare - headed until we start afresh , and I , accord to my father 's example , bow and lift my cap gravely to person salute we along the road . nor do I seek to know the reason for this excess of respectfulness ; I be begin to take to it naturally . at the end of a dusty high - road , where it descend the hill into a town , we draw up close by a high red wall , behind which I hear boy shout at play . we go among they , accompany by their master . my father tip the head boy for the benefit of the school , and follow lunch with the master and his daughter , to whom I give a kiss at her request , a half - holiday be grant to the boy in my name . how they cheer ! the young lady see my delight , and hold I at the window while my father talk with her ; and for a long time after I behold they in imagination talk : that be to say , my father issue his instruction and Mr. Rippenger receive they like a pliant hodman ; for the result of it be that two day later , without see my king of England , my home again , or London , I be Julia Rippenger 's intimate friend and the young pupil of the school . my father tell I subsequently that we sleep at an hotel those two night intervene . memory transplant I from the coach and scarlet livery straight to my place of imprisonment . 




 CHAPTER V. I make a DEAR FRIEND 

 Heriot be the name of the head boy of the school . Boddy be the name of one of the usher . they be both in love with Julia Rippenger . it be my fortune to outrun they in her favour for a considerable period , during which time , though I have cease to live in state , and be wear out my suit of velvet , and have neither visit nor letter from my father , I be in tolerable bliss . Julia 's kiss be shower on I for almost anything I say or do , but her admiration of heroism and daring be so fervent that I be in no great danger of become effeminate than Achilles when he wear girl 's clothe . she be seventeen , an age bewitch for boy to look up to and man to look down on . the puzzle of the school be how to account for her close relationship to old Rippenger . such an apple on such a crab - tree seem monstrous . Heriot say that he hop Boddy would marry old Rippenger 's real daughter , and , say he , that be birch - twig . I relate his sparkling speech to Julia , who laugh , accuse he , however , of impudence . she let I see a portrait of her dead mother , an irish lady raise dark eyelash , whom she resemble . I talk of the portrait to Heriot , and as I have privilege accord to none of the other boy and could go to she at any hour of the day after lesson , he make I beg for he to have a sight of it . she consider awhile , but refuse . on hearing of the unkind refusal , Heriot stick his hand into his pocket and give up cricketing . we see he lean against a wall in full view of her window , while the boy crowd round he try to get he to practise , a school - match of an important character come off with a rival academy ; and it be only through fear of our school be beat if she do not relent that Julia hand I the portrait , charge I solemnly to bring it back . I promise , of course . Heriot go into his favourite corner of the playground , and there look at it and kiss it , and then button his jacket over it tight , growl when I ask he to return it . Julia grow frightened . she send I with number of petition to he . 
 ' look here , young un , ' say Heriot ; ' you be a good little fellow , and I like you , but just tell she I believe in nothing but handwriting , and if she write to I for it humbly and nicely she shall have it back . say I only want to get a copy take by a first - rate painter . ' 
 Julia shed tear at his cruelty , call he cruel , wicked , false to his word . she write , but the letter do not please he , and his reply be scornful . at prayer morning and evening , it be pitiful to observe her glance of entreaty and her downfallen eyelash . I guess that in Heriot 's letter to she he want to make her confess something , which she would not do . ' now I write to he no more ; let he know it , my darling , ' she say , and the consequence of Heriot 's ungrateful obstinacy be that we all behold she , at the ceremony of the consecration of the new church , place her hand on Mr. Boddy 's arm and allow he to lead she about . Heriot keep his eye on they ; his mouth be sharp , and his arm stiff by his side . I be the bearer of a long letter to she that evening . she tear it to piece without read it . next day Heriot walk slowly past Mr. Boddy hold the portrait in his hand . the usher call to he ! 
 ' what have you there , Heriot ? ' 
 my hero star . ' only a family portrait , ' he answer , thrust it safe in his pocket and fix his gaze on Julia 's window . 
 ' permit I to look at it , ' say Mr. Boddy . 
 ' permit I to decline to let you , ' say Heriot . 
 ' look at I , sir , ' cry Boddy . 
 ' I prefer to look elsewhere , sir , ' reply Heriot , and there be Julia visible at her window . 
 ' I ask you , sir , civilly , ' quoth Boddy , ' for permission to look , I use the word intentionally ; I say I ask you for permission ... ' 
 ' no , you do not , ' Heriot retort , quite cool ; ' inferentially you do ; but you do not use the word permission . ' 
 ' and you turn upon I impudently , ' pursue Boddy , whose colour be thunder : ' you quibble , sir ; you prevaricated ; you conceal what you be carry ... ' 
 ' be carry , ' Heriot correct his tense ; ' and mean to , in spite of every Boddy , ' he murmur audibly . 
 ' like a rascal detect in an act of felony , ' roar Boddy , ' you conceal it , sir ... ' 
 ' conceal it , sir . ' 
 ' and I demand , in obedience to my duty , that you instantly exhibit it for my inspection , now , here , at once ; no parleying ; unbutton , or I call Mr. Rippenger to compel you . ' 
 I be stand close by my brave Heriot , rather tremble , studious of his manfulness though I be . his left foot be firmly in advance , as he say , just in the manner to start an usher furious : 
 ' I conceal it , I conceal it ; I be carry it , I carry it : you demand that I exhibit for your inspection what I mean no Boddy to see ? I have to assure you respectfully , sir , that family portrait be sacred thing with the son of gentleman . here , Richie , off ! ' 
 I find the portrait in my hand , and Heriot between I and the usher , in the attitude of a fellow keep another out of his home at prisoner' - base . he have spy Mr. Rippenger 's head at the playground gate . I have just time to see Heriot and the usher in collision before I run through the gate and into Julia 's arm in her garden , whither the dreadful prospect of an approach catastrophe have attract she . 
 Heriot be merely report guilty of insolence . he take his five hundred line of Virgil with his usual sarcastic dignity : all he say to Mr. Rippenger be , ' let it be about Dido , sir , ' which set several of the boy upon Dido 's history , but Heriot be condemn to the battle with Turnus . my share in this event secure Heriot 's friendship to I without cost I the slight inconvenience . ' papa would never punish you , ' Julia say ; and I feel my rank . nor be it wonderful I should when Mr. Rippenger be constantly speak of my father 's magnificence in my presence before company . allow to draw on he largely for pocket - money , I maintain my father 's princely reputation in the school . at time , especially when the holiday arrive and I be leave alone with Julia , I have fit of mournfulness , and almost think the boy happy than I be . go home begin to seem an unattainable thing to I . have a father , too , a regular father , instead of a dazzle angel that appear at interval , I consider a benefaction , in its way , some recompense to the boy , for their not possess one like mine . my anxiety be relieve by my writing letter to my father , address to the care of Miss Julia Rippenger , and post they in her work - basket . she favour I with very funny reply , sign , ' your own ever - love papa , ' about his be engage kill bengal tiger and capture white elephant , a noble occupation that give I exciting and consolatory dream of he . 
 we have at last a real letter of his , date from a foreign city ; but he mention nothing of come to I . I understand that Mr. Rippenger be disappoint with it . 
 gradually a kind of cloud steal over I . I no long like to ask for pocket - money ; I be clothe in a suit of plain cloth ; I be banish from the parlour , and only on Sunday be I permit to go to Julia . I cease to live in myself . through the whole course of lesson , at play - time , in my bed , and round to morning bell , I be hunt my father in an unknown country , generally with the sun set before I : I run out of a wood almost into a brook to see it sink as if I have again lose sight of he , and then a sense of darkness bring I back to my natural consciousness , without afflict I much , but astonishing I . why be I away from he ? I could repeat my lesson in the midst of these dream quite fairly ; it be the awakening among the circle of the boy that make I falter during a recital and ask myself why I be there and he absent ? they have give over speculating on another holiday and treat from my father ; yet he have produce such an impression in the school that even when I have descend to the level of a total equality with they , they continue to have some consideration for I . I be able to talk of foreign city and could tell story , and I be , besides , under the immediate protection of Heriot . but now the shadow of a great calamity fall on I , for my dear Heriot announce his intention of leave the school next half . 
 ' I can not   stand be pray at , morning and evening , by a fellow who have not   the pluck to strike I like a man , ' he say . Mr. Rippenger have the habit of signalize offender , in his public prayer , as boy whose heart he wish to be turn from callousness . he perpetually suspect plot ; and to hear he allude to some deep , long - hatch school conspiracy while we knelt motionless on the form , and fetch a big breath to bring out , ' may the heart of Walter Heriot be turn and he comprehend the multitudinous blessing , ' etc . , be intensely distressing . together with Walter Heriot , Andrew Saddlebank , our good bowler , the droll fellow in the world , John Salter , and little Gus Temple , be oftenest cite . they declare that they invariably utter ' amen , ' as Heriot do , but we none of we hear this defiant murmur of assent from their lip . Heriot pronounce it clearly and cheerfully , cause Julia 's figure to shrink as she kneel with her face in the chair hard by her father 's desk - pulpit . I receive the hearty congratulation of my comrade for sing out ' amen ' louder than Heriot , like a chorister , though not in so prolonged a note , on hear to my stupefaction Mr. Rippenger implore that the heart of ' he we know as Richmond Roy ' might be turn . I do it spontaneously . Mr. Rippenger gaze at I in descend from his desk ; Julia , too , look grieve . for my part , I exult in have do a thing that give I a likeness to Heriot . 
 ' Little Richmond , you be a little hero , ' he say , caress I . ' I see old Rippenger whisper to that beast , Boddy . never mind ; they will not   hurt you as long as I be here . grow tough , that be what you have   get to do . I would like to see you horse , only to see whether you be game to take it without wince if it do not   hurt you much , little lad . ' 
 he hug I up to he . 
 ' I would take anything for you , Heriot , ' say I. 
 ' all right , ' he answer , never mean I to suffer on his account . he have an inimitable manner of sweet speaking that endear he to young boy capable of appreciate it , with the supernatural power of music . it endear he , I suppose , to young woman also . Julia repeat his phrase , as for instance , ' silly boy , silly boy , ' speak with a wave of his hand , when a little fellow thank he for a kindness . she be angry at his approval of what she call my defiance of her father , and insist that I be the catspaw of one of Heriot 's plot to vex he . ' tell Heriot you have my command to say you belong to I and must not be mislead , ' she say . his answer be that he want it in writing . she request he to deliver up her previous letter . thereupon he charge I with a lengthy epistle , which plunge we into boiling water . Mr. Boddy sit in the schoolroom while Heriot 's pen be at work , on the wet Sunday afternoon . his keen little eye be busy in his flat bird 's head all the time Heriot continue writing . he see no more than that Heriot give I a book ; but as I be march away to Julia he call to know where I be go . 
 ' to Miss Rippenger , ' I reply . 
 ' what have you there ? ' 
 ' a book , sir . ' 
 ' show I the book . ' 
 I stand fast . 
 ' it be a book I have lend he , sir , ' say Heriot , rise . ' I shall see if it be a fit book for a young boy , ' say Boddy ; and before Heriot could interpose , he have knock the book on the floor , and out fall the letter . both spring down to seize it : their head encounter , but Heriot have the quick hand ; he catch the letter , and cry ' Off ! ' to I , as on another occasion . this time , however , he be not between I and the usher . I be seize by the collar , and shake roughly . 
 ' you will now understand that you be on a footing with the rest of the boy , you Roy , ' say Boddy . ' little scoundrelly spoilt urchin , upset the discipline of the school , will not   do here . Heriot , here be your book . I regret , ' he add , sneer , ' that a leaf be tear . ' 
 ' I regret , sir , that the poor boy be so savagely handle , ' say Heriot . 
 he be warn to avoid insolence . 
 ' oh , as much Virgil as you like , ' Heriot retort ; ' I know he by heart . ' 
 it be past the hour of my customary visit to Julia , and she come to discover the reason of my delay . Boddy stand up to explain . Heriot go forward , say , ' I think I be the one who ought to speak , Miss Rippenger . the fact be , I hear from little Roy that you be fond of tale of indian adventure , and I give he a book for you to read , if you like it . Mr. Boddy object , and treat the youngster rather rigorously . it must have be quite a misunderstanding on his part . here be the book it be extremely amusing . ' 
 Julia blush very red . she accept the book with a soft murmur , and the sallow usher have not a word . 
 ' stay , ' say Heriot . ' I take the liberty to write some note . my father be an indian officer , you know , and some of the term in the book be difficult without note . Richie , hand that paper . here they be , Miss Rippenger , if you will be so kind as to place they in the book . ' 
 I be hop with all my might that she would not deny he . she do , and my heart sink . 
 ' oh , I can read it without note , ' she say , cheerfully . 
 after that , I listen with indifference to her petition to Boddy that I might be allow to accompany she , and be not at all chagrin by his refusal . she lay down the book , say that I could bring it to she when I be out of disgrace . 
 in the evening we walk in the playground , where Heriot ask I to do a brave thing , which he would never forget . this be that I should take a sharp run right past Boddy , who be pace up and down before the gate lead into Julia 's garden , and force she to receive the letter . I go bound like a ball . the usher , suspect only that I hurry to speak to he , let I see how indignant he be with my behaviour by stride all the fast as I draw near , and so he pass the gate , and I rush in . I have just time to say to Julia , ' hide it , or I be in such a scrape . ' 
 the next minute she be address my enemy : 
 ' surely you would not punish he because he love I ? ' and he , though he speak of insubordination , merit chastisement ; and other usher phrase , seem to melt , and I have what I believe be a primary conception of the power of woman . she lead he to talk in the gentle way possible of how the rain have refresh her flower , and of this and that poor rise . 
 I could think of nothing but the darling letter , which have flash out of sight as a rabbit pop into burrow . Boddy depart with a rose . 
 ' ah , Richie , ' she say , ' I have to pay to have you with I now . ' 
 we walk to the summer - house , where she read Heriot 's letter through . ' but he be a boy ! how old be Heriot ? he be not so old as I be ! ' 
 these be her word , and she read the letter anew , and read it again after she have place it in her bosom , I meanwhile pour out praise of Heriot . 
 ' you speak of he as if you be in love with he , Richmond , ' she say . 
 ' and I do love he , ' I answer . 
 ' not with I ? ' she ask . 
 ' yes , I do love you too , if you will not make he angry . ' 
 ' but do you know what it be he want of I ? ' 
 I guess : ' yes ; he want you to let he sit close to you for half an hour . ' 
 she say that he sit very near she in church . 
 ' ah , ' say I , ' but he must not   interrupt the sermon . ' 
 she laugh , and mouth I over with laugh kiss . ' there be very little he have not   dare enough for ! ' 
 we talk of his courage . 
 ' be he good as well ? ' say Julia , more to herself than to I ; but I sing out , 
 ' good ! oh , so kind ! ' 
 this appear to convince she . 
 ' very generous to you and every one , be he not ? ' she say ; and from that moment be all question concern his kind treatment of the boy , and as to their look up to he . 
 I quit she , take her message to Heriot : ' you may tell he tell he that I can not   write . ' 
 Heriot frown on hear I repeat it . 
 ' Humph ! ' he go , and be bright in a twinkling : ' that mean she will come ! ' he smack his hand together , grow black , and ask , ' do she give that beast Boddy a rose ? ' 
 I have to confess she do ; and feel a twinge of my treason to she , feel her to Heriot . 
 ' Humph ! ' he go ; ' she shall suffer for that . ' 
 all this be like music go on until the curtain should lift and reveal my father to I . 
 there be soon a secret to be read in Heriot 's face for one who love it as I do . Julia 's betray nothing . I be not take into their confidence , and luckily not ; otherwise I fear I should have serve they ill , I be so poor a dissembler and be so hotly ply with interrogation by the suspicious usher . I feel sure that Heriot and Julia meet . his eye be on she all through prayer - time , and hers wander over the boy ' head till they rest on he , when they give a short flutter and drop , like a bird shoot dead . the boy must have have some knowledge that love be busy in their midst , for they speak of Heriot and Julia as a jolly couple , and of Boddy as one meaning to play the part of old Nick the first opportunity . she be kind to they than ever . it be not a new thing that she should send in cake of her own making , but it be extraordinary that we should get these thoughtful present as often as once a fortnight , and it become usual to hear a boy exclaim , either among a knot of fellow or to himself , ' by jingo , she be a pretty girl ! ' on she pass out of the room , and sometimes entirely of his own idea . I be persuade that if she have consent to marry Boddy , the boy would have be seriously dispose to conspire to jump up in the church and forbid the bann . we should have prefer to hand she to the junior usher , Catman , of whom the rumour run in the school that he once drink a bottle of wine and be sick after it , and he be therefore a weak creature to our mind ; the truth of the rumour be confirm by his pale complexion . that we would have hand our blooming princess to he be full proof of our abhorrence of Boddy . I might have think with the other boy that she be grow pretty , only I never could imagine her so delicious as when she smile at my father . 
 the consequence of the enlistment of the whole school in Heriot 's interest be that at cricket - match , picnic on the hill , and boat on the canal , Mr. Boddy be begirt with spy , and little Temple report to Heriot a conversation that he , lie hide in tall grass , have hear between Boddy and Julia . Boddy ask she to take private lesson in French from he . Heriot listen to the monstrous tale as he be on the point of enter Julia 's boat , where Boddy sit beside she , and Heriot row stroke - oar . he dip his blade , and say , loud enough to be hear by I in Catman 's boat , 
 ' do you think french useful in a military education , sir ? ' 
 and Boddy say , ' yes , of course it be . ' 
 say Heriot , ' then I think I shall take lesson . ' 
 Boddy tell he he be take lesson in the school . 
 ' oh ! ' say Heriot , ' I mean private lesson ' ; and here he repeat one of Temple 's piece of communication : ' so much more can be impart in a private lesson ! ' 
 Boddy spring half up from his seat . ' row , sir , and do not   talk , ' he growl . 
 ' sit , sir , and do not   dance in the boat , if you please , or the lady will be overset , ' say Heriot . 
 Julia request to be allow to land and walk home . Boddy catch the rudder line and leapt on the bank to hand she out ; then all the boy in her boat and in Catman 's shout , ' Miss Julia ! dear Miss Julia , do not   leave we ! ' and we hear wheedle voice : ' do not   go off with he alone ! ' Julia bid we behave well or she would not be able to come out with we . at her entreaty Boddy step back to his post , and the two boat go forward like swan that have do ruffle their feather . 
 the boy be exceedingly disappointed that no catastrophe follow the event of the day . Heriot , they think , might have upset the boat , save Julia , and drown Boddy , and give we a feast of pleasurable excitement : instead of which Boddy live to harass we with his tyrannical imposition and spiteful slap , and it be to he , not to our Heriot , that Julia be most gracious . some of we discuss her conduct . 
 ' she be a coquette , ' say little Temple . I go off to the French dictionary . 
 ' be Julia Rippenger a coquette , Heriot ? ' I ask he . 
 ' keep girl out of your head , you little fellow , ' say he , deal I a smart thump . 
 ' be a coquette a nasty girl ? ' I persist . 
 ' no , a nice one , as it happen , ' be his answer . 
 my only feeling be jealousy of the superior knowledge of the sex possess by Temple , for I could not fathom the meaning of coquette ; but he have sister . Temple and I walk the ground together , mutually declare how much we would forfeit for Heriot 's sake . by this time my Sunday visit to Julia have be interdict : I be plunge , as it be , in the pit of the school , and my dream of my father be lose distinctness . a series of box on the ear from Boddy begin to astound and transform I . Mr. Rippenger , too , threaten I with carving , though my offence be slight . ' yes , ' say Temple and I , in chorus , ' but you dare not   strike Heriot ! ' this be our consolation , and the sentiment of the school . fancy , then , our amazement to behold he lay the cane on Heriot 's shoulder as fiercely as he could , and Boddy second he . the scene be terrible . we be all at our desk do evening task for the morrow , a great matchday at cricket , Boddy watch over we , and bellowing , ' silence at your work , you lazy fellow , if you want lesson to be finish at ten in the morning ! ' a noise come grow up to we from below , up the stair from the wet - weather shed , and Heriot burst into the room , old Rippenger after he , pant . 
 ' Mr. Boddy , you be right , ' he cry , ' I find he a prowler , break all rule of discipline . a pervert , impudent rascal ! an example shall be set to my school , sir . we have be fall lax . what ! I find the puppy in my garden whistle he confess for one of my servant here , Mr. Boddy , if you please . my school shall see that none insult I with impunity ! ' he lay on Heriot like a wind on a bulrush . Heriot bend his shoulder a trifle , not his head . 
 ' hit away , sir , ' he say , during the storm of blow , and I , through my tear , imagine he   or I do now   a young eagle force to bear the thunder , but with his face to it . then we see Boddy lie hand on he , and in a twinkling down pitch the usher , and the boy cheer chirp , I should say , they exult so , and merely sing out like bird , without any wilfulness of delight or defiance . after the fall of Boddy we have no sense of our hero suffer shame . Temple and I clutched finger tight as long as the blow go on . we hop for Boddy to make another attempt to touch Heriot ; he hold near the master , look ready to spring , like a sallow panther ; we keep hop he would , in our horror of the murderous slash of the cane ; and not a syllable do Heriot utter . Temple and I start up , unaware of what we be go to do , or of anything until we have get a blow a - piece , and be in the thick of it , and Boddy have we both by the collar , and be knock our head together , as he drag we back to our seat . but the boy tell we we stop the execution . Mr. Rippenger address we before he leave the school - room . Saddlebank , Salter , and a good many other , plug their ear with their fist . that night Boddy and Catman pace in the bedchamber , to prevent plotting and conspiracy , they say . I long to get my arm about Heriot , and thought of he , and dream of blood , and wake in the morning wonder what make I cry , and my arm and back very stiff . Heriot be gay as ever , but have fit of reserve ; the word pass round that we be not to talk of yesterday evening . we fear he would refuse to play in the match . 
 ' why not ? ' say he , star at we angrily . ' have Saddlebank break his arm , and can not   bowl ? ' 
 no , Saddlebank be in excellent trim , though shameface , as be Salter , and most of the big boy be . they beg Heriot to let they shake his hand . 
 ' wait till we win our match , ' say Heriot . 
 Julia do not appear at morning prayer . 
 ' ah , ' say Temple , ' it would make she sick to hear old Massacre pray . ' it have nearly make he sick , he add , and I immediately feel that it have nearly make I sick . 
 we suppose we should not see Julia at the match . she come , however , and talk to everybody . I could not contain myself , I want so to tell she what have befall Heriot overnight , while he be bat , and the whole ground cheer his hit . I on one side of she whisper : 
 ' I say , Julia , my dear , I say , do you know ... ' 
 and Temple on the other : ' Miss Julia , I wish you would let I tell you ' 
 we long to arouse her pity for Heriot at the moment she be admire he , but she check we , and as she be surround by lady and gentleman of the town , and particular friend of her , we could not speak out . Heriot bring his bat to the booth for eighty - nine run . his sleeve happen to be unbutton , and there , on his arm , be a mark of the cane . 
 ' look ! ' I say to Julia . but she look at I . 
 ' Richie , be you ill ? ' 
 she assure I I be very pale , and I feel she tremble excessively , and her parasol be cover we . 
 ' here , Roy , Temple , ' we hear Heriot call ; ' here , come here and bowl to I . ' 
 I go and bowl till I think my head be fly after the ball and get knock , it swam and throb so horribly . 
 Temple relate that I fall , and be carry all the way from the cricket - field home by Heriot , who would not give I up to the usher . I be in Julia 's charge three day . every time I speak of her father and Heriot , she cry , ' oh , hush ! ' and have tear on her eyelid . when I be quite strong again , I make she hear I out . she hold I and rock over I like a green tree in the wind and rain . 
 ' be any name mention ? ' she ask , with her mouth work , and to my ' no , ' say ' no , she know there be none , ' and seem to drink and choke , and be one minute calm , all but a trembling hang underlip , next smile on I , and next have her face carve in grimace by the jerk little tug of her mouth , which I dislike to see , for she would say nothing of what she think of Heriot , and I think to myself , though I forbear to speak unkindly , ' it be no use your make yourself look ugly , Julia . ' if she have talk of Heriot , I should have think that cry person ' kiss be agreeable . 
 on my return into the school , I find it in a convulsion of excitement , owe to Heriot 's send Boddy a challenge to fight a duel with pistol . Mr. Rippenger preach a sermon to the boy concern the unChristian spirit and hideous moral perversity of one who would even consent to fight a duel . how much more reprehensible , then , be one that could bring himself to defy a fellow - creature to mortal combat ! we be not of his opinion ; and as these question be carry by majority , we decide that Boddy be a coward , and approve the idea that Heriot would have to shoot or scourge he when the holiday come . Mr. Rippenger conclude his observation by remark that the sharp punishment he could inflict upon Heriot be to leave he to his own conscience ; which he do for three day , and then ask he if he be in a fit state of mind to beg Mr. Boddy 's pardon publicly . 
 ' I be quite prepared to tell he what I think of he publicly , sir , ' say Heriot . 
 a murmur of exultation pass through the school . Mr. Rippenger seize little Temple , and flog he . far from dread the rod , now that Heriot and Temple have taste it , I think of punishment as a mad pleasure , not a bit more awful than the burn furze - bush plunge into by our fellow in a follow - my - leader scamper on the common ; so I catch Temple 's hand as he go by I , and say , eagerly , ' shall I sing out hurrah ? ' 
 ' bother it ! ' be Temple 's answer , for he have take a stinging dozen , and have a tender skin . 
 Mr. Rippenger call I up to he , to inform I , that whoever I be , and whatever I be , and I might be a little impostor foist on his benevolence , yet he would bring I to a knowledge of myself : he give I warn of it ; and if my father object to his method , my father must write word to that effect , and attend punctually to business duty , for Surrey House be not an almshouse , either for the son of gentleman of high connection , or for the son of vagabond . Mr. Rippenger add a spurning shove on my shoulder to his recommendation to I to resume my seat . I do not understand he at all . I be , in fact , indebte to a boy name Drew , a know sneak , for the explanation , in itself difficult to comprehend . it be , that Mr. Rippenger be lose patience because he have receive no money on account of my boarding and schooling . the intelligence fill my head like the buzz of a fly , occupy my meditation without lead they anywhere . I speak on the subject to Heriot . 
 ' oh , the sordid old brute ! ' say he of Mr. Rippenger . ' how can he know the habit and feeling of gentleman ? your father 's travelling , and can not   write , of course . my father 's in India , and I get a letter from he about once a year . we know one another , and I know he be one of the good officer in the british army . it be just the way with schoolmaster and tradesman : they do not   care whether a man be do his duty to his country ; he must attend to they , settle account with they hang they ! I will send you money , dear little lad , after I have   leave . ' 
 he disperse my brood fit . I be sure my father be a fountain of gold , and only happen to be travel . besides , Heriot 's love for Julia , whom none of we see now , be an incessant distraction . she do not appear at prayer . she sit up in the gallery at church , hardly to be spy . a letter that Heriot fling over the gardenwall for she be return to he , open , enclose by post . 
 ' a letter for Walter Heriot , ' exclaim Mr. Boddy , lift it high for Heriot to walk and fetch it ; and his small eye blink when Heriot say aloud on his way , cheerfully , 
 ' a letter from the colonel in India ! ' 
 Boddy wait a minute , and then say , ' be your father in good health ? ' 
 Heriot 's face be scarlet . at first he stutter , ' my father ! I hope so ! what have you in common with he , sir ? ' 
 ' you state that the letter be from your father , ' say Boddy . 
 ' what if it be , sir ? ' 
 ' oh , in that case , nothing whatever to I . ' 
 they talk on , and the young of we could perceive Boddy be burst with devilish glee . Heriot get a letter post to Julia . it be lay on his desk , with her name scratch completely out , and his put in its place . he grow pale and sad , but do his work , play his game , and only let his friend speak to he of lesson and play . his counsel to I be , that in spite of everything , I be always to stick to my task and my cricket . his sadness he could not conceal . he look like an old lamp with a poor light in it . not a boy in the school miss see how Boddy 's flat head perpetually have a side - eye on he . 
 all this come to an end . John Salter 's father live on the other side of the down , and invite three of we to spend a day at his house . the selection include Heriot , Saddlebank , and I . Mr. Rippenger , not like to refuse Mr. Salter , consent to our going , but pretend that I be too young . Salter say his mother and sister very much wish to make my acquaintance . we go in his father 's carriage . a jolly wind blow cloud and dust and leave : I could have fancy I be go to my own father . the sensation of freedom have a magical effect on I , so that I be the wildest talker of they all . even in the middle of the family I lead the conversation ; and I do not leave Salter 's house without receive an assurance from his elder sister that they be in love with I . we drive home back to prison , we call it full of good thing , talk of Salter 's father 's cellar of wine and of my majority Burgundy , which I say , believe it be true , amount to twelve hundred dozen ; and an appointment be make for we to meet at Dipwell Farm , to assist in consume it , in my honour and my father 's . that matter settle , I feel myself roll over and over at a great rate , and clasp a juniper tree . the horse have trench from the chalk road on to the down . I have be shoot out . Heriot and Salter have jump out Heriot to look after I ; but Saddlebank and the coachman be drive at a great rate over the dark slope . Salter feel some anxiety concern his father 's horse , so we leave he to pursue they , and walk on laugh , Heriot praise I for my pluck . 
 ' I say good - bye to you to - night , Richie , ' say he . ' we be certain to meet again . I shall go to a military school . mind you enter a cavalry regiment when you be man enough . look in the Army List , you will find I there . my aunt shall make a journey and call on you while you be at Rippenger 's , so you shall not   be quite lonely . ' 
 to my grief , I discover that Heriot have resolve he would not return to school . 
 ' you will get thrash , ' he say ; ' I can not   help it : I hope you have   grow tough by this time . I can not   stay here . I feel more like a dog than a man in that house now . I will see you back safe . no crying , young cornet ! ' 
 we have lose the sound of the carriage . Heriot fall to musing . he remark that the accident take away from Mr. Salter the responsibility of deliver he at Surrey House , but that he , Heriot , be bind , for Mr. Salter 's sake , to conduct I to the door ; an unintelligible refinement of reasoning , to my wit . we reach our town between two and three in the morning . there be a ladder lean against one of the house in repair near the school . ' you be here , be you ! ' say Heriot , speak to the ladder : ' you will do I a service the last I shall want in the neighbourhood . ' he manage to poise the ladder on his shoulder , and move forward . 
 ' be we go in through the window ? ' I ask , see he fix the ladder against the school - house wall . 
 he say , ' hush ; keep a look - out . ' 
 I see he mount high . when he tap at the window I remember it be Julia 's ; I hear she cry out inside . the window rise slowly . Heriot speak : 
 ' I have come to say good - bye to you , Julia , dear girl : do not   be afraid of I . ' she answer inaudibly to my ear . he beg she to come to he at once , only once , and hear he and take his hand . she be timid ; he have her finger first , then her whole arm , and she lean over he . ' Julia , my sweet , dear girl , ' he say ; and she : 
 ' Heriot , Walter , do not   go do not   go ; you do not care for I if you go . oh , do not   go . ' 
 ' we have   come to it , ' say Heriot . 
 she ask why he be not in bed , and moan on : 
 ' do not   go . ' I be speechless with wonder at the night and the scene . they whisper ; I see their face close together , and Heriot 's arm round her neck . ' oh , Heriot , my darling , my Walter , ' she say , cry , I know by the sound of her voice . 
 ' tell I you love I , ' say Heriot . 
 ' I do , I do , only do not   go , ' she answer . 
 ' will you love I faithfully ? ' 
 ' I will ; I do . ' 
 ' say , " I love you , Walter . " ' 
 ' I love you , Walter . ' 
 ' for ever . ' 
 ' for ever . oh ! what a morning for I . do you smell my honeysuckle ? oh , do not   go away from I , Walter . do you love I so ? ' 
 ' I would go through a regiment of sabre to get at you . ' 
 ' but smell the night air ; how sweet ! oh , how sweet ! no , not kiss I , if you be go to leave I ; not kiss I , if you can be so cruel ! ' 
 ' do you dream of I in your bed ? ' 
 ' yes , every night . ' 
 ' God bless the bed ! ' 
 ' every night I dream of you . oh ! brave Heriot ; dear , dear Walter , you do not betray I ; my father strike you , and you let he for my sake . every night I pray heaven to make you forgive he : I think you would hate I . I cry till I be glad you could not see I . look at those two little star ; no , they hurt I , I can not   look at they ever again . but no , you be not go ; you want to frighten I . do smell the flower . do not   make they poison to I . oh , what a morning for I when you be lose ! and I , to look out on the night alone ! no , no more kiss ! oh , yes , I will kiss you , dear . ' 
 Heriot say , ' your mother be irish , Julia . ' 
 ' yes . she would have love you . ' 
 ' I ' ve   irish blood too . give I her portrait . it be the image of you . ' 
 ' to take away ? Walter ! not to take it away ? ' 
 ' you darling ! to keep I sure of you . ' 
 ' part with my mother 's portrait ? ' 
 ' why , yes , if you love I one bit . ' 
 ' but you be young than I , Heriot . ' 
 ' then good - night , good - bye , Julia . ' 
 ' Walter , I will fetch it . ' 
 Heriot now tell she I be below , and she look down on I and call my name softly , send kiss from her finger while he give the cause for our late return . 
 ' some one must be sit up for you be we safe ? ' she say . 
 Heriot laugh , and press for the portrait . 
 ' it be all I have . why should you not have it ? I want to be remember . ' 
 she sob as she say this and disappear . Heriot still talk into her room . I think I hear a noise of the garden - door opening . a man come out rush at the ladder . I call in terror : ' Mr. Boddy , stop , sir . ' he push I savagely aside , pitch his whole force against the ladder . Heriot pull down Julia 's window ; he fall with a heavy thump on the ground , and I hear a shriek above . he try to spring to his foot , but drop , support himself on one of his hand , and cry : 
 ' all right ; no harm do ; how do you do , Mr. Boddy ? I think I would try one of the attic , as we be late , not to disturb the house . I ' m not hurt , I tell you , ' he cry as loud as he could . 
 the usher 's word be in a confusion of rage and inquiry . he command Heriot to stand on his leg , abuse he , ask he what he mean by it , accuse he of depravity , of crime , of disgraceful conduct , and attempt to pluck he from the spot . 
 ' hand off I , ' say Heriot ; ' I can help myself . the youngster will help I , and we will go round to the front door . I hope , sir , you will behave like a gentleman ; make no row here , Mr. Boddy , if you have   any respect for people inside . we be upset by Mr. Salter 's carriage ; it be damage my leg , I believe . have the goodness , sir , to go in by your road , and we will go round and knock at the front door in the proper way . we shall have to disturb the house after all . ' 
 Heriot insist . I be astonish to see Boddy obey he and leave we , after my dear Heriot have hop with his hand on my shoulder to the corner of the house front the road . while we be stand alone a light cart drive by . Heriot hail it , and hop up to the driver . 
 ' take I to London , there be a good fellow , ' he say ; ' I be a gentleman ; you need not   look fix . I will pay you well and thank you . but quick . haul I up , up ; here be my hand . by jingo ! this be pain . ' 
 the man say , ' scamp it out of school , sir ? ' 
 Heriot reply : ' Mum . rely on I when I tell you I be a gentleman . ' 
 ' well , if I pick up a gentleman , I can not   be do a bad business , ' say the man , haul he in tenderly . 
 Heriot sing to I in his sweet manner , ' good - bye , little Richie . Knock when five minute be over . God bless you , dear little lad ! Leg will get well by morning , never fear for I ; and we will meet somehow ; we will drink the Burgundy . no crying . kiss your hand to I . ' 
 I kiss my hand to he . I have no tear to shed ; my chest keep heave enormously . my friend be go . I stand in the road strain to hear the last of the wheel after they have long be silent . 




 CHAPTER VI . a tale of a GOOSE 

 from that hour till the day Heriot 's aunt come to see I , I live systematically out of myself in extreme flight of imagination , lock my door up , as it be , all the fast for the extreme stroke of Mr. Rippenger 's rod . he remark justly that I grow an impenetrably sullen boy , a constitutional rebel , a callous lump : and assure I that if my father would not pay for I , I at least should not escape my debt . the title of little impostor , transmit from the master 's mouth to the school in designation of one who have come to he as a young prince , and for whom he have not receive one penny 's indemnification , naturally cause I to have fight with several of the boy . Whereupon I be report : I be pray at to move my spirit , and flog to exercise my flesh . the prayer I soon learn to laugh to scorn . the flogging , after they be over , crown I with delicious sensation of martyrdom . even while the sting last I could say , it be for Heriot and Julia ! and it give I a wonderful penetration into the mournful ecstasy of love . Julia be send away to a relative by the sea - side , because , one of the housemaid tell I , she could not bear to hear of my be beat . Mr. Rippenger summon I to his private room to bid I inform he whether I have other relative besides my father , such as grandfather , grandmother , uncle , or aunt , or a mother . I dare say Julia would have lead I to break my word to my father by speak of old Riversley , a place I half long for since my father have grow so distant and dim to I ; but confession to Mr. Rippenger seem , as he say of Heriot 's behaviour to he , a gross breach of trust to my father ; so I refuse steadily to answer , and suffer the consequence now on my dear father 's behalf . Heriot 's aunt bring I a cake , and in a letter from he an extraordinary sum of money for a boy of my age . he write that he know I should want it to pay my debt for treat to the boy and keep they in good humour . he believe also that his people mean to have I for the Christmas holiday . the sum he send I be five pound , carefully enclose . I feel myself a prince again . the money be like a golden gate through which freedom twinkle a finger . forthwith I pay my debt , amount to two pound twelve shilling , and instruct a couple of day - boarder , commercial fellow , whose heavy and mysterious charge for commission run up a bill in no time , to prepare to bring we material for a feast on Saturday . Temple abominate the trading propensity of these boy . ' they never get lick and they have   always get money , at least I know they always get mine , ' say he ; ' but you and I and Heriot despise they . ' our position toward they be that of an encumbered aristocracy , and really they pay we great respect . the fact be that , when they have trust we , they be compel to continue obsequious , for Heriot have instill the sentiment in the school , that gentleman never fail to wipe out debt in the long run , so it be their interest to make we feel they know we to be gentleman , who be at some time or other sure to pay , and thus also they operate on our conscience . from which it follow that one title of superiority among we , rank next in the order of nobility to the dignity confer by Mr. Rippenger 's rod , be the be down in their book . Temple and I walk in the halo of unlimited credit like more than mortal twin . I give an order for four bottle of champagne . 
 on the Friday evening Catman walk out with we . his studious habit endear he to we immensely , owe to his have his head in his book on all occasion , and a walk under his superintendence be first cousin to liberty . some boy roam ahead , some lag behind , while Catman turn over his page , sound the return only when it grow dark . the rumour of the champagne have already intoxicate the boy . there be a companion and most auspicious rumour that Boddy be go to be absent on Saturday . if so , we say , we may drink our champagne under Catman 's nose and he be none the wiser . Saddlebank undertake to manage our feast for we . come home over the down , just upon twilight , Temple and I see Saddlebank carry a long withy upright . we ask he what it be for . he shout back : ' it be for fortune . you keep the rear guard . ' then we see he follow a man and a flock of geese , and imitate the action of the man with his green wand . as we be ready to laugh at anything Saddlebank do , we laugh at this . the man walk like one half asleep , and appear to wake up now and then to find that he be right in the middle of his geese , and then he wait , and Saddlebank wait behind he . presently the geese pass a lane lead off the down . we see Saddlebank duck his wand in a coax way , like an angler drop his fly for fish ; he make all sort of curious easy flourish against the sky and branch up the lane . we strike after he , little suspecting that he have a goose in front , but he have ; he have cut one of the loiterer off from the flock ; and to see he handle his wand on either side his goose , encourage it to go forward , and remonstrate , and address it in bit of Latin , and the creature patter stiff and astonished , send we in a dance of laughter . 
 ' what have you do , old saddle ? ' say Temple , though it be perfectly clear what Saddlebank have do . 
 ' I have   carve off a slice of Michaelmas , ' say Saddlebank , and he hew the air to flick delicately at his goose 's head . 
 ' what do you mean a slice ? ' say we . 
 we want to be certain the goose be capture booty . Saddlebank would talk nothing but his fun . Temple fetch a roar sigh : 
 ' oh ! how good this goose would be with our champagne . ' 
 the idea seize and enrapture I . ' Saddlebank , I will buy he off you , ' I say . 
 ' chink will not   flavour he , ' say Saddlebank , still at his business : ' here , you two , cut back by the down and try all your might to get a dozen apple before Catman count head at the door , and you hold your tongue . ' 
 we shoot past the man with the geese I pity he clip a corner of the down , and by dint of hard running reach the main street , mad for apple , before Catman appear there . apple , champagne , and cake be now provide ; all that be leave to think of be the goose . we glorify Saddlebank 's cleverness to the boy . 
 ' by jingo ! what a treat you will have , ' Temple say among they , burst with our secret . 
 Saddlebank plead that he have miss his way on present himself ten minute after time . to I and Temple he breathe of goose , but he shun we ; he have no fun in he till Saturday afternoon , when Catman call out to hear if we be for cricket or a walk . 
 ' a walk on the down , ' say Saddlebank . 
 Temple and I echo he , and Saddlebank motion his hand as though he be wheedle his goose along . Saddlebank speak a word to my commissioner . I be to leave the arrangement for the feast to he , he say . John Salter be at home unwell , so Saddlebank be chief . no soon do we stand on the down than he gather we all in a circle , and take off his cap throw in it some slip of paper . we have to draw lot who should keep by Catman out of twenty - seven ; fifteen blank be mark . Temple dash his hand into the cap first ' like my luck , ' he remark , and pocket both fist as he begin strut away to hide his desperation at draw a blank . I buy a substitute for he at the price of half - a - crown , Drew , a fellow we be glad to get rid of ; he want five shilling . the feast be worth fifty , but to haggle about price show the sneak . he beg we to put by a taste for he ; he be groan out of hearing . the fifteen look so wretched when they see themselves divide from we that I give they a shill a - piece to console they . they take their instruction from Saddlebank as to how they be to surround Catman , and make he fancy we to be all in his neighbourhood ; and then we shake hand , they request we feebly to drink their health , and we say , ay , that we would . 
 Temple be in distress of spirit because of his have be ignominiously buy off . Saddlebank , however , put on such a pace that no one have leisure for melancholy . ' I will get you fellow up to boiling point , ' say he . there be a tremendously hot sun overhead . on a sudden he halt , exclaim : ' cook and gridiron ! what about sage and onion ? ' only Temple and I jump at the meaning of this . we draw lot for a messenger , and it be miserable to behold an unfortunate fellow touch Saddlebank 's hand contain the notched bit of stick , and find himself condemn to go and buy sage and onion somewhere , without know what it be for how could he guess we be go to cook a raw goose ! the lot fall to a boy name Barnshed , a big slow boy , half way up every class he be in , but utterly stupid out of school ; which make Saddlebank say : ' they will take it he be the bird that want stuff . ' barnshe be direct where to rejoin we . the other ask why he be trot after sage and onion . ' because he be an awful goose , ' say Saddlebank . 
 Temple and I think the word be out and hurrah , and back come barnshed . we have a task in persuade he to resume his expedition , as well as Saddlebank to forgive we . Saddlebank 's anger be excessive . we conciliate he by call he captain , and pretend to swear an oath of allegiance . he now lead we through a wood on to some field down to a shady dell , where we be to hold the feast in privacy . he do not descend it himself . vexatious as it be to see a tramp 's tent there , we nevertheless acknowledge the respectful greeting of the woman and the man with a few question about tentpeg , pot , and tin mug . Saddlebank remain aloft , keep a look - out for the day - school fellow , Chaunter , Davis , and Bystop , my commissioner . they do not keep we wait long . they have drive to the spot in a cart , accord to Saddlebank 's direction . our provision be in three large hamper . we praise their forethought loudly at the sight of an extra bottle of champagne , with two bottle of ginger - wine , two of currant , two of raisin , four pint bottle of ale , six of ginger - beer , a dutch cheese , a heap of tart , three sally - lunn , and four shillingsworth of toffy . Temple and I join our apple to the mass : a sight at which some of the boy exult aloud . the tramp - woman insist on spread thing out for we : ten yard off their child squat star : the man smoke and chaff we . 
 at last Saddlebank come run over the hill - side , make as if he mean to bowl down what look a black body of a baby against the sky , and shout , ' see , you fellow , here be a find ! ' he run through we , swing his goose up to the hamper , say that he have find the goose under a furze - bush . while the word be come out of his mouth , he see the tramp , and the male tramp 's eye and his meet . 
 the man have one eyebrow and his lip at one corner screw in a queer lift : he wink slowly . ' Odd ! be not   it ? ' he say . 
 Saddlebank shoulder round on we , and cry , ' confound you fellow ! here be a beastly place you have   pitch upon . ' his face be the colour of scarlet in patch . 
 ' now , I call it a beautiful place , ' say the man , ' and if you find goose hereabout grow ready for the fire , all but pluck , why , it be a bountiful place , I call it . ' 
 the woman try to keep he silent . but for they we should have move our encampment . ' why , of course , young gentleman , if you want to eat the goose , we will pluck it for you and cook it for you , all nice , ' they say . ' how can young gentleman do that for theirselve ? ' 
 it be clear to we we must have a fire for the goose . certain observation current among we about the necessity to remove the goose 's inside , and not to lose the giblet , which even the boy who name they confess his inability to recognize , incline the majority to accept the woman 's proposal . Saddlebank say it be on our head , then . 
 to revive his good humour , Temple uncork a bottle of champagne . the tramp - woman lend we a tin mug , and round it go . one boy say , ' that be a commencement ' ; another say , ' hang old Rippenger . ' Temple snap his finger , and Bystop , a farmer 's son , say , ' well , now I have   drunk champagne ; I mean to before I die ! ' Most of the boy seem puzzled by it . as for I , my heart spring up in I like a colt turn out of stable to graze . I determine that the humblest of my retainer should feed from my table , and drink to my father 's and Heriot 's honour , and I pour out champagne for the woman , who just sip , and the man , who vow he prefer beer . a spoonful of the mashed tart I send to each of the child . only one , the eld , a girl about a year old than I , or young , with black eyebrow and rough black hair , refuse to eat or drink . 
 ' let she bide , young gentleman , ' say a woman ; ' she be a regular obstinate , once she set in for it . ' 
 ' ah ! ' say the man , ' I have   see pig druv , and I have   see iron bent double . she be hard ' n both , once she take ' t into her head . ' 
 ' by jingo , she be pig - iron ! ' cry Temple , and sigh , ' oh , dear old Heriot ! ' 
 I fling myself beside he to talk of our lose friend . 
 a great commotion stir the boy . they shriek at behold their goose vanish in a pot for stew . they want roast - goose , they exclaim , not boil ; who care for boil goose ! but the woman ask they how it be possible to roast a goose on the top of wood - flame , where there be nothing to hang it by , and nothing would come of it except smoke bone ! 
 the boy groan in consternation , and Saddlebank sow discontent by grumble , ' now you see what your jolly new acquaintance have do for you . ' 
 so we play at catch with the dutch cheese , and afterwards bowl it for long - stopping , when , to the disgust of Saddlebank and other , down run the black - haired girl and catch the ball clean at wicket - distance . as soon as she have do it she be ashamed , and slunk away . 
 the boy call out , ' now , then , pig - iron ! ' 
 one fellow enrage I by throw an apple that hit she in the back . we exchange half - a - dozen blow , whereupon he consent to apologize , and roar , ' Hulloa , pig - iron , sorry if I hurt you . ' 
 Temple urge I to insist on the rascal be go on his knee for fling at a girl . 
 ' why , ' say Chaunter , ' you be the first to call her pig - iron . ' 
 Temple declare he be a blackguard if he say that . I make the girl take a piece of toffy . 
 ' aha ! ' Saddlebank grumble , ' this come of the precious company you would keep in spite of my caution . ' 
 the man tell we to go it , for he like to observe young gentleman enjoy themselves . Temple toss he a pint bottle of beer , with an injunction to he to shut his trap . 
 ' now , you talk my mother tongue , ' say the man ; ' you be what go by the name of a learn gentleman . thank ye , sir . you will be a counsellor some day . ' 
 ' I will not   get off thief , I can tell you , ' say Temple . he be the son of a barrister . 
 ' nor you will not   help cook their goose for they , may be , ' say the man . ' well , kindness be kindness , all over the world . ' 
 the woman storm at he to command he not to anger the young gentleman , for Saddlebank be swear awfully in an undertone . he answer they that he be the mild lamb afloat . 
 Despairing of the goose , we resolve to finish the cold repast await we . the dutch cheese have be bowl into bit . with a portion of the mashed tart on it , and champagne , it taste excellently ; toffy to follow . those boy who choose ginger - wine have it , and drank , despise . the ginger - beer and ale , apple and sallylunn , be reserve for supper . my mind become like a drive sky , with glimpse of my father and Heriot burst through . 
 ' if I be not a prince , I be a nobleman , ' I say to Temple . 
 he reply , ' Army or Navy . I do not   much care which . we be sure of a foreign war some time . then you will see fellow rise : lieutenant , captain , colonel , General quick as barrel pop at a bird . I should like to be Governor of Gibraltar . ' 
 ' I will come and see you , Temple , ' say I. 
 ' do ! old Richie , ' he say , grasp my hand warmly . 
 ' the truth be , Temple , ' I confide to he , ' I have   an uncle - I mean a grandfather - of enormous property ; he own half Hampshire , I believe , and hat my father like poison . I will not   stand it . you have   see my father , have not   you ? gentleman never forget their servant , Temple . let us drink lot more champagne . I wish you and I be knight rid across that country there , as they use to , and you say , " I wonder whether your father 's at home in the castle expect our arrival . " ' 
 ' the Baron ! ' say Temple . ' he be like a Baron , too . his health . your health , sir ! it be just the wine to drink it in , Richie . he be one of the man I look up to . it be odd he never come to see you , because he be fond of you ; the right sort of father ! big man can not   be always look after little boy . not that we be so young , though , now . lot of fellow of our age have do thing fellow write about . I feel ' Temple sit up swell his chest to deliver an important sentiment ; ' I feel uncommonly thirsty . ' 
 so do I. we attribute it to the air of the place , Temple go so far as to say that it come off the chalk , which somehow stick in the throat . 
 ' saddleback , do not   look glum , ' say Temple . ' Lord , Richie , you should hear my father plead in Court with his wig on . they use to say at home I be a clever boy when I be a baby . saddleback , you have   look glum all the afternoon . ' 
 ' treat your superior respectfully , ' Saddlebank retort . 
 the tramp be irritate he . that tramp have never leave off smoking and lean on his arm since we first see he . two boy name Hackman and Montague , not bad fellow , grow desirous of a whiff from his pipe . they have it , and lie down silent , back to back . bystop be lead away in a wretched plight . two other , Paynter and Ashworth , attack the apple , render desperate by thirst . Saddlebank repel they furiously . he harangue those who might care to listen . 
 ' you fellow , by George ! you shall eat the goose , I tell you . you have   spoil everything , and I tell you , whether you like it or not , you shall have apple with it , and sage and onion too . I do not   ask for thank . and I propose to post outpost in the wood to keep watch . ' 
 he want we to draw lot again . his fun have entirely depart from he ; all he think of be see the goose out of the pot . I have a feeling next to hatred for one who could talk of goose . Temple must have share it . 
 ' we ' ve   no real captain now dear old Heriot 's gone , ' he say . ' the school 's topsy - turvy : we be like a lot of thing rattle in a box . oh , dear ! how I do like a good commander . on he go , you after he , never mind what happen . ' 
 a pair of inseparable friend , Happitt and Larkins , nickname Happy - go - lucky , be roll arm - in - arm , declare they be perfectly sober , and , for a proof of it , try to direct their foot upon a lump of chalk , and marching , and miss it . up come Chaunter to they : ' fat goose ? ' he say - no more . both the boy rush straight as far as they could go ; both sing out , ' I be do ! ' and they be . 
 Temple and I contemplate these proceeding as matter belong to the ordinary phenomenon of feast . we agree that gentleman be always the last to drop , and be assure , therefore , of our live out the field ; but I dread the moment of the goose 's appearance , and I think he do also . Saddlebank 's pertinacity in withhold the cool ginger - beer and the apple offend we deeply ; we should have conspire against he have we repose confidence in our leg and our tongue . 
 Twilight be around we . the tramp - child lie in little bundle in one tent ; another be be build by the woman and the girl . overhead I count number of star , all small ; and light in the valley - light of palace to my imagination . star and tramp seem to I to go together . house imprison we , I think a lost father be never to be discover by remain in they . plunge among dark green leaf , smell wood - smoke , at night ; at morning wake up , and the world alight , and you stand high , and mark the hill where you will see the next morning and the next , morning after morning , and one morning the dear person in the world surprising you just before you wake : I think this a heavenly pleasure . but , observe the narrowness of the tent , it strike I there would be snore companion . I feel so intensely sensitive , that the very idea of a snore give I tremour and qualm : it be associate with the sense of fat . Saddlebank have the lid of the pot in his hand ; we smell the goose , and he cry , ' now for supper ; now for it ! Halloa , you fellow ! ' 
 ' bother it , Saddlebank , you will make Catman hear you , ' say Temple , wipe his forehead . 
 I perspire coldly . 
 ' Catman ! he be be at it for the last hour and a half , ' Saddlebank reply . 
 one boy run up : he be ready , and the only one who be . presently Chaunter rush by . 
 ' barnshe 's in custody ; I be away home , ' he say , pass . 
 we star at the black opening of the dell . 
 ' oh , it be Catman ; we do not   mind he , ' Saddlebank reassure we ; but we hear ominous voice , and perceive people stand over a prostrate figure . then we hear a voice too well know to we . it say , ' the explanation of a pupil in your charge , Mr. Catman , be send bareface into the town a scholar of mine - for sage and onion ... ' 
 ' Old Rippenger ! ' breathe Temple . 
 we sit paralyze . now we understand the folly of despatch a donkey like barnshe for sage and onion . 
 ' oh , what ass we have be ! ' Temple continue . ' come along - we run for it ! come along , Richie ! they be pick up the fellow like windfall . ' 
 I tell he I would not run for it ; in fact , I distrust my leg ; and he be staggering , answer Saddlebank 's reproach for have come among tramp . 
 ' Temple , I see you , sir ! ' call Mr. Rippenger . poor Temple have advance into the firelight . 
 with the instinct to defeat the master , I crawl in the line of the shadow to the farth side of a tent , where I feel a hand clutch mine . ' hide I , ' say I ; and the curtain of the tent be raise . after squeeze through box and straw , I lie flat , cover by a mat smelling of abominable cheese , and feel a head outside it on my chest . several time Mr. Rippenger pronounce my name in the way habitual to he in anger : ' rye ! ' 
 Temple 's answer be inaudible to I . Saddlebank speak , and other boy , and the man and the woman . then a light be thrust in the tent , and the man say , ' I deceive you , sir ! see for yourself , to satisfy yourself . here be our little un lay warm , and a girl there , head on the mat , go down to join her tribe at Lipcombe , and one of our woman sleep here , and all tell . but for you to suspect I of combine Thank ye , sir . you have   get my word as a man . ' 
 the light go away . my chest be relieve of the weight on it . I sit up , and the creature who have be kind to I lay mat and straw on the ground , and draw my head on her shoulder , where I sleep fast . 




 CHAPTER VII . a free life on the road 

 I wake very early , though I have take kindly to my pillow , as I find by my have an arm round my companion 's neck , and her finger intertwiste with mine . for awhile I lay look at her eye , which have every imaginable light and signification in they ; they advise I to lie quiet , they laugh at my wonder , they say , ' dear little fellow ! ' they flash as from under a cloud , darken , flash out of it , seem to dip in water and shine , and be sometimes like a view into a forest , sometimes intensely sunny , never quite still . I trust she , and could have sleep again , but the sight of the tent stupefy I ; I fancy the sky have fall , and gasp for air ; my head be extremely dizzy too ; not one idea in it be keep from wheel . this confusion of my head fly to my leg when , imitate she , I rise to go forth . in a fit of horror I think , ' I ' ve   forget how to walk ! ' 
 summon my manful resolution , I make the attempt to step across the child swaddle in matting and straw and old gown or petticoat . the necessity for do it with a rush seize I after the first step . I pitch over one little bundle , right on to the figure of a sleep woman . all she do be to turn round , murmur , ' Naughty Jackie . ' my companion pull I along gravely , and once in the air , with a good breath of it in my chest , I feel tall and strong , and know what have occur . the tent where I have sleep strike I as more curious than my own circumstance . I lift my face to the sky ; it be just sunrise , beautiful ; bit of long and curl cloud brush any way close on the blue , and rosy and white , deliciously cool ; the grass be all grey , our dell in shadow , and the top of the tree burn , a few bird twitter . 
 I suck a blade of grass . 
 ' I wish it be all water here , ' I say . 
 ' come and have a drink and a bathe , ' say my companion . 
 we go down the dell and over a juniper slope , remind I of my day at John Salter 's house and the last of dear Heriot . rather to my shame , my companion beat I at run ; she be very swift , and my leg be stiff . 
 ' can you swim ? ' she ask I . 
 ' I can row , and swim , and fence , and ride , and fire a pistol , ' I say . 
 ' oh , dear , ' say she , after eye I enviously . I could see that I have check a recital of her accomplishment . 
 we arrive at a clear stream in a gentleman 's park , where grass roll smooth as sea - water on a fine day , and cow and horse be feed . 
 ' I can catch that horse and mount he , ' she say . 
 I be astonish . 
 ' straddle ? ' 
 she nod down for ' yes . ' 
 ' no saddle ? ' 
 she nod level for ' no . ' 
 my respect for she return . but she could not swim . 
 ' only up to my knee , ' she confess . 
 ' have a look at I , ' say I ; and I strip and shoot into the water , happy as a fish , and think how much nice it be than champagne . my enjoyment make she so envious that she pluck off her stocking , and come in as far as she dare . I call to she . ' you be like a cow , ' and she show her tooth , bid I not say that . 
 ' a cow ! a cow ! ' I repeat , in my superior pleasure . 
 she spin out in a breath , ' if you say that , I will run away with every bit of your clothe , and you will come out and run about naked , you will . ' 
 ' now I float , ' be my answer , ' now I dive ' ; and when I come up she welcome I with a big bright grin . 
 a smart run in the heat dry I . I dress , find half my money on the grass . she ask I to give she one of those bit - a shilling . I give she two , upon which she ask I , invitingly , if ever I toss . I reply that I never toss for money ; but she have catch a shilling , and I could not resist guess ' head , ' and win ; the same with her second shilling . she hand they to I sullenly , sob , yet she would not take they back . 
 ' by - and - by you give I another two , ' she say , grow lively again . we agree that it would be a good thing if we enter the village and buy something . none of the shop be open . we walk through the churchyard . I say , ' here be where dead people be bury . ' 
 ' I will dance if you talk about dead people , ' say she , and begin whoop at the pitch of her voice . on my wishing to know why she do it , her reply be that it be to make the dead people hear . my feeling be strange : the shop not open , and no live people to be see . we climb tree , and sit on a branch talk of bird ' egg till hunger drive we to the village street , where , near the public - house , we meet the man - tramp , who whistle . 
 he be rather amusing . he remark that he put no question to I , because he put no question to anybody , because answer excite he about subject that have no particular interest to he , and do not benefit he to the extent of a pipe of ' tobacco ; and all through not be inquisitive , yesterday afternoon he have obtain , as if it have be chuck into his lap , a fine - flavour fat goose honourably for his supper , besides bottle of ale , bottle of ginger - pop , and a fair - earn half - crown . that be through his not be inquisitive , and he be not go to be inquisitive now , know I for a gentleman : my master have tip he half - a - crown . 
 fortunately for he , and perhaps for my liberty , he employ a verb marvellously enlighten to a schoolboy . I tip he another half - crown . he thank I , observe that there be day when you lay on your back and the sky rain apple ; while there be other day when you wear your finger down to the first joint to catch a flea . such be Fortune ! 
 in a friendly manner he advise I to go to school ; if not there , then to go home . my idea , which I have only partly conceive , be to have a look at Riversley over a hedge , kiss my aunt Dorothy unaware , and fly subsequently in search of my father . Breakfast , however , be my immediate thought . he and the girl sit down to breakfast at the inn as my guest . we eat muttonchop and egg , and drink coffee . after it , though I have no suspicion , I notice that the man grow thoughtful . he propose to I , suppose I have no objection against slow travelling , to join company for a couple of day , if I be for Hampshire , which I state be the county I mean to visit . 
 ' well then , here now , come along , d ' ye see , look , ' say he , ' I must not   be pounce on , and no miss young gentleman in my society , and I take half - a - crown for his absence ; that will not   do . you get on pretty well with the gal , and that be a screaming farce : none of we do . Lord ! she look down on such scum as we . she be gipsy blood , true sort ; everything 's sausage that get into their pocket , no matter what it be when it be out . well then , now , here , you and the gal go t ' other side o ' Bed'lming , and you wait for we on the heath , and we will be there to comfort ye ' fore dark . be it a fister ? ' 
 he hold out his hand ; I agree ; and he remark that he now count a breakfast in the list of his gain from never ask question . 
 I be glad enough to quit the village in a hurry , for the driver of the geese , or a man dreadfully resemble he , pass I near the public - house , and attack my conscience on the cowardly side , which be , I fear , the first to awaken , and always the lively half while we be undisciplined . I would have pay he money , but the idea of a conversation with he indicate the road back to school . my companion relate her history . she belong to a Hampshire gipsy tribe , and have be on a visit to a relative down in the East county , who die on the road , leave she to be bring home by these tramp : she call they mumper , and make face when she speak of they . gipsy , she say , be a different sort : gipsy camp in gentleman 's park ; gipsy , horse , fiddle , and the wide world that be what she like . the wide world she describe as a heath , where you look and never see the end of it I let she talk on . for I to talk of my affair to a girl without bonnet and boot would have be absurd . otherwise , her society please I : she be so like a boy , and unlike any boy I know . 
 my mental occupation on the road be to calculate how many hill - top I should climb before I behold Riversley . the Sunday bell sound homely from village to village as soon as I be convince that I hear no bell summon boarder to Rippenger 's school . the shop in the village continue shut ; however , I tell the girl they should pay I for it next day , and we have an interesting topic in discuss as to the various thing we would buy . she be for bright riband and draper 's stuff , I for pastry and letter - paper . the smell of people 's dinner unite our appetite . go through a village I see a man carry a great bake pie , smell overpoweringly , so that to ask he his price for it be a natural impulse with I . ' what ! sell my Sunday dinner ? ' he say , and appear ready to drop the dish . nothing stop his staring until we have finish a plateful a - piece and some beer in his cottage among his family . he want to take I in alone . ' she be a common tramp , ' he say of the girl . 
 ' that be a lie , ' she answer . 
 of course I would not leave she hungry outside , so in the end he reluctantly invite we both , and introduce we to his wife . 
 ' here be a young gentleman ask a bit o ' dinner , and a young I - d'n - know - what be after the same ; I leave it to you , missus . ' 
 his wife take it off his shoulder in good humour , say it be lucky she make the pie big enough for her family and stray . they would not accept more than a shilling for our joint repast . the man say that be the account to a farthing , if I be too proud to be a poor man 's guest , and insist on treat he like a public . perhaps I would shake hand at part ? I do cordially , and remember he when people be not so civil . they want to know whether we have make a runaway match of it . the fun of pass a boys'-school and hear the usher threaten to punish one fellow for stray from rank , entertain I immensely . I laugh at they just as the stupid people we meet laugh at I , which be unpleasant for the time ; but I know there be not a single boy who would not have change place with I , only give he the chance , though my companion be a gipsy girl , and she certainly do look odd company for a gentleman 's son in a tea - garden and public - house parlour . at nightfall , however , I be glad of she and she of I , and we walk hand in hand . I narrate tale of roman history . it be very well for she so say , ' I will mother you , ' as we lie down to sleep ; I discover that she would never have hoot over churchyard graf in the night . she confess she believe the devil go about in the night . our bed be a cart under a shed , our bed - clothe fern - leaf and armful of straw . the shaft of the cart be down , so we lay between upright and level , and awakening in the early light I find our four leg hang over the seat in front . ' how you have be kick ! ' say I. she accuse I of the same . next minute she point over the side of the cart , and I see the tramp 's horse and his tent beneath a broad roadside oak - tree . her face be comical , just like a boy 's who think he have escape and be catch . ' let us run , ' she say . prefer positive independence , I follow she , and then she tell I that she have overhear the tramp last night swear I be as good as a fistful of half - crown lose to he if he miss I . the image of Rippenger 's school overshadow I at this communication . with some melancholy I say : ' you will join your friend , will not   you ? ' 
 she snap her finger : ' Mumpers ! ' and walk on carelessly . 
 we be now on the great heath . they bring the memory of my father vividly ; the smell of the air half incline I to turn my step toward London , I grow so full of longing for he . nevertheless I resolve to have one gaze at Riversley , my aunt Dorothy , and Sewis , the old grey - brown butler , and the lamb that have grow a sheep ; wonderful contrast to my grand king of England career . my first clear recollection of Riversley be here , like an outline of a hill see mile away . I might have shed a tear or two out of love for my father , have not the thought that I be a very queer boy displace his image . I could not but be a very queer boy , such a lot of thing happen to I . suppose I join the gipsy ? my companion wish I to . she have brother , horse - dealer , beautiful fiddler . suppose I learn the fiddle ? suppose I learn their language and go about with they and become king of the gipsy ? my companion shake her head ; she could not encourage this ambitious idea because she have never hear of a king of the gipsy or a queen either . ' we fool people , ' she say , and offend I , for our school believe in a gipsy king , and one fellow , Hackman , use to sing a song of a gipsy king ; and it be as much as to say that my schoolfellow be fool , every one of they . I accuse she of tell lie . she grin angrily . ' I do not   tell them to friend , ' she say . we have a quarrel . the truth be , I be enrage at the sweeping out of my prospect of rise to distinction among the gipsy . after breakfast at an inn , where a waiter laugh at we to our face , and we feed scowl , shy , and hungry , we have another quarrel . I inform she of my opinion that gipsy could not tell fortune . 
 ' they can , and you come to my mother and my aunt , and see if they can not   tell your fortune , ' say she , in a fury . 
 ' yes , and that be how they fool people , ' say I. I enjoy see the flash of her tooth . but my daring of she to look I in the eye and swear on her oath she believe the fortune true one , send she into a fit of sullenness . 
 ' go along , you nasty little fellow , your shadow be not   half a yard , ' she say , and I could smile at that ; my shadow stretch half across the road . we have a quarrelsome day wherever we go ; rarely walk close together till nightfall , when she edge up to my hand , with , ' I say , I will keep you warm to - night , I will . ' she hug I almost too tight , but it be warm and social , and help to the triumph of a feeling I have that nothing make I regret run away from Rippenger 's school . 
 an adventure befell we in the night . a farmer 's wife , whom we ask for a drink of water after dark , lend we an old blanket to cover we in a dry ditch on receive our promise not to rob the orchard . an old beggar come limp by we , and want to share our covering . my companion sink right under the blanket to peer at he through one of its hole . he stand enormous above I in the moonlight , like an apparition touch earth and sky . 
 ' cold , cold , ' he whine : ' there be ne'er a bad off but there be a well off . young un ! ' his word disperse the fancy that he be something horrible , or else my father in disguise go to throw off his rag , and shine , and say he have find I . ' be ye one , or be ye two ? ' he ask . 
 I reply that we be two . 
 ' then I will come and lie in the middle , ' say he . 
 ' you can not ; there be no room , ' I sing out . 
 ' Lord , ' say he , ' there be room for any reckon o ' empty stomach in a ditch . ' 
 ' no , I prefer to be alone : good - night , ' say I. 
 ' why ! ' he exclaim , ' where ha ' you be t ' learn language ? Halloa ! ' 
 ' please , leave I alone ; it be my intention to go to sleep , ' I say , vex at have to conciliate he ; he have a big stick . 
 ' Oho ! ' go the beggar . then he recommence :
