 ' tell I you have   stole nothing in your life ! you have   stole a gentleman 's tongue , I know the ring o ' that . how come you out here ? who be your mate there down below ? now , see , I be go to lift my stick . ' 
 at these menacing word the girl jump out of the blanket , and I call to he that I would rouse the farmer . 
 ' why ... because I be go to knock down a apple or two on your head ? ' he inquire , in a tone of reproach . ' it be a young woman you have   get there , eh ? well , odd grow odder , like the man who turn three shilling into five . now , you gi ' I a lie under your blanket , I will knock down a apple apiece . if ever you have   taste gin , you will say a apple at night 's a cordial , though it do not   intoxicate . ' 
 the girl whisper in my ear , ' he be lame as duck . ' her meaning seize I at once ; we both spring out of the ditch and run , drag our blanket behind we . he pursue , but we elude he , and drop on a quiet sleep - place among furze . next morning , when we take the blanket to the farm - house , we hear that the old wretch have traduce our character , and get a breakfast through charge we with the robbery of the apple - tree . I prove our innocence to the farmer 's wife by put down a shilling . the sight of it satisfy she . she comb my hair , bring I a bowl of water and a towel , and then give we a bowl of milk and bread , and dismiss we , tell I I have a fair face and dare - devil write on it : as for the girl , she say of she that she know gipsy at a glance , and what God Almighty make they for there be no guessing . this set I think all through the day , ' what can they have be make for ? ' I buy a red scarf for the girl , and other thing she fix her eye on , but I lose a great deal of my feeling of fellowship with she . ' I dare say they be make for fun , ' I think , when people laugh at we now , and I laugh also . 
 I have a day of rollick laughter , puzzle the girl , who could only grin two or three second at a time , and then star like a dog that wait for his master to send he off again run , the corner of her mouth twitch for I to laugh or speak , exactly as a dog might wag his tail . I study she in the light of a harmless sort of unaccountable creature ; witness at any rate for the fact that I have escape from school . 
 we loiter half the morning round a cricketer ' booth in a field , where there be moderately good cricketing . the people think it of first - rate quality . I tell they I know a fellow who could bowl out either eleven in an hour and a half . one of the man frighten I by say , ' by Gearge ! I will in with you into a gig , and off with you after that ther ' faller . ' he pretend to mean it , and start up . I watch he without flinch . he remark that if I ' have not cut my lucky from school , and toss my cap for a free life , he be ' whatever may be express by a slap on the thigh . we play a single - wicket side game , he give I six run , and crestfallen he be to find himself beat ; but , as I let he know , one who have bowl to Heriot for hour and stand against Saddlebank 's bowling , be a tough customer , never mind his age . 
 this man offer I his friendship . he make I sit and eat beside he at the afternoon dinner of the eleven , and send plateful of food to the girl , where she be allow to squat ; and say he , ' you and I will tie a knot , and be friend for life . ' 
 I reply , ' with pleasure . ' 
 we nod over a glass of ale . in answer to his question , I state that I like farm , I would come and see his farm , I would stay with he two or three day , I would give he my address if I have one , I be on my way to have a look at Riversley Grange . 
 ' hey ! ' say he , ' Riversley Grange ! well , to be sure now ! I be a tenant of Squire Beltham 's , and a right sort of landlord , too . ' 
 ' oh ! ' say I , ' he be my grandfather , but I do not   care much about he . ' 
 ' Lord ! ' say he . ' what ! be you the little boy , why , Master Harry Richmond that be carry off in the night , and the old squire shut up door for a fortnight , and make out you be go in a hearse ! why , I know all about you , you see . and back you be , hurrah ! the squire will be hearty , that he will . we have   notice a change in he ever since you leave . Gout be be at his leg , off and on , a deal shrewd . but he rid to hound , and din his tenant still , that he do ; he be one o ' th ' old style . everything you eat and drink be off his estate , the day he din his tenant . no humbug 'bout old Squire Beltham . 
 I ask he if Sewis be alive . 
 ' why , old Sewis , ' say he , ' you be acquaint with old sewis ? why , of course you be . yes , old Sewis 's alive , Master Harry . and you bet I at single - wicket ! that will be something to relate to them all . by Gearge , if I do not   think I would get a nettle in my fist when I see you pitch into my stump . dash it ! think I. but th ' old squire will be proud of you , that he will . my farm lie three mile away . you look at a crow fly due South - east five minute from Riversley , and he be over Throckham farm , and there I will drive ye to - night , and to - morrow , clean and tidy out o ' my wife 's soap and water , straight to Riversley . do , eh ? my name 's Eckerthy . no matter where you come from , here you be , eh , Master Harry ? and I see you last time in a donkey - basket , and here you come in breech and defy I to singlewicket , and you bet I too ! ' 
 he laugh for jollity . an extraordinary number of emotion have possession of I : the most intelligible one be a restless vexation at myself , as the principal person concern , for not experience anything like the farmer 's happiness . I prefer a gipsy life to Riversley . gipsy be on the road , and that road lead to my father . I endeavour to explain to Farmer Eckerthy that I be travel in this direction merely to have a short look at Riversley ; but it be impossible ; he could not understand I . the more I try , the more he press I to finish my glass of ale , which have nothing to do with it . I drank , nevertheless , and I suppose say many funny thing in my anxiety that the farmer should know what I mean ; he laugh enough . 
 while he be field against the opposite eleven , the tramp come into the booth , and we have a match of cunning . 
 ' Schoolmaster be out after you , young gentleman , ' say he , advise I to hurry along the road if I seek to baffle pursuit . 
 I pretend alarm , and then say , ' oh , you will stand by I , ' and treat he to ale . 
 he assure I I leave as many track behind I as if I go spill a box of lucifer - match . he be always for my hastening on until I order fresh ale for he . the girl and he grimace at one another in contempt . so we remain see the game out . by the time the game end , the tramp have drunk number of glass of ale . 
 ' a fine - flavour fat goose , ' he count his gain since the commencement of our acquaintance , ' bottle of ale and ginger - pop , two half - crown , more ale , and more to follow , let us hope . you only stick to your friend , young gentleman , will not   you , sir ? it be a hard case for a poor man like I if you do not . we be not   get such chance every morning of our life . do you perceive , sir ? I request you to inform I , do you perceive , sir ? I be muddle a bit , sir , but a man must look after his interest . ' 
 I perceive he be so muddle as to be unable to conceal that his interest be involve in my capture ; but I be merry too . Farmer Eckerthy deal the tramp a scatter slap on the back when he return to the booth , elated at have beat the enemy by a single run . 
 ' Master Harry Richmond go to Riversley to his grandfather in your company , you scoundrel ! ' he cry in a rage , after listen to he . ' I mean to drive he over . it be a comfortable ten - mile , and no more . but I say , Master Harry , what do you say to a peck o ' supper ? ' 
 he communicate to I confidentially that he do not like to seem to slink away from the other , who have make up their mind to stop and sup ; so we would drive home by moonlight , sing song . and so we do . I sit beside the farmer , the girl scramble into the hinder part of the cart , and the tramp stand moan , ' oh dear ! oh dear ! you go away to Riversley without your good friend . ' 
 I toss he a shilling . we sing beginning and end of song . the farmer look at the moon , and say , ' Lord ! she star at we ! ' then he sing : 
  ' the moon be shin on Latworth lea , 
  and where will she see such a jovial three 
  as we , boy , we ? and why be she pale ? 
  it be because she drink water instead of ale . ' 
 ' where be the remainder ? there be the song ! 
  " oh ! handsome Miss Gammon 
  have marry Lord Mammon , 
  and jilt her suitor , 
  all Cupid 's sharpshooter , 
  and go in a carriage 
  and six to her marriage , 
  sing hey ! for I have   land my salmon , my salmon ! " 
 where be the remainder ? I hear it th ' only time I ever be in London town , never rest till I have learn it , and now it be clean gone . what be come to I ? ' 
 he sing to ' Mary of Ellingmere ' and another maid of some place , and a loud song of Britons . 
 it be startling to I to wake up to twilight in the open air and silence , for I be unaware that I have fall asleep . the girl have rouse I , and we creep down from the cart . horse and farmer be quite motionless in a green hollow beside the roadway . look across field and fir plantation , I behold a house in the strange light of the hour , and my heart begin beat ; but I be overcome with shyness , and say to myself , ' no , no , that be not Riversley ; I be sure it be not ' ; though the certainty of it be , in my tooth , refute I . I run down the field to the park and the bright little river , and gaze . when I could say , ' yes , it be Riversley ! ' I turn away , hurt even to a sense of smart pain , without know the cause . I dare say it be true , as the girl declare subsequently , that I behave like one in a fit . I drop , and I may have roll my body and cry . an indefinite resentment at Riversley be the feeling I grow conscious of after very fast walking . I would not have accept breakfast there . 
 about mid - day , cross a stubble - field , the girl meet a couple of her people - man . near evening we enter one of their tent . the woman set up a cry , ' Kiomi ! Kiomi ! ' like a rise rookery . their eye and tooth make such a flashing as when you dabble a hand in a dark waterpool . the strange tongue they talk , with a kind of peck of the voice at a word , rapid , never high or low , and then a slide of similar tone all round , not musical , but catch and incessant , give I an idea that I have fall upon a society of bird , exceedingly curious one . they welcome I kindly , each of they look I in the face a bright second or so . I have two help from a splendid pot of broth that hang over a fire in the middle of the tent . 
 Kiomi be my companion 's name . she have sister Adeline and Eveleen , and brother Osric and William , and she have a cousin a prizefighter . ' that be what I will be , ' say I. Fiddling for money be not a prospect that charm I , though it be pleasant lying in Kiomi 's arm to hear Osric play we off to sleep ; it be like float down one of a number of visible river ; I could see they converge and break away while I float smoothly , and a wonderful fair country nod drowsy . from that to cock - crow at a stride . sleep be no more than the passage through the arch of a canal . Kiomi and I be on the heath before sunrise , jump gravel - pit , chase sandpiper , mimic pewit ; it seem to I I have only just hear the last of Osric 's fiddle when yellow colour fill in along the sky over Riversley . the curious dark thrill of the fiddle in the tent by night seem close up behind the sun , and my quiet fancy as I lay drop to sleep , follow I like unobtrusive shadow during daylight , or , to speak truthfully , till about dinner - time , when I think of nothing but the great stew - pot . we feed on plenty ; nicer food than Rippenger 's , minus pudding . after dinner I be ready for mischief . my sensation on see Kiomi beg of a gentleman be remarkable . I reproach she . she show I sixpence shin in the palm of her hand . I give she a shilling to keep she from it . she have now get one and sixpence , she say : meaning , I suppose upon reflection , that her begging have produce that sum , and therefore it be a good thing . the money remain in my pocket amount to five shilling and a penny . I offer it to Kiomi 's mother , who refuse to accept it ; so do the father , and Osric also . I might think of they , they observe , on my return to my own house : they point at Riversley . ' no , ' say I , ' I shall not   go there , you may be sure . ' the woman grin , and the man yawn . the business of the man appear to be to set to work about everything as if they have a fire inside they , and then to stretch out their leg and lie on their back , exactly as if the fire have go out . Excepting Osric 's practice on the fiddle , and the father 's bringing in and lead away of horse , they do little work in my sight but brown themselves in the sun . one morning Osric 's brother come to our camp with their cousin the prizefighter a young man of light complexion , upon whom I gaze , remember John Thresher 's reverence for the heroical profession . Kiomi whisper some story concern her brother have meet the tramp . I do not listen ; I be full of a temp , owe to two cause : a studious admiration of the smart young prizefighter 's person , and wrathful disgust at he for call Kiomi his wife , and tell she he be prepared to marry she as soon as she play she harp like King David . the intense folly of his ask a girl to play like David make I despise he , but he be splendidly handsome and strong , and to see he put on the glove for a spar with big William , Kiomi 's brother , and evade and ward the huge blow , would have be a treat to other besides old John of Dipwell Farm . he have the agile grace of a leopard ; his waistcoat remind I of one ; he be like a piece of machinery in free action . please by my enthusiasm , he give I a lesson , promise I more . 
 ' he will be champion some day , ' say Kiomi , at gnaw upon an apple he have give she . 
 I knock the apple on the ground , and stamp on it . she slap my cheek . in a minute we stand in a ring . I behold the girl actually square at I . 
 ' fight away , ' I say , to conceal my shame , and imagine I could slip from her hit as easily as the prizefighter do from big William 's . I be mistaken . 
 ' oh ! you think I can not   defend myself , ' say Kiomi ; and rush in with one , two , quick as a cat , and cool as a statue . 
 ' fight , my merry one ; she take punishment , ' the prizefighter sing out . ' first blood to you , Kiomi ; uncork his claret , my duck ; straight at the nozzle , he see more lamp than shine in London , I warrant . make he lively , cook he ; tell he who teach you ; a downer to he , and I will marry you to - morrow ! ' 
 I conceive a fury against she as though she have injure I by appear the man 's property and I be get the bad of it ; her little fist shoot straight and hard as bar of iron ; she like fight ; she be at least my match . to avoid the disgrace of seriously strike she , or of be beat at an open exchange of blow , I make a feint , and catch she by the waist and throw she , not very neatly , for I fall myself in her grip . they have to pluck she from I by force . 
 ' and you have   go a course of tuition in wrestling , squire ? ' the prizefighter say to I rather savagely . 
 the other be cordial , and do not snarl at I for go to the rope , as he call it . Kiomi desire to renew the conflict . I say aloud : 
 ' I never fight girl , and I tell you I do not   like their lick I . ' 
 ' then you come down to the river and wash your face , ' say she , and pull I by the finger , and when she have wash my face clear of blood , kiss I . I think she taste of the prizefighter . 
 late in the afternoon Osric propose that he and I and the prizefighter should take a walk . I stipulate for Kiomi to be of the party , which be allow , and the gipsy - woman shake my hand as though I have be depart on a long expedition , entreat I not to forget they , and never to think evil of poor gipsy - folk . 
 ' why , I mean to stay with you , ' say I. 
 they grin delightedly , and say I must be back to see they break up camp in the evening . every two or three minute Kiomi nudge my elbow and point behind , where I see the woman wave their coloured neckerchief . out of sight of our tent we come in view of the tramp . Kiomi say , ' hide ! ' I dive into a furze dell . the tramp approach , call out for news of I . now at Rippenger 's school , thank to Heriot , lying be not the fashion ; still I have hear boy lie , and they can let it out of their mouth like a fish , so lively , simple , and solid , that you could fancy a master have ask they for it and they answer , ' there it be . ' but boy can not lie in one key spontaneously , a number of they to the same effect , as my friend here do . I be off , they say ; all swing round to signify the direction of my step ; my plan be hint at ; particular be not state on the plea that there should be no telling ; it be remark that I ought to have fair play and ' law . ' Kiomi say she hop he would not catch I . the tramp wince with vexation , and the gipsy chaff he . I thank they in my heart for their loyal conduct . creep under cover of the dell I pass round to the road over a knoll of fir as quick as my foot could carry I , and have just cry , ' now I be safe ' ; when a lady step from a carriage on the road , catch I in her arm and hug I blind . it be my aunt Dorothy . 




 CHAPTER VIII . JANET ILCHESTER 

 I be a prisoner , capture by fraud , and with five shilling and a penny still remain to I for an assurance of my power to enjoy freedom . Osric and Kiomi do not show themselves on the road , they answer none of my shout . 
 ' she be afraid to look I in the face , ' I say , keep my anger on Kiomi . 
 ' Harry , Harry , ' say my aunt , ' they must have see I here ; do you grieve , and you have I , dear ? ' 
 her eager brown eye devour I while I stand pant to be happy , if only I might fling my money at Kiomi 's foot , and tell she , ' there , take all I have ; I hate you ! ' one minute I be curiously peruse the soft shade of a moustache on my aunt 's upper lip ; the next , we jump into the carriage , and she be my dear aunt Dorothy again , and the world begin roll another way . 
 the gipsy have make an appointment to deliver I over to my aunt ; Farmer Eckerthy have speak of I to my grandfather ; the tramp have fetch Mr. Rippenger on the scene . Rippenger pay the tramp , I dare say ; my grandfather pay Rippenger 's bill and for Saddlebank 's goose ; my aunt pay the gipsy , and I think it doubtful that they hand the tramp a share , so he come to the end of his list of benefit from not ask question . 
 I return to Riversley more of a man than most boy of my age , and more of a child . a small child would not have sulk as I do at Kiomi 's behaviour ; but I meet my grandfather 's ridiculous politeness with a man 's indifference . 
 ' so you be back , sir , be you ! ' 
 ' I be , sir . ' 
 ' ran like a hare , ' stead of a fox , eh ? ' 
 ' I do not   run like either , sir . ' 
 ' do you ride ? ' 
 ' yes , sir ; a horse . ' 
 that be his greeting and how I take it . I have not run away from he , so I have a quiet conscience . 
 he say , shortly after , ' look here ; your name be Harry Richmond in my house do you understand ? my servant have order to call you Master Harry Richmond , accord to your christening . you be bear here , sir , you will please to recollect . I will have no vagabond name here ' he puff himself hot , muttering , ' nor vagabond air neither . ' 
 I know very well what it mean . a sore spirit on my father 's behalf keep I alive to any insult of he ; and feel that we be immeasurably superior to the Beltham blood , I merely say , apart to old Sewis , shrug my shoulder , ' the squire expect I to recollect where I be bear . I be not likely to forget his nonsense . ' 
 Sewis , in reply , counsel I to direct a great deal of my attention to the stable , and drink claret with the squire in the evening , thing so little difficult to do that I moralize reflectively , ' here be a way of gain a relative 's affection ! ' the squire 's punctilious regard for payment impress I , it be true . he have save I from the disgrace of owe money to my detest schoolmaster ; and , besides , I be under his roof , eat of his bread . my late adventurous life teach I that I incur an obligation by it . Kiomi be the sole victim of my anger that really seem to lie down to be trample on , as she deserve for her unpardonable treachery . 
 by degree my grandfather get use to I , and commence say in approval of certain of my performance , ' there be Beltham in that Beltham in that ! ' once out hunting , I take a nasty hedge and ditch in front of he ; he bawl proudly , ' Beltham all over ! ' and praise I . at night , drink claret , he say on a sudden , ' and , egad , Harry , you must jump your head across hedge and ditch , my little fellow . it will not   do , in these confound day , to have you clever all at the wrong end . in my time , good in the saddle be good for everything ; but now you must get your brain where you can pick here , pick there and sell them like a huckster ; some do . nature 's go it be damned artifice rule , I tell ye ; and a squire of our country must be three part lawyer to keep his own . you must learn ; by God , sir , you must cogitate ; you must stew at book and map , or you will have some infernal upstart take the lead of you , and leave you nothing but the whiff of his tail . ' he conclude , ' I be glad to see you toss down your claret , my boy . ' 
 thus I grow in his favour , till I hear from he that I be to be the heir of Riversley and his estate , but on one condition , which he do not then mention . if I might have speak to he of my father , I should have love he . as it be , I like old Sewis well , for he would talk to I of the night when my father carry I away , and though he never utter the flattering word I long to hear , he repeat the story often , and make the red hall glow with beam of my father 's image . my walk and ride be divide between the road he must have follow toward London , bear I in his arm , and the vacant place of Kiomi 's camp . Kiomi stand for freedom , point into the darkness I wish to penetrate that I might find he . if I speak of he to my aunt she tremble . she say , ' yes , Harry , tell I all you be think about , whatever you want to know ' ; but her excessive trembling check I , and I keep my feeling to myself a boy with a puzzle in his head and hunger in his heart . at time I ride out to the utmost limit of the hour give I the proper number of minute to race back and dress for dinner at the squire 's table , and a great wrestling I have with myself to turn my little horse 's head from hill and valley lie East ; they seem to have the secret of my father . blank enough they look if ever I despair of their know more than i. my Winter and Summer be the mood of my mind constantly shift . I would have a week of the belief that he be near Riversley , call for I ; a week of the fear that he be dead ; long dream of he , as travel through foreign country , pat the forehead of boy and girl on his way ; or drive radiantly , and people bow . radiantly , I say : have there be touch of colour in these vision , I should have be lure off in pursuit of he . the dream pass colourlessly ; I put colour touch to the figure see in they afterward , when I be cool , and could say , ' what be the use of fancy thing ? ' yet know that fancy thing be a consolation . by such mean I come to paint the mystery surround my father in tender colour . I build up a fretted cathedral from what I imagine of he , and could pass entirely away out of the world by enter the door . 
 Want of boy ' society as well as hard head - work produce this mischief . my lesson be intermittent Resident tutor arrive to instruct I , one after another . they be clergyman , and they soon propose to marry my aunt Dorothy , or they rebuke the squire for swearing . the devil be in the parson , he say : in his time they be modest creature and stick to the bottle and heaven . my aunt be of the opinion of our neighbour , who send their boy to school and think I should be send likewise . 
 ' no , no , ' say the squire ; ' my life 's short when the gout 's marching up to my middle , and I will see as much of my heir as I can . why , the lad 's my daughter 's son : he shall grow up among his tenantry . we will beat the country and start a man at last to drive his yard of learn into he without roll sheep 's eye right and leave . ' 
 unfortunately the squire 's description of man be not start . my aunt be handsome , an heiress   that be , she have money of her own come from her mother 's side of the family , and the tender woman alive , with a voice sweet than flute . there be a saying in the county that to marry a Beltham you must po'chay she . 
 a great - aunt of mine , the squire 's sister , have be carry off . she die childless . a favourite young cousin of his likewise have run away with a poor baronet , Sir Roderick Ilchester , whose son Charles be now and then our playmate , and be a scapegrace . but for I he would have be select by the squire for his heir , he say ; and he often ' confound ' I to my face on that account as he shake my hand , break out : ' I would as lief fetch you a cuff o ' the head , Harry Richmond , upon my honour ! ' and curse at his luck for have to study for his living , and be what he call a sloppy curate now that I have come to Riversley for good . 
 he inform I that I should have to marry his sister Janet ; for that they could not allow the money to go out of the family . Janet Ilchester be a quaint girl , a favourite of my aunt Dorothy , and the squire 's especial pet ; red - cheek , with a good upright figure in walking and riding , and willing to be friendly , but we always quarrel : she detest hearing of Kiomi . 
 ' do not   talk of creature you meet when you be a beggar , Harry Richmond , ' she say . 
 ' I never be a beggar , ' I reply . 
 ' then she be a beggar , ' say Janet ; and I could not deny it ; though the only difference I see between Janet and Kiomi be , that Janet continually beg favour and gift of people she know , and Kiomi of people who be stranger . 
 my allowance of pocket - money from the squire be fifty pound a year . I might have spend it all in satisfy Janet 's wish for riding - whip , knife , pencil - case , cairngorm button , and dog . a large part of the money go that way . she be always get notice of fine dog for sale . I buy a mastiff for she , a brown retriever , and a little terri . she be permit to keep the terri at home , but I have to take care of the mastiff and retriever . when Janet come to look at they she call they by their name ; of course they follow I in preference to she ; she cry with jealousy . we have a downright quarrel . Lady Ilchester invite I to spend a day at her house , Charley be home for his Midsummer holiday . Charley , Janet , and I fish the river for trout , and Janet , to flatter I   of which I be quite aware , while I dress her rod as if she be likely to catch something , talk of Heriot , and then say : 
 ' oh ! dear , we be good friend , be not   we ? Charley say we shall marry one another some day , but mama be such a proud woman she will not   much like your have such a father as you ' ve   get unless he be dead by that time and I need not   go up to he to be kiss . ' 
 I star at the girl in wonderment , but not too angrily , for I guess that she be merely repeat her brother 's candid speculation upon the future . I say : ' now mind what I tell you , janet : I forgive you this once , for you be an ignorant little girl and know no well . speak respectfully of my father or you never see I again . ' 
 here Charley sing out : ' Hulloa ! you do not   mean to say you be talk of your father . ' 
 Janet whimper that I have call she an ignorant little girl . if she have be silent I should have pardon she . the meanness of the girl in turn on I when the glaring offence be her , strike I as contemptible beyond word . Charley and I meet half way . he advise I not to talk to his sister of my father . they all know , he say , that it be no fault of mine , and for his part , have he a rascal for a father , he should pension he and cut he ; to tell the truth , no objection against I exist in his family except on the score of the sort of father I own to , and I have well make up my mind to shake he off before I grow a man ; he speak as a friend . I might frown at he and clench my fist , but he do speak as a friend . 
 Janet all the while be nibble a biscuit , glance over it at I with mouse - eye . her short frock and her greediness , contrast with the talk of my marry she , fill I with renew scorn , though my heart be sick at the mention of my father . I ask she what she know of he . she nibble her biscuit , mumbling , ' he go to Riversley , pretend he be a singing - master . I know that be true , and more . ' 
 ' oh , and a drawing - master , and a professor of legerdemain , ' add her brother . ' expunge he , old fellow ; he be no good . ' 
 ' no , I be sure he be no good , ' say Janet . 
 I take her hand , and tell she , ' you do not   know how you hurt I ; but you be a child : you do not   know anything about the world . I love my father , remember that , and what you want I to do be mean and disgraceful ; but you do not   know well . I would forfeit everything in the world for he . and when you be of age to marry , marry anybody you like you will not   marry I . and good - bye , Janet . think of learn your lesson , and not of marry . I can not   help laugh . ' so I say , but without the laughter . her brother try hard to get I to notice he . 
 Janet betake herself to the squire . her prattle of our marriage in day to come be excuseable . it be the squire 's notion . he use to remark generally that he like to see thing look safe and fast , and he have , as my aunt confide to I , arrange with Lady Ilchester , in the girl 's hearing , that we should make a match . my grandfather pledge his word to Janet that he would restore we to an amicable footing . he think it a light task . invitation be send out to a large party at Riversley , and Janet come with all my gift on her dress or in her pocket . the squire lead the company to the gate of his stable ; the gate open , and a beautiful pony , with a side - saddle on , be trot forth , amid cry of admiration . then the squire put the bridle - rein in my hand , bid I present it myself . I ask the name of the person . he point at Janet . I present the pony to Janet , and say , ' it be from the squire . ' 
 she forget , in her delight , our be at variance . 
 ' no , no , you stupid Harry , I be to thank you . he be a darling pony . I want to kiss you . ' 
 I retire promptly , but the squire have hear she . 
 ' back , sir ! ' he shout , swear by this and that . ' you slink from a kiss , and you be Beltham blood ? 
 back to she , lad . take it . up with she in your arm or down on your knee . take it manfully , somehow . see there , she be get it ready for you . ' 
 ' I have   get a letter ready for you , Harry , to say oh ! so sorry for offend you , ' Janet whisper , when I reach the pony 's head ; ' and if you would rather not be kiss before people , then by - and - by , but do shake hand . ' 
 ' pull the pony 's mane , ' say I ; ' that will do as well . observe I pull , and now you pull . ' 
 Janet mechanically follow my action . she grimace , and whimper , ' I could pull the pony 's mane right out . ' 
 ' do not   treat animal like your doll , ' say I. 
 she run to the squire , and refuse the pony . the squire 's face change from merry to black . 
 ' young man , ' he address I , ' do not   show that bad half of your in genteel society , or , by the Lord ! you will not   carry Beltham button for long . this young lady , mind you , be a lady by birth both side . ' 
 ' she think she be marriageable , ' say I ; and walk away , leave loud laughter behind I . 
 but laughter do not console I for the public aspersion of he I love . I walk off the ground , and think to myself it be quite time I should be move . wherever I stay for any length of time I be certain to hear abuse of my father . why not wander over the country with Kiomi , go to sea , mount the Andes , enlist in a prussian regiment , and hear the soldier tell tale of Frederick the Great ? I walk over Kiomi 's heath till dark , when one of our groom on horseback overtake I , say that the squire beg I to jump on the horse and ride home as quick as possible . two other lad and the coachman be out scour the country to find I , and the squire be anxious , it appear . I ride home like a wound man make to feel proud by victory , but with no one to stop the bleeding of his wound : and the more my pride rise , the more I suffer pain . there at home sit my grandfather , deject , tell I that the loss of I a second time would kill he , beg I to overlook his roughness , call I his little Harry and his heir , his brave - spirited boy ; yet I be too sure that a word of my father to he would have bring he very near another ejaculation concern Beltham button . 
 ' you be a fiery young fellow , I suspect , ' he say , when he have recover his natural temper . ' I like you for it ; pluck 's Beltham . have a will of your own . sweat out the bad blood . here , drink my health , Harry . you be three part Beltham , at least , and it will go hard if you be not all Beltham before I die . old blood always win that race , I swear . we be the old in the county . 
 damn the mixing . my father never let any of his daughter marry , if he could help it , nor'll I , bar rascal . 
 here be to you , young squire Beltham . Harry Lepel Beltham do that suit ye ? Anon , anon , as they say in the play . take my name , and drop the Richmond no , drop the subject : we will talk of it by - and - by . ' 
 so he wrestle to express his hatred of my father without offend I ; and I study he coldly , think that the sight of my father in beggar 's clothe , raise a hand for I to follow his step , would draw I forth , though Riversley should beseech I to remain clad in wealth . 




 CHAPTER IX . an evening with CAPTAIN bulste 

 a dream that my father lie like a wax figure in a bed give I thought of die . I be ill and do not know it , and imagine that my despair at the foot of the stair of ever reach my room to lie down peacefully be the sign of death . my aunt Dorothy nurse I for a week : none but she and my dog enter the room . I have only two faint wish leave in I : one that the squire should be keep out of my sight , the other that she would speak to I of my mother 's love for my father . she happen to say , musing , ' Harry , you have your mother 's heart . ' 
 I say , ' no , my father 's . ' 
 from that we open a conversation , the sweetest I have ever have away from he , though she speak shyly and tell I very little . it be enough for I in the narrow world of my dog ' face , and the red - leaved creeper at the window , the fir - tree on the distant heath , and her hand clasp mine . my father have many fault , she say , but he have be cruelly use , or deceive , and he bear a grievous burden ; and then she say , ' yes , ' and ' yes , ' and ' yes , ' in the voice one suppose of a ghost retire , to my question of his merit . I be refresh and satisfied , like the parch earth with dew when it get no rain , and I be soon well . 
 when I walk among the household again , I find that my week of seclusion have endow I with a singular gift ; I find that I could see through everybody . look at the squire , I think to myself , ' my father have fault , but he have be cruelly use , ' and immediately I forgave the old man ; his antipathy to my father seem a craze , and to account for it I lay in wait for his numerous illogical act and word , and smile visibly in contemplation of his rough unreasonable nature , and of my magnanimity . he catch the smile , and interpret it . 
 ' grin at I , Harry ; have I make a slip in my grammar , eh ? ' 
 who could feel any further sensitiveness at his fit of irritation , read he as I do ? I see through my aunt : she be always in dread of a renewal of our conversation . I could see her idea flutter like bird to escape I . and I penetrate the other who come in my way just as unerringly . Farmer Eckerthy would acknowledge , astonish , his mind be run on cricket when I tax he with it . 
 ' crop be the cart - load of my thought , Master Harry , but there be a bit o ' cricket in it , too , ne'er a doubt . ' 
 my aunt 's maid , Davis , be shock by my discernment of the fact that she be in love , and it be useless for she to pretend the contrary , for I have see she grant tender liberty to Lady Ilchester 's footman . 
 Old Sewis say gravely , ' you have   be to the witch , Master Harry ' ; and other be sure ' I have get it from the gipsy off the common . ' 
 the maid be partly incredulous , but I perceive that they disbelieve as readily as they believe . with my late tutor , the Rev. Simon Hart , I be not sufficiently familiar to offer he proof of my extraordinary power ; so I beg favour of he , and lay hot - house flower on his table in the name of my aunt , and have the gratification of see he blush . his approval of my latin exercise be verbal , and weak praise in comparison ; besides I care nothing for praise not refer to my grand natural accomplishment . ' and my father now be think of I ! ' that be easy to imagine , but the certainty of it confirm I in my conceit . 
 ' how can you tell ? how be it possible for you to know people 's thought ? ' say Janet Ilchester , whose head be as open to I as a hat . she pretend to be rather more frightened of I than she be . 
 ' and now you think you be flatter I ! ' I say . 
 she look nervous . 
 ' and now you be ask yourself what you can do well than I can ! ' 
 she say , ' go on . ' 
 I stop . 
 she charge I with be pull up short . 
 I deny it . 
 ' guess , guess ! ' say she . ' you can not . ' 
 my reply petrify she . ' you be think that you be a lady by birth on both side . ' 
 at first she refuse to admit it . ' no , it be not   that , Harry , it be not   really . I be think how clever you be . ' 
 ' yes , after , not before . ' 
 ' no , Harry , but you be clever . I wish I be half as clever . fancy read people 's idea ! I can read my pony 's , but that be different ; I know by his ear . and as for my be a lady , of course I be , and so be you I mean , a gentleman . I be think now this be really what I be think I wish your father live near , that we might all be friend . I can not   bear the squire when he talk .... and you quite as good as I , and well . do not   shake I off , Harry . ' 
 I shake she in the gentle manner , not suspect that she have read my feeling fully as well as I her thought . Janet and I fall to talk of my father incessantly , and be constantly together . the squire catch one of my smile rise , when he applaud himself lustily for the original idea of match we ; but the idea be no long distasteful to I . it appear to I that if I must some day be marry , a wife who would enjoy my narrative , and travel over the four quarter of the globe , as Janet promise to do , in search of he I love , would be the preferable person . I swear she to secresy ; she be not to tell her brother Charley the subject we converse on . 
 ' oh dear , no ! ' say she , and tell he straightway . 
 Charley , home for his winter holiday , blurt out at the squire 's table : ' so , Harry Richmond , you be the clever fellow in the world , be you ? there be Janet tell everybody your father 's the clever next to you , and she be never see he ! ' 
 ' how ? hulloa , what be that ? ' sing out the squire . 
 ' Charley be speak of my father , sir , ' I say , prepare for thunder . 
 we all rise . the squire look as though an apoplectic seizure be come on . 
 ' do not   sit at my table again , ' he say , after a terrible struggle to be articulate . 
 his hand be stretch at I . I swing round to depart . ' no , no , not you ; that fellow , ' he call , get his arm level toward Charley . 
 I try to intercede the last who should have do it . 
 ' you like to hear he , eh ? ' say the squire . 
 I be ready to say that I do , but my aunt , whose courage be up when occasion summon it , hush the scene by pass the decanter to the squire , and speak to he in a low voice . 
 ' Biter 's bit . I have   dish myself , that be clear , ' say Charley ; and he speak the truth , and such be his frankness that I forgave he . 
 he and Janet be stay at Riversley . they leave next morning , for the squire would not speak to he , nor I to Janet . 
 ' I will tell you what ; there be no doubt about one thing , ' say Charley ; ' Janet 's right some of those girl be tremendously deep : you be about the clever fellow I have   ever meet in my life . I think of work into the squire in a sort of collateral manner , you know . a cornetcy in the Dragoon Guards in a year or two . I think the squire might do that for I without much damage you ; perhaps a couple of hundred a year , just to reconcile I to a nose out of joint . for , upon my honour , the squire speak of make I his heir or word to that effect neatly conjugate before you come back ; and rather than be a curate like that Reverend Hart of your , who hand raisin and almond , and orange - flower biscuit to your aunt the way of all the Reverends who drop down on Riversley I would betray my bosom friend . I be regularly " hoist on my own petard , " as they say in the newspaper . I be a curate and no mistake . you do it with a turn of the wrist , without strike out : and I like neat boxing . I bear no malice when I be floor neatly . ' 
 five minute after he have speak it would have be impossible for I to tell he that my simplicity and not my cleverness have cause his overthrow . from this I learn that simplicity be the keen weapon and a beautiful refinement of cleverness ; and I affect it extremely . I push it so far that I could make the squire dance in his seat with suppress fury and jealousy at my way of talk of Venice , and other Continental city , which he know I must have visit in my father 's society ; and though he rag at I and pshawe the continent to the deuce , he be ready , out of sheer rivalry , to grant anything I please to covet . at every stage of my growth one or another of my passion be alert to twist I awry , and now I be get a false self about I and become liker to the creature people suppose I to be , despise they for blockhead in my heart , as boy may who preserve a last trace of the ingenuousness deny to seasoned man . 
 happily my aunt write to Mr. Rippenger for the address of little Gus Temple 's father , to invite my schoolfellow to stay a month at Riversley . Temple come , everybody like he ; as for I my delight be unbounded , and in spite of a feeling of superiority due to my penetrative capacity , and the suspicion it originate , that Temple might be act the plain well - breed schoolboy he be , I soon prefer his pattern to my own . he confess he have find I change at first . his father , it appear , be work he as hard at Latin as Mr. Hart work I , and he sit down beside I under my tutor and stumble at Tacitus after his fluent Cicero . I offer excuse for he to Mr. Hart , say he would soon prove himself the well scholar . ' there be my old Richie ! ' say Temple , fondle I on the shoulder , and my nonsensical air fall away from I at once . 
 we roam the neighbourhood talk old school - day over , visit house , hunting and dancing , declare every day we would write for Heriot to join we , instead of which we write a valentine to Julia Rippenger , and despatch a companion one compose in a very different spirit to her father . Lady Ilchester do we the favour to draw a sea - monster , an Andromeda , and a Perseus in the shape of a fly british hussar , for Julia 's valentine . it seem to we so successful that we scatter half - a - dozen over the neighbourhood , and ride round it on the morning of St. Valentine 's Day to see the effect of they , meet the postman on the road . he give I two for myself . one be transparently from Janet , a provoking counterstroke of mine to she ; but when I open the other my heart begin beat . the standard of Great Britain be paint in colour at the top ; down each side , encricle in laurel , be king and queen of England with their sceptre , and in the middle I read the initial , a. F - G. R. R. , embed in blue forget - I - hot . I could not doubt it be from my father . rid out in the open air as I receive it , I could fancy in my hot joy that it have drop out of heaven . 
 ' he be alive ; I shall have he with I ; I shall have he with I soon ! ' I cry to Temple . ' oh ! why can not   I answer he ? where be he ? what address ? let us ride to London . do not   you understand , Temple ? this letter 's from my father . he know I be here . I will find he , never mind what happen . ' 
 ' yes , but , ' say Temple , ' if he know where you be , and you do not   know where he be , there be no good in your go off adventure . if a fellow want to be hit , the good thing he can do be to stop still . ' 
 strike by the perspicacity of his view , I turn homeward . Temple have be previously warn by I to avoid speak of my father at Riversley ; but I be now in such a boiling state of happiness , believe that my father would certainly appear as he have do at Dipwell farm , brilliant and cheerful , to bear I away to new scene and his own dear society , that I toss the valentine to my aunt across the breakfast - table , laugh and tell she to guess the name of the sender . my aunt flush . 
 ' Miss Bannerbridge ? ' she say . 
 a stranger be present . the squire introduce we . 
 ' my grandson , Harry Richmond , Captain William Bulsted , frigate Polyphemus ; Captain Bulsted , Master Augustus Temple . ' 
 for the sake of conversation , Temple ask he if his ship be fully man . 
 ' all but a mate , ' say the captain . 
 I know he by reputation as the brother of Squire Gregory Bulsted of Bulsted , notorious for his attachment to my aunt , and laugh - stock of the county . 
 ' so you have   get a valentine , ' the captain address I . ' I go on shore at Rio last year on this very day of the month , just as lively as you youngster for one . saltwater keep a man 's youth in pickle . no valentine for I ! pay off my ship yesterday at Spithead , and here I be again on Valentine 's Day . ' 
 Temple and I star hard at a big man with a bronzed skin and a rubicund laugh who expect to receive valentine . 
 my aunt thrust the letter back to I secretly . ' it must be from a lady , ' say she . 
 ' why , who would have a valentine from any but a lady ? ' exclaim the captain . 
 the squire wink at I to watch his guest . Captain Bulsted feed heartily ; he be thoroughly a sailor - gentleman , between the old school and the new , and , as I perceive , as far go in love with my aunt as his brother be . presently Sewis enter carry a foaming tankard of old ale , and he and the captain exchange a word or two upon Jamaica . 
 ' now , when you have   finish that washy tea of your , take a draught of our October , brew here long before you be a lieutenant , captain , ' say the squire . 
 ' thank you , sir , ' the captain reply ; ' I know that ale ; a moment , and I will gladly . I wish to preserve my faculty ; I do not   wish to have it suppose that I speak under fermenting influence . Sewis , hold by , if you please . ' 
 my aunt make an effort to retire . 
 ' no , no , fair play ; stay , ' say the squire , try to frown , but twinkle ; my aunt try to smile , and sit as if on spring . 
 ' Miss Beltham , ' the captain bow to she , and to each one as he speak , ' Squire Beltham , Mr. Harry Richmond ; Mr. Temple ; my ship be pay off yesterday , and till a captain 's ship be pay off , he be not his own master , you be aware . if you think my behaviour call for comment , reflect , I beseech you , on the nature of a sailor 's life . a three - year ' cruise in a cabin be pretty much equivalent to the same amount of time spend in a coffin , I can assure you ; with the difference that you be hard at work think all the time like the hum . ' 
 ' ay , he think hard enough , ' the squire strike in . 
 ' pardon I , sir ; like the hum plumb - line on a leeshore , I mean to observe . this be now the third the fourth occasion on which I have practise the observance of pay my first visit to Riversley to know my fate , that I might not have it on my conscience that I have miss a day , a minute , as soon as I be a free man on english terra firma . my brother Greg and I be bring up in close association with Riversley . one of the Beauties of Riversley we lose ! one be leave , and we both try our luck with she ; honourably , in turn , each of we , nothing underhand ; above - board , on the quarter - deck , before all the company . I will say it of my brother , I can say it of myself . Greg 's chance , I need not remark , be superior to mine ; he be always in port . if he win , then I tell he " God bless you , my boy ; you have   win the fine woman , the handsome , and the good , in or out of Christendom ! " but my chance be my property , though it may be value only one farthe coin of the realm , and there be always pity for poor sinner in the female bosom . Miss Beltham , I trespass on your kind attention . if I be to remain a bachelor and you a maiden lady , why , the will of heaven be do ! if you marry another , never mind who the man , there be my stock to the fruit of the union , never mind what the sex . but , if you will have one so unworthy of you as I , my hand and heart be at your foot , ma'am , as I have lose no time in come to tell you . ' so Captain Bulsted conclude . our eye be direct on my aunt . the squire bid she to speak out , for she have his sanction to act accord to her judgement and like . 
 she say , with a gracefulness that give I a little aching of pity for the poor captain : ' I be deeply honour by you , Captain Bulsted , but it be not my intention to marry . ' 
 the captain stand up , and bow humbly , reply ' I be ever your servant , ma'am . ' 
 my aunt quit the room . 
 ' now for the tankard , Sewis , ' say the captain . 
 gradually the bottom of the great tankard turn up to the ceiling . he drink to the last drop in it . 
 the squire ask he whether he find consolation in that . 
 the captain sigh prodigiously and say : ' it be a commencement , sir . ' 
 ' egad , it be a commencement would be something like a final end to any dozen of our fellow round about here . I will tell you what : if stout stomach gain the day in love - affair , I suspect you would run a good race against the male half of our county , William . and a damned good test of a man 's metal , I say it be ! what be you go to do to - day ? ' 
 ' I be go to get drunk , sir . ' 
 ' well , you might do bad . then , stop here , William , and give my old Port the preference . no tongue in the morning , I promise you , and pleasant dream at night . ' the captain thank he cordially , but decline , say that he would rather make a beast of himself in another place . 
 the squire vainly press his hospitality by assure he of perfect secresy on our part , as regard my aunt , and offer he Sewis and one of the footman to lift he to bed . ' you be very good , squire , ' say the captain ; ' nothing but a sense of duty restrain I . I be bind to convey the information to my brother that the coast be clear for he . ' 
 ' well , then , fall light , and for'ard , ' say the squire , shake he by the hand . forty year ago a gentleman , a baronet , have fall on the back of his head and never recover . 
 ' ay , ay , launch stern foremost , if you like ! ' say the captain , nodding ; ' no , no , I do not   go into port pull by the tail , my word for it , squire ; and good day to you , sir . ' 
 ' no ill will about this bother love - business of your , William ? ' 
 ' on my soul , sir , I cherish none . ' 
 Temple and I follow he out of the house , fascinate by his manner and oddness . he invite we to jump into the chariot beside he . we be witness of the meeting between he and his brother , a little sniffle man , as like the captain as a withered nut be like a milky one . 
 ' same luck , William ? ' say Squire Gregory . 
 ' not a point of change in the wind , Greg , ' say the captain . 
 they wrench hand thereupon , like two carpet - shaker , with a report , and much in a similar attitude . 
 ' these young gentleman will testify to you solemnly , Greg , that I take no unfair advantage , ' say the captain ; ' no whispering in passage , no appointment in garden , no letter . I speak out . bravely , man ! and now , Greg , refer to the state of your cellar , our young friend here mean to float with we to - night . it be now half - past eleven A.M. your dinner - hour the same as usual , of course ? therefore at four P.M. the hour of execution . and come , Greg , you and I will visit the cellar . a dozen and half of light and half - a - dozen of the old family that will be about the number of bottle to give I my quietus , and you yours all of we ! and you , young gentleman , take your gun or your rod , and back and be dress by the four bell , or you will not find the same man in Billy Bulsted . ' 
 Temple be enrapture with he . he declare he have be think seriously for a long time of enter the Navy , and his admiration of the captain must have give he an intuition of his character , for he persuade I to send to Riversley for our evening - dress clothe , appear in which at the dinner - table , we receive the captain 's compliment , as be gentleman who know how to attire ourselves to suit an occasion . the occasion , Squire Gregory say , happen to he too often for he to distinguish it by the cut of his coat . 
 ' I observe , nevertheless , Greg , that you have a black tie round your neck instead of a red one , ' say the captain . 
 ' then it come there by accident , ' say Squire Gregory . 
 ' accident ! there be no such thing as accident . if I wander out of the house with a half dozen or so in I , and topple into the brook , be I accidentally drown ? if a squall upset my ship , be she an accidental residue of spar and timber and old iron ? if a woman refuse I , be that an accident ? there be a cause for every disaster : too much cargo , want of foresight , want of pluck . Pooh ! when I be haul prisoner into a foreign port in time of war , you may talk of accident . Mr. Harry Richmond , Mr. Temple , I have the accidental happiness of drink to your health in a tumbler of hock wine . nominative , hic , haec , hoc . ' 
 Squire Gregory carry on the declension , not without pride . the Vocative confuse he . 
 ' Claret will do for the Vocative , ' say the captain , gravely ; ' the more so as there be plenty of it at your table , Greg . Ablative hoc , hac , hoc , which sound as if the gentleman have become incapable of speech beyond the name of his wine . so we will abandon the declension of the article for a dash of champagne , which there be no decline , I hope . wonderful man , those Romans ! they fight their ship well , too . a question to you , Greg . those heathen Pagan dog have a religion that encourage they to swear . now , my experience of life pronounce it to be a human necessity to rap out an oath here and there . what do you say ? ' 
 Squire Gregory say : ' drinking , and no thinking , at dinner , William . ' the captain pledge he . 
 ' I will take the opportunity , as we be not on board ship , of drink to you , sir , now , ' Temple address the captain , whose face be resplendent ; and he bow , and drank , and say , 
 ' as we be not on board ship ? I like you ! ' 
 Temple thank he for the compliment . 
 ' no compliment , my lad . you see I in my weakness , and you have the discernment to know I for something well than I seem . you promise to respect I on my own quarter - deck . you be of the right stuff . do I speak correctly , Mr. Harry ? ' 
 ' Temple be my dear friend , ' I reply . 
 ' and he would not be so if not of the right stuff ! good ! that be a way of put much in little . by Jove ! a royal style . ' 
 ' and Harry be a royal fellow ! ' say Temple . 
 we all drink to one another . the captain 's eye scrutinize I speculatingly . 
 ' this boy might have be yours or mine , Greg , ' I hear he say in a falter rough tone . 
 they forget the presence of Temple and I , but speak as if they think they be whispering . the captain assure his brother that Squire Beltham have give he as much fair play as one who hold a balance . Squire Gregory doubt it , and sip and keep his nose at his wineglass , crabbedly repeat his doubt of it . the captain then remark , that doubt it , his conscience permit he to use stratagem , though he , the captain , not doubt it , have no such permission . 
 ' I count I run away with she every night of my life , ' say Squire Gregory . ' nothing come of it but empty bottle . ' 
 ' court she , serenade she , ' say the captain ; ' blockade the port , lie siege to the citadel . I would give a year of service for your chance , Greg . half a word from she , and you have your horse ready . ' 
 ' she be past po'chaise , ' Squire Gregory sigh . 
 ' she be to be win by a bold stroke , brother Greg . ' 
 ' oh , Lord , no ! she be past po'chaise . ' 
 ' Humph ! it be come to be half - bottle , half - beauty , with your worship , Greg , I suspect . ' 
 ' no . I tell you , William , she be get her mind on that fellow . you can not   po'chay she . ' 
 ' after he jilt she for her sister ? Wrong , Greg , wrong . you be muddle . she have a fright about matrimony a common thing at her age , I be tell . where be the man ? ' 
 ' in the Bench , of course . where would you have he ? ' 
 ' I , sir ? if I know my bad enemy to be there , I would send he six dozen of the good in my cellar . ' 
 Temple shoot a walnut at I . I pretend to be meditate carelessly , and I have the heat and roar of a conflagration round my head . 
 presently the captain say , ' be you sure the man be in the Bench ? ' 
 ' cock , ' Squire Gregory reply . 
 ' he have money from his wife . ' 
 ' and he have the wheel to make it go . ' here they whisper in earnest . 
 ' oh , the billing be as rich as the Belthams , ' say the captain , aloud . 
 ' Pretty nigh , William . ' 
 ' that be our curse , Greg . money settle on their male issue , and money in hand ; by the Lord ! we have   always have the look of a pair of highwayman lurk for purse , when it be the woman , the woman , penniless , naked , mean , destitute ; nothing but the woman we want . and there be one apiece for we . Greg , old boy , when will the old county show such another couple of Beauties ! Greg , sir , you be not half a man , or you would have carry she , with your opportunity . the fellow 's in the Bench , you say ? how be you cocksure of that , Mr. Greg ? ' 
 ' company , ' be the answer ; and the captain turn to Temple and I , apologize profusely for talk over family matter with his brother after a separation of three year . I have guess but hastily at the subject of their conversation until they mention the Billings , the family of my maternal grandmother . the name be like a tongue of fire shoot up in a cloud of smoke : I see at once that the man in the Bench must be my father , though what the Bench be exactly , and where it be , I have no idea , and as I be leave to imagination I became , as usual , childish in my notion , and brood upon thought of the Man in the Iron Mask ; thing I dare not breathe to Temple , of whose manly sense I stand in awe when under these distracting influence . 
 ' remember our feast in the combe ? ' I sing across the table to he . 
 ' never forget it ! ' say he ; and we repeat the tale of the goose at Rippenger 's school to our entertainer , make they laugh . 
 ' and next morning Richie run off with a gipsy girl , ' say Temple ; and I compose a narrative of my wandering with Kiomi , much more amusing than the real one . the captain vow he would like to have we both on board his ship , but that time be too bad for he to offer we a prospect of promotion . ' spin round the decanter , ' say he ; ' now be the hour for they to go like a humming - top , and each man lend a hand : whip hard , my lad . it be once in three year , hurrah ! and the cause be a cruel woman . Toast she ; but no name . here be to the nameless fair ! for it be not my intention to marry , say she , and , ma'am , I be a man of honour or I would catch you tight , my nut - brown maid , and clap you into a cage , fal - lal , like a squirrel ; to trot the wheel of mat - trimony . shame to the first man down ! ' 
 ' that will not   be I , ' say Temple . 
 ' be I , sir , I , ' the captain correct his grammar . 
 ' pardon I , Captain Bulsted ; the verb " to be " govern the nominative case in our climate , ' say Temple . 
 ' then I be nominative hic ... I say , sir , I be in the tropic , Mr. Tem ... Mr. Tempus . point of honour , not forget a man 's name . Rippenger , your schoolmaster ? Mr. Rippenger , you have   knock some knowledge into this young gentleman . ' Temple and I take counsel together hastily ; we cry in a breath : ' here be to Julia Rippenger , the prettiest , nice girl live ! ' and we drink to she . 
 ' Julia ! ' the captain echo we . ' I join your toast , gentleman . Mr. Richmond , Mr. Tempus - Julia ! by all that be holy , she float a sink ship ! Julia console I for the fair , cruell woman alive . a rough sailor , Julia ! at your foot . ' 
 the captain fall commendably forward . Squire Gregory have already drop . Temple and I try to meet , but do not accomplish it till next morning at breakfast . a couple of footman carry we each upstairs in turn , as if they be remove furniture . 
 out of this strange evening come my discovery of my father , and the captain 's winning of a wife . 




 CHAPTER X. AN EXPEDITION 

 I wonder audibly where the Bench be when Temple and I sit together alone at Squire Gregory 's breakfast - table next morning , very thirsty for tea . he say it be a place in London , but do not add the sort of place , only that I should soon be come to London with he ; and I remark , ' shall I ? ' and smile at he , as if in a fit of careless affection . then he talk runningly of the theatre and pantomime and London 's charm . 
 the fear I have of this Bench make I passingly conscious of Temple 's delicacy in not repeat its name , though why I fear it there be nothing to tell I . I must have dream of it just before wake , and I burn for reasonable information concern it . Temple respect my father too much to speak out the extent of his knowledge on the subject , so we drink our tea with the grandeur of London for our theme , where , Temple assure I , you never have a headache after a carouse overnight : a communication that lead I to think the country a far less favourable place of abode for gentleman . we quit the house without see our host or the captain , and greatly admire by the footman , the maid , and the groom for have drunk their master under the table , which it could not be doubt that we have do , as Temple modestly observe while we saunter off the ground under the eye of the establishment . we have do it fairly , too , with none of those Jack the Giant - Killer trick my grandfather accuse we of . 
 the squire would not , and he could not , believe our story until he hear the confession from the mouth of the captain . after that he say we be man and hero , and he tip we both , much to Janet Ilchester 's advantage , for the squire be a royal giver , and Temple 's money have already begin to take the same road as mine . 
 Temple , in fact , be fall desperately in love ; for this reason he shrink from quit Riversley . I perceive it as clearly as a thing see through a windowpane . he be always meditate upon dog , and what might be the price of this dog or that , and whether lapdog be good traveller . the fashionable value of pug fill he with a sort of despair . ' my goodness ! ' he use an exclamation more suitable to woman , ' forty or fifty pound you say one cost , Richie ? ' 
 I pretend to estimate the probable cost of one . ' yes , about that ; but I will buy you one , one day or other , Temple . ' 
 the dear little fellow colour hot ; he be too much in earnest to laugh at the absurdity of his be suppose to want a pug for himself , and walk round I , throw himself into attitude with shrug and loud breathing . ' I do not ... do not   think that I ... I care for nothing but Newfoundlands and mastiff , ' say he . he go on shrug and kick up his heel . 
 ' girl like pug , ' I remark . 
 ' I fancy they do , ' say Temple , with a snort of indifference . 
 then I suggest , ' a pocket - knife for the hunting - field be a very good thing . ' 
 ' do you think so ? ' be Temple 's rejoinder , and I see he be dreadfully afraid of my speak the person 's name for whom it would be such a very good thing . 
 ' you can get one for thirty shilling . we will get one when we be in London . they be just as useful for woman as they be for we , you know . ' 
 ' why , of course they be , if they hunt , ' say Temple . 
 ' and we must not   lose time , ' I draw he to the point I have at heart , ' for hunting will soon be over . it be February , mind ! ' 
 ' oh , lot of time ! ' Temple cry out , and on every occasion when I try to make he understand that I be burst to visit London , he keep evade I , simply because he hat say good - bye to Janet Ilchester . his dulness of apprehension in not perceive that I could not commit a breach of hospitality by beg he downright to start , strike I as extraordinary . and I be so acute . I see every single idea in his head , every shift of , his mind , and how he half know that he profit by my shunning to say flatly I desire to set out upon the discovery of the Bench . he take the benefit of my shamefacedness , for which I daily punish his . I really feel that I be justify in give my irritability an airing by curious allusion to Janet ; yet , though I make he wince , it be impossible to touch his conscience . he admit to have repeatedly speak of London 's charm , and ' oh , yes ! you and I will go back together , Richie , ' and say that satisfy he : he double our engagement with Janet that afternoon , and it be a rid party , a dancing - party , and a drawing of a pond for carp , and we over to Janet , and Janet over to we , until I grow so sick of she I be incapable of summon a spark of jealousy in order the well to torture Temple . 
 now , he be a quick - witted boy . well , I one day hear Janet address my big dog , Ajax , in the style she usually employ to inform her hearer , and especially the proprietor , that she covet a thing : ' oh , you own dear precious pet darling beauty ! if I might only feed you every day of my life I should be happy ! I curtsey to he every time I see he . if I be his master , the man should all off hat , and the woman all curtsey , to Emperor Ajax , my dog ! my own ! my great , dear irresistible love ! then she nod at I , ' I would make they , though . ' and then at Temple , ' you see if I would not . ' 
 Ajax be a source of pride to I . however , I hear Temple murmur , in a tone totally unlike himself , ' he would be a great protection to you ' ; and I say to he , ' you know , Temple , I shall be go to London to - morrow or the next day , not later : I do not   know when I shall be back . I wish you would dispose of the dog just as you like : get he a kind master or mistress , that be all . ' 
 I sacrifice my dog to bring Temple to his sens . I think it would touch he to see how much I could sacrifice just to get an excuse for beg he to start . he do not even thank I . Ajax soon wear one of Janet 's collar , like two or three other of the Riversley dog , and I have the satisfaction of hear Temple accept my grandfather 's invitation for a further fortnight . and , meanwhile , I be the one who be charge with go about look lovelorn ! I smother my feeling and my reflection on the wisdom of people . 
 at last my aunt Dorothy find the mean of set I at liberty on the road to London . we have relate to she how Captain Bulsted toast Julia Rippenger , and we have both declare in joke that we be sure the captain wish to be introduce to she . my aunt reserve her idea on the subject , but by - and - by she propose to we to ride over to Julia , and engage she to come and stay at Riversley for some day . kiss I , my aunt say , ' she be my Harry 's friend when he be an outcast . ' 
 the word revive my affection for Julia . strong in the sacred sense of gratitude , I turn on Temple , reproach he with selfish forgetfulness of her good heart and pretty face . without defend himself , as he might have do , he entreat I to postpone our journey for a day ; he and Janet have some appointment . here be give I a noble cause and matter I need not shrink from speak of . I lash Temple in my aunt 's presence with a rod of real eloquence that astonish she , and he , and myself too ; and as he have a sense of guilt not quite explicable in his mind , he consent to bear what be in reality my burden ; for Julia have distinguish I and not he with all the sign of affection , and of the two I have the more thoroughly forget she ; I believe Temple be first in toast she at Squire Gregory 's table . there be nothing like a pen - up secret of the heart for accumulate power of speech ; I mean in youth . the mental distilling process set in later , and then you have irony instead of eloquence . from brood on my father , and not dare to mention his name lest I should hear evil of it , my thought be a proud family , proud of their origin , proud of their isolation , and not to be able to divine they be for the world to confess itself basely beneath their level . but , when they do pour out , they be tremendous , as Temple find . this oratorical display of mine give I an ascendancy over he . he adore eloquence , not to say grandiloquence : he be the son of a barrister . ' let us go and see she at once , Richie , ' he say of Julia . ' I ' m ready to be off as soon as you like ; I be ready to do anything that will please you ' ; which be untrue , but it be useless to tell he so . I sigh at my sad gift of penetration , and toss the fresh example of it into the treasury of vanity . 
 ' Temple , ' say I , dissemble a little ; ' I tell you candidly : you will not   please I by do anything disagreeable to you . a dog pull by the collar be not much of a companion . I start for Julia to - morrow before daylight . if you like your bed well , stop there ; and mind you amuse Janet for I due my absence . ' 
 ' I be not go to let any one make comparison between we , ' Temple mutter . 
 he drop dozen of similar remark , and sometimes talk downright flattery , I have so deeply impressed he . 
 we breakfast by candle - light , and ride away on a frosty foggy morning , keep our groom fifty yard to the rear , a laughable sight , with both his coat - pocket bulging , a couple of Riversley turnover pasty in one , and a bottle of champagne in the other , for our lunch on the road . now and then , when near he , we gallop for the fun of see he nurse the bottle - pocket . he be generally invisible . Temple do not think it strange that we should be rid out in an unknown world with only a little ring , half a stone's - throw clear around we , and blot of copse , and queer vanish cottage , and hard grey meadow , fir - tree wonderfully magnify , and larch and birch rig like fairy ship , all start up to we as we pass , and melt instantly . one could have fancy the fir - tree black torch . and here the shoulder of a hill invite we to race up to the ridge : some way on we come to crossroad , careless of our luck in hit the right one : yonder hang a village church in the air , and church - steeple pierce ever so high ; and out of the heart of the mist leap a brook , and to hear it at one moment , and then to have the sharp freezing silence in one 's ear , be piercingly weird . it all toss the mind in my head like hay on a pitchfork . I forget the existence of everything but what I love passionately , and that have no shape , be like a wind . 
 up on a knoll of fir in the middle of a heath , glow rosy in the frost , we dismount to lunch , lean against the warm saddle , Temple and I , and Uberly , our groom , who remind I of a certain tramp of my acquaintance in his decide preference of beer to champagne ; he drank , though , and sparkle after his draught . no soon be we on horseback again ere the flank of the dear friendly brute be in any way cool than Temple shout enthusiastically , ' Richie , we shall do it yet ! I have   be funk , but now I be sure we shall do it . Janet say , " what be the use of my come over to dine at Riversley if Harry Richmond and you do not   come home before ten or eleven o'clock ? " I tell she we would do it by dinner - time : do not   you like Janet , Richie ? that be , if our horse ' hic - haec - hock do not   get strained on this hard nominative - plural - masculine of the article road . do not   you fancy yourself din with the captain , Richie ? dative huic , say old Squire Gregory . I like to see he at dinner , because he love the smell of his wine . oh ! it be nothing to boast of , but we do drink they under the table , it can not   be deny . Janet hear of it . Hulloa ! you talk of a hunting - knife . what do you say to a pair of skate ? here we be in for a frost of six week . it strike I , a pair of skate ... ' 
 this be the champagne in Temple . in I it do not bubble to speech , and I soon draw he on at a pace that render conversation impossible . uberly shout after we to spare the horse ' leg . we hear he twice out of the deepening fog . I call to Temple that he be right , we should do it . Temple hurrah rather breathlessly . at the end of an hour I pull up at an inn , where I leave the horse to be groom and feed , and walk away rapidly as if I know the town , Temple follow I with perfect confidence , and , indeed , I have no intention to deceive he . we enter a new station of a railway . 
 ' oh ! ' say Temple , ' the rest of the way by rail . ' 
 when the railway clerk ask I what place I want ticket for , London spring to my mouth promptly in a murmur , and take the ticket I reply to Temple , 
 ' the rest of the way by rail . Uberly 's sure to stop at that inn ' ; but my heart beat as the carriage slide away with we ; an affectionate commiseration for Temple touch I when I hear he count on our be back at Riversley in time to dress for dinner . 
 he laugh aloud at the idea of our plump down on Rippenger 's school , get a holiday for the boy , tip they , and then off with Julia , exactly like two Gods of the Mythology , Apollo and Mercury . 
 ' I often use to think they have the jolly life that ever be live , ' he say , and try to catch glimpse of the country , and musing , and singing , he continue to feel like one of those blissful god until wonder at the passage of time supervene . amazement , when he look at my watch , strike he dumb . ten minute later we be in yellow fog , then in brown . Temple star at both window and at I ; he jump from his seat and fall on it , mutter , ' no ; nonsense ! I say ! ' but he have accurately recognize London 's fog . I leave he unanswered to bring up all his sens , which the railway have outstrip , for the contemplation of this fact , that we two be in the city of London . 




 CHAPTER XI . the GREAT FOG and the FIRE AT midnight 

 it be London city , and the Bench be the kernel of it to I . I throb with excitement , though I sit look out of the window into the subterranean atmosphere quite still and firm . when you think long undividedly of a single object it gather light , and when you draw near it in person the strange thing to your mind be the absence of that light ; but I , approach it in this dense fog , seem to myself to be only think of it a little more warmly than usual , and instead of fade it reverse the process , and become , from light , luminous . not be able , however , to imagine the Bench a happy place , I correct the excess of brightness and give its wall a pine - torch glow ; I set they in the middle of a great square , and hang the standard of England droop over they in a sort of mournful family pride . then , because I next conceive it a foreign kind of place , different altogether from that home growth of our , the Tower of London , I top it with a multitude of dome of pumpkin or turban shape , resemble the Kremlin of Moscow , which have once leap up in the eye of Winter , glow like a million pine - torch , and fling shadow of stretch red horse on the black smoke - drift . but what be the Kremlin , that have see a city perish , to this Bench where my father languish ! there be no compare they for tragic horror . and the Kremlin have snow - field around it ; this Bench be catch out of sight , hem in by an atmosphere thick as Charon breathe ; it might as well be underground . 
 ' oh ! it be London , ' Temple go on , correct his incorrigible doubt about it . he jump on the platform ; we have to call out not to lose one another . ' I say , Richie , this be London , ' he say , link his arm in mine : ' you know by the size of the station ; and besides , there be the fog . oh ! it be London . we have   overshot it , we be positively in London . ' 
 I could spare no sympathy for his feeling , and I do not respond to his inquire look . now that we be here I certainly wish myself away , though I would not have retreat , and for awhile I be glad of the discomfort beset I ; my step be hearty as I lead on , meditate upon ask some one the direction to the Bench presently . we have to walk , and it be nothing but traverse on a slippery pavement atmospheric circle of black brown and brown red , and sometimes a large circle of pale yellow ; the colour of old bruise fruit , medlar , melon , and the smell of they ; nothing be more desolate . neither of we know where we be , nor where we be go . we struggle through an interminable succession of squalid street , from the one lamp visible to its neighbour in the darkness : you might have fancy yourself peer at the head of an old saint on a smoky canvas ; it be like the painting of light rather than light . figure rush by ; we see no face . 
 Temple speak solemnly : ' our dinner - hour at home be half - past six . ' a street - boy overhear he and chaff he . Temple get the bad of it , and it do he good , for he have the sweetest nature in the world . we decline to be attend by link - boy ; they would have hurt our sense of independence . possess of a sovereign faith that , by dint of resolution , I should ultimately penetrate to the great square enclose the Bench , I walk with the air of one who have the map of London in his eye and could thread it blindfold . Temple be thereby deceive into thinking that I must somehow have learn the direction I mean to take , and know my way , though at the slight indication of my halting and glancing round his suspicion begin to boil , and he be for ask some one the name of the ground we stand on : he murmur , ' fellow get lose in London . ' by this time he clearly understand that I have come to London on purpose : he could not but be aware of the object of my coming , and I be too proud , and he still too delicate , to allude to it .
