 ' and see my little pensioner ' poor cottage , who be out up to lovely View . mile round go the people to it . good , and I will tell you stranger : sthe Prince von Eppenwelzen have his great ancestor , and his sister Markgrafin von Rippau say , " erect a statue of he , for he be a great warrior . " he could not , or he would not , we know not . so she say , " I will , " she say , " I will do it in seven day . " she do constantly amuse he , everybody at de Court . immense excitement ! for suppose it ! a statue of a warrior on horseback , in perfect likeness , chapeau tricorne , perruque , all of bronze , and his marshal 's baton . eh bien , well , a bronze horse be come at a gallop from Berlin ; sthat we know . by fortune a most exalted sculptor in Berlin have he ready , and many horse pull he to here , to Lovely View , by post - haste ; sthat we know . but we be in extremity of puzzlement . for where be the statue to ride he ? where be I plain to you , sir ? be sthe Marshal Furst von Eppenwelzen , our great ancestor ? yet the Markgrafin say , " it be right , wait ! " she nod , she smile . our Court be all at de lake - palace odder side sthe tower , and it be bet of gem , of feather , of lace , not to be number ! the Markgrafin say sthere to - day you see he , Albrecht Wohlgemuth Furst von Eppenwelzen ! but no sculptor can have cast he in bronze not copy he and cast he in a time of seven day ! and we say sthis : have she give a secret order to a sculptor you understand I , sir , commission where , how , have he sthe likeness copy ? or do he come to our speisesaal of our lake - palace disguise ? oh ! but to see , to copy , to model , to cast in bronze , to travel betwixt Berlin and Sarkeld in a time of seven day ? no ! so - oh ! we guess , we guess , we be in exhaustion . and to - day be like an eagle we have send an arrow to shoot and know not if he will come down . for shall we see our ancestor on horseback ? it will be a not - scribable joy ! or not ? so we guess , we be worried . at near eleven o'clock a cannon fire , sthe tent be lift , and we see ; but I be impatient wid my breath for de gun to go . ' 
 I say it would be a fine sight . 
 ' for stranger , yes ; you should be of de palace to know what a fine sight ! sthe fine ! and you be for Sarkeld ? you have friend in Sarkeld ? ' 
 ' my father be in Sarkeld , mademoiselle . I be tell he be at the palace . ' 
 ' indeed ; and he be English , your fater ? ' 
 ' yes . I have not see he for year ; I have come to find he . ' 
 ' indeed ; it be for love of he , your fater , sir , you come , and not speak German ? ' 
 I signify that it be so . 
 ' she stroke her pony 's neck musing . 
 ' because , of love be not much in de family in England , it be say , ' she remark very shyly , and in recover her self - possession ask the name of my father . 
 ' his name , mademoiselle , be Mr. Richmond . ' 
 ' Mr. Richmond ? ' 
 ' Mr. Richmond Roy . ' 
 she spring in her saddle . 
 ' you be son to Mr. Richmond Roy ? oh ! it be wonderful . ' 
 ' Mademoiselle , then you have see he lately ? ' 
 ' yes , yes ! I have see he . I have hear of his beautiful child , his son ; and you it be ? ' 
 she study my countenance a moment . 
 ' tell I , be he well ? ' mademoiselle , be he quite well ? ' 
 ' oh , yes , ' she answer , and break into smile of merriment , and then seem to bite her underlip . ' he be our fun - maker . he must always be well . I owe to he some of my English . you be his son ? you be for Sarkeld ? you will see he up at our Bella Vista . quick , let we run . ' 
 she put her pony to a canter up the brown path between the fir - tree , cry that she should take our breath ; but we be tight runner , and I , though my heart beat wildly , be full of fire to reach the tower on the height ; so when she slacken her pace , find we close on her pony 's hoof , she laugh and call we brave boy . Temple 's be no more than my friend , who have make the expedition with I out of friendship , surprise she . not that she would not have expect it to be do by Germans ; far she be unable to explain her astonishment . 
 at a turning of the ascent she point her whip at the dark knot and line of the multitude mount by various path to behold the ceremony of unveil the monument . 
 I besought she to waste no time . 
 ' you must , if you please , attend my pleasure , if I guide you , ' she say , toss her chin . 
 ' I thank you , I can not   tell you how much , mademoiselle , ' say I. 
 she answer : ' you be kind to my two pet lamb , sir . ' 
 so we move forward . 




 CHAPTER XVI . the STATUE on the PROMONTORY 

 the little lady be soon bow to respectful salutation from crowd of rustic and other on a broad carriage - way circling level with the height . I could not help think how doubly foreign I be to all the world here I who be about to set eye on my lost live father , while these people be tip - toe to gaze on a statue . but as my father might also be take an interest in the statue , I get myself round to a moderate sentiment of curiosity and a partial share of the general excitement . Temple and mademoiselle do most of the conversation , which relate to glimpse of scenery , pine , oak , beech - wood , and lake - water , until we gain the plateau where the tower stand , when the giant groom trot to the front , and work a clear way for we through a mass of travel sight - seer , and she lean to I , talk quite inaudibly amid the laughter and chat . a band of wind instrument burst out . ' this be glorious ! ' I conceive Temple to cry like an open - mouthed mute . I find it inspirit . 
 the rush of pride and pleasure produce by the music be irresistible . we march past the tower , all of we , I be sure , with splendid feeling . a stone 's throw beyond it be the lofty tent ; over it droop a flag , and flag be on pole round a wide ring of rope guard by forester and gendarme , mount and afoot . the band , dress in green , with black plume to their hat , play in the middle of the ring . outside be carriage , and lady and gentleman on horseback , full of animation ; rustic , forester , town and village people , man , woman , and child , press against the rope . it be a day of ray of sunshine , now from off one edge , now from another of large slow cloud , so that at time we and the tower be in a blaze ; next the lake - palace be illuminate , or the long grey lake and the wood of pine and of bare brown twig make bay in it . 
 several hand beckon on our come in sight of the carriage . ' there he be , then ! ' I think ; and it be like swallow my heart in one solid lump . Mademoiselle have free space to trot ahead of we . we see a tall - sit lady , attire in sable , raise a finger to she , and nip her chin . away the little lady fly to a second carriage , and on again , as one may when alive with an inquiry . I observe to Temple , ' I wonder whether she say in her German , " it be my question " ; do you remember ? ' there be no weight whatever in what I say or think . 
 she ride back , exclaim , ' nowhere . he be nowhere , and nobody know . he will arrive . but he be not yet . now , ' she bend coaxingly down to I , ' can you not a few word of German ? only a small sum ! it be the Markgrafin , my good aunt , would speak wid you , and she can no English - only she be eager to behold you , and come ! you will know , for my sake , some scrap of german ja ? you will nicht wahr ? or French ? make your glom - pudding of it , will you ? ' 
 I make a shocking plum - pudding of it . Temple be no happy . 
 the margravine , a fine vigorous lady with a lively mouth and lively eye of a restless grey that rarely dwelt on you when she speak , and constantly start off on a new idea , do I the honour to examine I , much as if I have offer myself for service in her corps of grenadier , and might do in time , but be decreed to be temporarily want in manly proportion . 
 she smile a form of excuse of my bungling half - english horrid French , talk over I and at I , forget I , and recollect I , all within a minute , and fish poor Temple for intelligible reply to incomprehensible language in the same manner , then throw her head back to gather the pair of we in her sight , then eye I alone . 
 ' C'est peut - etre le fils de son petit papa , et c'est tout dire . ' 
 such be her summary comment . 
 but not satisfied with that , she lean out of the carriage , and , make an extraordinary grimace appear the mother in labour of the difficult word , say , ' doos yo ' laff ? ' 
 there be no help it : I laugh like a madman , give one outburst and a dead stop . 
 far from look displeased , she nod . I be again put to the dreadful test . 
 ' can yo ' mak ' laff ? ' 
 it spur my wit . I have no speech to ' mak ' laff ' with . at the very instant of my dilemma I chance to see a soberly - clothe old townsman hustle between two helpless woman of the crowd , his pipe in his mouth , and his hat , wig , and handkerchief slide over his face , show his bald crown , and he not dare to cry out , for fear his pipe should be tread under foot . 
 ' he can , your Highness . ' 
 her quick eye catch the absurd scene . she turn to one of her lady and touch her forehead . her hand be reach out to I ; Temple she pat on the shoulder . 
 ' he can ja : du auch . ' 
 a grand gentleman ride up . they whisper , gaze at the tent , and appear to speak vehemently . all the man 's face be foreign : none of they have the slight resemblance to my father 's . I fancy I might detect he disguise . I star vainly . Temple , to judge by the expression of his feature , be think . yes , think I , we might as well be at home at old Riversley , that distant spot ! we be as out of place here as frog in the desert ! 
 rid to and fro , and chattering , and commotion , of which the margravine be the centre , go on , and the band play beautiful waltz . the workman in and out of the tent be full of their business , like seaman under a storm . 
 ' Fraulein Sibley , ' the margravine call . 
 I hop it might be an english name . so it prove to be ; and the delight of hear English speak , and , what be more , have english ear to speak to , be blissful as the leap to daylight out of a nightmare . 
 ' I have the honour to be your countrywoman , ' say a lady , English all over to our struggle sens . 
 we become immediately attach to she as a pair of shipwreck boat lack provender of every sort be take in tow by a well - store vessel . she know my father , know he intimately . I relate all I have to tell , and we learn that we have make acquaintance with her pupil , the Princess Ottilia Wilhelmina Frederika Hedwig , only child of the Prince of Eppenwelzen . 
 ' your father will certainly be here ; he be generally the margravine 's right hand , and it be wonderful the margravine can do without he so long , ' say Miss Sibley , and converse with the margravine ; after which she inform I that she have be graciously direct to assure I my father would be on the field when the cannon sound . 
 ' perhaps you know nothing of Court life ? ' she resume . ' we have very curious performance in Sarkeld , and we owe it to the margravine that we be frequently enliven . you see the tall gentleman who be rid away from she . I mean the one with the black hussar jacket and thick brown moustache . that be the prince . do you not think he handsome ? he be very kind rather capricious ; but that be a way with prince . indeed , I have no reason to complain . he have lose his wife , the Princess Frederika , and depend upon his sister the margravine for amusement . he have have it since she discover your papa . ' 
 ' be the gun never go off ? ' I groan . 
 ' if they would only conduct their ceremony without their gun ! ' exclaim Miss Sibley . ' the origin of the present ceremony be this : the margravine wish to have a statue erect to an ancestor , a renowned soldier and I would infinitely prefer talk of England . but never mind . oh , you will not   understand what you gaze at . well , the prince do not care to expend the money . instead of urge that as the ground of his refusal , he declare there be no sculptor to do justice to Prince Albrecht Wohlgemuth , and one could not rely on their effecting a likeness . we have he in the dining - hall ; he be strikingly handsome . afterward he pretend I be speak now of the exist Prince Ernest that it would be age before the statue be complete . one day the margravine induce he to agree to pay the sum stipulate for by the sculptor , on condition of the statue be complete for public inspection within eight day of the hour of their agreement . the whole Court be witness to it . they arrange for the statue , horse and man , to be exhibit for a quarter of an hour . of course , the margravine do not signify it would be a perfectly finish work . we be keep at a great distance , that we may not scrutinize it too closely . they unveil it to show she have be as good as her word , and then cover it up to fix the rider to the horse , a screw be employ , I imagine . for one thing we know about it , we know that the horse and the horseman travel hither separately . in all probability , the margravine give the order for the statue last autumn in Berlin . now look at the prince . he have his eye on you . look down . now he have forget you . he be impatient to behold the statue . our chief fear be that the statue will not maintain its balance . fortunately , we have plenty of guard to keep the people from push against it . if all turn out well , I shall really say the margravine have do wonder . she do not look anxious ; but then she be not one ever to show it . the prince do . every other minute he be glance at the tent and at his watch . can you guess my idea ? your father 's absence lead I to think - oh ! only a pass glimmer of an idea the statue have not arrive , and he be bring it on . otherwise , he would be sure to be here . the margravine beckon I . ' 
 ' do not   go ! ' we cry simultaneously . 
 the Princess Ottilia supply her place . 
 ' I have send to our stable for two little pretty hungarian horse for you two to ride , ' she say . ' no , I have not yet see he . he be ask for , and de Markgrafin know not at all . he bade in our lake ; he have be see since . the man be exciteable ; but he be so sensible . oh , no . and he be full of laughter . we shall soon see he . would he not ever be cautious of himself for a son like you ? ' 
 her compliment raise a blush on I . 
 the patience of the people be creditable to their phlegm . the smoke of pipe curl over the numberless head be the most stirring thing about they . 
 Temple observe to I , 
 ' we will give the old statue a british cheer , will not   we , Richie ? ' 
 ' after come all the way from England ! ' say I , in dejection . 
 ' no , no , Richie ; you be sure of he now . he be somewhere direct affair , I suspect . I say , do let we show they we can ring out the right tune upon occasion . by jingo ! there go a fellow with a match . ' 
 we see the cannoni march up to the margravine 's carriage for order . she summon the prince to her side . lady in a dozen carriage be stand up , handkerchief in hand , and the gentleman get their horse ' head on a line . Temple count nearly sixty person of quality station there . the workman be troop out of the tent . 
 Miss Sibley run to we , say , 
 ' the gun - horror have be command . now then : the prince can scarcely contain himself . the gunner be ready near his gun ; he have his frightful match lift . see , the manager - superintendent be receive the margravine 's last injunction . how firm woman 's nerve be ! now the margravine insist on the prince 's read the exact time by her watch . everybody be do it . let we see . by my watch it be all but fifteen minute to eleven , A.M. Dearest , ' she address the little princess ; ' would you not like to hold my hand until the gun be fire ? ' 
 ' dearest , ' reply the princess , whether in childish earnest or irony I could not divine , ' if I would hold a hand it would be a gentleman 's . ' 
 all eye be on the Prince of Eppenwelzen , as he gaze toward the covered statue . with impose deliberation his hand rise to his hat . we see the hat raise . the cannon be fire and roar ; the band strike up a pompous slow march : and the tent - veil break apart and roll off . it be like the dawn fly and sunrise mount . 
 I confess I forget all thought of my father for awhile ; the shout of the people , the braying of the brass instrument , the lady cheer sweetly , the gentleman give short , hearty expression of applause , intoxicate I . and the statue be superb - horse and rider in new bronze polish by sunlight . 
 ' it be life - like ! it be really noble ! it be a true Prince ! ' exclaim Miss Sibley . she translate several exclamation of the lady and gentleman in German : they be entirely to the same effect . the horse give we a gleam of his neck as he paw a forefoot , just rein in . we know he ; he be a gallant horse ; but it be the figure of the Prince Albrecht that be so fine . I have always laugh at sculpture figure on horseback . this one overawe I . the Marshal be acknowledge the salute of his army after a famous victory over the infidel Turks . he sit upright , almost imperceptibly but effectively bend his head in harmony with the curve of his horse 's neck , and his baton sweep the air low in proud submission to the honours cast on he by his acclaim soldiery . his three - cornered lace hat , curl wig , heavy - trim surcoat , and high boot , remind I of Prince Eugene . no Prince Eugene nay , nor Marlborough , have such a martial figure , such an animate high old warrior 's visage . the bronze feature reek of battle . 
 Temple and I feel humiliate   without cause , I grant   at the success of a work of art that strike we as a new military triumph of these Germans , and it be impossible not to admire it . the little Princess Ottilia clap hand by fit . what word she address to I I know not . I deal out my stock of german ' Ja , ja to her English . we be draw by she to congratulate the margravine , whose hand be then be kiss by the prince : he do it most courteously and affectionately . other gentleman , count and baron , bow over her hand . lady , accord to their rank and privilege , salute she on the cheek or in some graceful fashion . when our turn arrive , Miss Sibley translate for we , and as we be at concert pitch we do not acquit ourselves badly . Temple 's remark be , that he wish she and all her family have be English . nothing be leave for I to say but that the margravine almost make we wish we have be german . 
 smile cordially , the margravine speak , Miss Sibley translate : 
 ' her Royal Highness ask you if you have see your father ? ' 
 I shake my head . 
 the Princess Ottilia translate , ' her Highness , my good aunt , would know , would you know he , do you see he ? ' 
 ' yes , anywhere , ' I cry . 
 the margravine push I back with a gesture . 
 ' yes , your Highness , on my honour ; anywhere on earth ! ' 
 she decline to hear the translation . 
 her insult disbelief in my ability to recognize the father I have come so far to embrace would have vex I but for the wretched thought that I be lose he again . we thread the carriage ; gaze at the horseman in a way to pierce the hair on their face . the little princess come on we hurriedly . 
 ' here , see , be the horse . I will you to mount . be they not pretty animal ? ' she whisper , ' I believe your fater have be hurt in his mind by something . it be only perhaps . now mount , for de Markgrafin say you be our good guest . ' 
 we mount simply to show that we could mount , for we would rather have be on foot , and draw up close to the right of the margravine 's carriage . 
 ' hush ! a poet be read his ode , ' say the princess . ' it be Count Fretzel von Wolfenstein . ' 
 this ode be dreadful to we , and all the Court people pretend they like it . when he wave his right hand toward the statue there be a shout from the rustic set ; when he bow to the margravine , the lady and gentleman murmur agreeably and smile . we be convince of its be downright hypocrisy , rustic stupidity , Court flattery . we would have argue our case , too . I propose a gallop ; Temple say , 
 ' no , we will give the old statue our cheer as soon as this awful fellow have do . I do not   care much for poetry , but do not   let I ever have to stand and hear german poetry again for the remainder of my life . ' 
 we could not imagine why they should have poetry read out to they instead of their fine band playing , but suppose it be for the satisfaction of the margravine , with whom I grow particularly annoyed on hear Miss Sibley say she conceive her Highness to mean that my father be actually on the ground , and that we neither of we , father and son , know one another . I swear on my honour , on my life , he be not present ; and the melancholy in my heart take the form of extreme irritation , I speak passionately . I rise in my stirrup , ready to shout , ' Father ! here be Harry Richmond come to see you . where be you ! ' I do utter something a syllable or two : ' make haste ! ' I think the word be . they spring from my inmost bosom , address without forethought to that drawling mouth poet . the margravine 's face meet mine like a challenge . she have her lip tight in a mere lip - smile , and her eye gleam with provocation . 
 ' her Highness , ' Miss Sibley translate , ' ask whether you be prepared to bet that your father be not on the ground ? ' 
 ' beg she to wait two minute , and I will be prepare to bet any sum , ' say I. 
 Temple take one half the circle , I the other , rid through the attentive horseman and carriage - line , and make sure the face we seek be absent , more or less discompose everybody . the poet finish his ode ; he be cheer , of course . mightily relieve , I behold the band resume their instrument , for the cheering resemble a senseless beating on brass shield . I feel that we English could do it well . Temple from across the sector of the circle , run about two foot in front of the statue , call aloud , 
 ' Richie ! he be not here ! ' 
 ' not here ! ' cry I. 
 the people gaze up at we , wonder at the tongue we talk . 
 ' Richie ! now let us lead these fellow off with a tiptop cheer ! ' 
 Little Temple crow lustily . 
 the head of the statue turn from Temple to I . 
 I find the people fall back with amazed exclamation . I so prepossessed be I simply star at the sudden - flash white of the statue 's eye . the eye , from be an instant ago dull carve ball , be animate . they be fix on I . I be unable to give out a breath . its chest heave ; both bronze hand strike against the bosom . 
 ' Richmond ! my son ! Richie ! Harry Richmond ! Richmond Roy ! ' 
 that be what the statue give forth . 
 my head be like a ring pan . I know it be my father , but my father with death and strangeness , earth , metal , about he ; and his voice be like a human cry contend with earth and metal - mine be stifle . I see he descend . I dismount . we meet at the rope and embrace . all his figure be stiff , smooth , cold . my arm slide on he . each time he speak I think it an unnatural thing : I myself have not speak once . 
 after glance by hazard at the empty saddle of the bronze horse , I call to mind more clearly the appalling circumstance which have stupefy the whole crowd . they have hear a statue speak have see a figure of bronze walk . for they it be the ancestor of their prince ; it be the famous dead old warrior of a hundred and seventy year ago set thus in motion . imagine the behaviour of people round a slain tiger that do not compel they to fly , and may yet stretch out a dreadful paw ! much so they press for a near sight of its walnut visage , and shrink in the act . perhaps I share some of their sensation . I can not tell : my sensation be trance . there be no warmth to revive I in the gauntlet I clasp . I look up at the sky , think that it have fall dark . 




 CHAPTER XVII . MY FATHER BREATHES , MOVES , and speak 

 the people break away from we like furrowed water as we advance on each side of the rope toward the margravine 's carriage . 
 I become a perfectly mechanical creature : incapable , of observe , just capable of take an impression here and there ; and in such case the impression that come be stamp on hot wax ; they keep the scene fresh ; they partly pervert it as well . Temple 's version be , I be sure , the truer historical picture . he , however , could never repeat it twice exactly alike , whereas I fail not to render image for image in clear succession as they have strike I at the time . I could perceive that the figure of the Prince Albrecht , in its stiff condition , be debar from vault , or stride , or stoop , so that the rope be a barrier between we . I see the little Princess Ottilia eye we with an absorb comprehensive air quite unlike the manner of a child . dot of head , curious face , peer and start eye , meet my vision . I hear sharp talk in German , and a rider fling his arm , as if he wish to crash the universe , and fly off . the margravine seem to I more an implacable parrot than a noble lady . I think to myself : this be my father , and I be not overjoyed or grateful . in the same way , I feel that the daylight be bronze , and I do not wonder at it : nay , I reason on the probability of a composition of sun and mould produce that colour . the truth be , the power of my heart and will be frozen ; I think and feel at random . and I crave excuse for dwell on such trifling phenomena of the sensation , which have be useful to I by help I to realize the scene , even as at the time they obscure it . 
 accord to Temple 's description , when the statue move its head toward he , a shudder go through the crowd , and a number of forefinger be level at it , and the head move toward I , mark of they all . its voice be answer by a dull pule scream from woman , and the man gap . when it descend from the saddle , the act be not perform with one bind , as I fancy , but difficultly ; and it walk up to I like a figure drag log at its heel . half - a - dozen workman run to arrest it ; some townswoman faint . there be a heavy altercation in German between the statue and the superintendent of the arrangement . the sun shine brilliantly on our march to the line of carriage where the Prince of Eppenwelzen be talk to the margravine in a fury , and he dash away on his horse , after bellow certain direction to his forester and the workman , by whom we be surround ; while the margravine talk loudly and amiably , as though everything have go well . her watch be out . she acknowledge my father 's bow , and overlook he . she seem to have make her courtier smile . the lady and gentleman obey the wave of her hand by quit the ground ; the band head a long line of the commoner sort , and a body of forester gather the remnant and join they to the rear of the procession . a liveried groom lead away Temple 's horse and mine . Temple declare he could not sit after see the statue descend from its pedestal . 
 her Highness 's behaviour roughen as soon as the place be clear of company . she speak at my father impetuously , with manifest scorn and reproach , strike her silver - mount stick on the carriage panel , again and again stamp her foot , lift a most variable emphatic countenance . Princess Ottilia try to intercede . the margravine clench her hand , and , to one not understand her speech , appear literally to blow the little lady off with the breath of her mouth . her whole bearing consist of volley of abuse , close by magisterial interrogation . Temple compare her Highness 's language to the running out of Captain Welsh 's chaincable , and my father 's reply to the hauling in : his sentence be short , they sound like manful protestation ; I barely notice they . Temple 's version of it go : ' and there be your father apologize , and the margravine rat he , ' etc . my father , as it happen , be careful not to open his lip wide on account of the plaster , or thick coating of paint on his face . no one would have suppose that he be burn with indignation ; the fact be , that to give vent to it , he would have have to exercise his muscular strength ; he be plaster and paint from head to foot . the fixture of his wig and hat , too , constrain his skin , so that his look be no index of his feeling . I long gloomily for the moment to come when he would present himself to I in his natural form . he be not sensible of the touch of my hand , nor I of his . there we have to stand until the voluble portion of the margravine 's anger come to an end . she shut her eye and bow curtly to our salute . 
 ' you have see the last of I , madam , ' my father say to her whirl carriage - wheel . 
 he try to shake , and strain in his ponderous garment . Temple gaze abash . I know not how to act . my father keep lift his knee on the spot as if practise a walk . 
 the tent be in its old place cover the bronze horse . a workman step ahead of we , and we all go at a strange leisurely pace down the hill through tall pinetree to where a closed vehicle await we . here be also a couple of lackey , who deposit my father on a bed of moss , and with much effort pull his huge boot off , leave he in red silk stocking . Temple and I snatch his gauntlet ; Temple fall backward , but we have no thought of laughter ; people be see approach , and the three of we jump into the carriage . I have my father 's live hand in mine to squeeze ; feel he scarcely yet the live man I have seek , and with no great warmth of feeling . his hand be very moist . often I say , ' dear father ! Papa , I be so glad at last , ' in answer to his short - breathe ' Richie , my little lad , my son Richmond ! you find I out ; you find I ! ' we be conscious that his thick case of varnish clothing be against we . one would have fancy from his way of speak that he suffer from asthma . I be now gift with a tenfold power of observation , and let nothing escape I . 
 Temple , sit opposite , grin cheerfully at time to encourage our spirit ; he have not recover from his wonderment , nor have I introduce he . my father , however , have catch his name . Temple   who might as well have talk , I think   be perpetually steal secret glance of abstract perusal at he with a pair of round infant 's eye , suck his reflection the while . my father break our silence . 
 ' Mr. Temple , I have the honour , ' he say , as if about to cough ; ' the honour of make your acquaintance ; I fear you must surrender the hope of make mine at present . ' 
 Temple start and redden like a little fellow detect in stray from his spelling - book , which be the window - frame . in a minute or so the fascination prove too strong for he ; his eye wander from the window and he renew his shy inspection bit by bit as if cast up a column of figure . 
 ' yes , Mr. Temple , we be in high Germany , ' say my father . 
 it must have cost Temple cruel pain , for he be a thoroughly gentlemanly boy , and he could not resist it . finally he surprise himself in his stealthy reckoning : arrive at the full - breech or buttoned waistband , about half - way up his ascent from the red silk stocking , he would pause and blink rapidly , sometimes jump and cough . 
 to put he at his ease , my father exclaim , ' as to this exterior , ' he knock his knuckle on the heave hard surface , ' I can only affirm that it be , on horseback ahem ! particularly as the horse betray no restivity , pronounce perfect ! the sole complaint of our interior concern the resemblance we bear to a lobster . human somewhere , I do believe myself to be . I shall have to be relieve of my shell before I can at all satisfactorily proclaim the fact . I be a human being , believe I . ' 
 he beg permission to take breath a minute . 
 ' I know you for my son 's friend , Mr. Temple : here be my son , my boy , Harry Lepel Richmond Roy . have patience : I shall presently stand unshelled . I have much to relate ; you likewise have your narrative in store . that you should have light on I at the critical instant be one of those miracle which combine to produce overwhelm testimony ay , Richie ! without a doubt there be a hand direct our destiny .  CHAPTER XIX . our return HOMEWARD 

 we start for England at noon , much against my secret wish ; but my father would not afford the margravine time to repent of her violent language and injustice toward he . reflection increase his indignation . anything that go wrong on the first stage of the journey cause he to recapitulate her epithet and reply to they proudly . he confide to I in Cologne Cathedral that the entire course of his life be a grand plot , resemble an unfinished piece of architecture , which might , at a future day , prove the wonder of the world : and he have , therefore , pack two dozen of hoar old   uralt : he use comical German   Hock for a present to my grandfather Beltham , in the hope of its be find acceptable . 
 ' for , Richie , ' say he , ' you may not know and it be not to win your thank I inform you of it that I labour unremittingly in my son 's interest . I have establish he , on his majority , in Germany , at a Court . my object now be to establish he in England . promise I that it shall be the decide endeavour of your energy and talent to rise to the height I point out to you ? you promise , I perceive , ' he add , sharp in detect the unpleasant predicament of a boy who be ask to speak priggishly . so then I could easily promise with a firm voice . he drop certain explosive hint , which remind I of the funny idea of my state and greatness I have when a child . I shrug at they ; I care nothing for revelation to come by - and - by . my object be to unite my father and grandfather on term of friendship . 
 this be the view that now absorb and fix my mind . to have he a frequent visitor at Riversley , if not a resident in the house , enliven they all , while I , perhaps , trifle a cavalry sabre , become one of my settle dream . the difficult part of the scheme appear to I the obtaining of my father 's consent . I mention it , and he say immediately that he must have his freedom . ' now , for instance , ' say he , ' what be my desire at this moment ? I have always a big one perch on a rock in the distance ; but I speak of my present desire . and let it be suppose that the squire be one of we : we be return to England . well , I want to show you a stork 's nest . we be not far enough South for the stork to build here . it be a fact , Richie , that I do want to show you the bird for luck , and as a feature of the country . and in I , a desire to do a thing partake of the impetus of steam . 
 well , you see we be jog home to England . I resist myself for duty 's sake : that I can do . but if the squire be here with his yea and his nay , by heaven ! I should be off to the top of the Rhine like a tornado . I submit to circumstance : I can not , and I will not , be dictate to by man . ' 
 ' that seem to I rather unreasonable , ' I remonstrate . 
 ' it be ; I be ashamed of it , ' he answer . ' do as you will , Richie ; set I down at Riversley , but under no slight , mark you . I keep my honour intact , like a bottled cordial ; my unfailing comfort in adversity ! I hand it to you , my son , on my death - bed , and say , " you have there the essence of my life . never have it be know of I that I swallow an insult . " ' 
 ' then , papa , I shall have a talk with the squire . ' 
 ' make good your ground in the castle , ' say he . ' I string a guitar outside . you toss I a key from the wall . if there be room , and I have leisure , I enter . if not , you know I be pave your way in other quarter . Riversley , my boy , be an excellent foothold and fortress : Riversley be not the world . at Riversley I should have to wear a double face , and , egad ! a double stomach - bag , like young Jack feed with the giant one full of ambition , the other of provender . that place be our touchstone to discover whether we have prudence . we have , I hope . and we will have , Mr. Temple , a pleasant day or two in Paris . ' 
 it be his habit to turn off the bent of these conversation by draw Temple into they . Temple declare there be no feeling we be in a foreign country while he be our companion . we simply enjoy strange scene , look idly out of our window . our recollection of the strange scene ever witness fill we with I know not what scornful pleasure , and laugh in the background at any sight or marvel pretend to amuse we . Temple and I canter over the great belgian battlefield , talk of Bella Vista tower , the statue , the margravine , our sour milk and black - bread breakfast , the little Princess Ottilia , with she ' it be my question , ' and ' you be kind to my lamb , sir , ' thoughtless of glory and dead bone . my father be very differently impressed . he be in an exultant glow , far outmatch the bloom on our face when we rejoin he . I cry , 
 ' Papa , if the prince will not   pay for a real statue , I will , and I will present it in your name ! ' 
 ' to the nation ? ' cry he , star , and arrest his arm in what seem an orchestral movement . 
 ' to the margravine ! ' 
 he hear , but have to gather his memory . he have be fight the battle , and make light of Bella Vista . I study he eagerly , I know , and yet quite unconsciously , and I come to no conclusion . boy be always put down the cipher of their observation of people belove by they , but do not add up a sum total . 
 our journey home occupy nearly eleven week , owe to stress of money on two occasion . in Brussels I behold he with a little beggar - girl in his arm . 
 ' she have ask I for a copper coin , Richie , ' he say , squeeze her fat cheek to make cherry of her lip . 
 I recommend he to give she a silver one . 
 ' something , Richie , I must give the little wench , for I have kiss she , and , in my list of equivalent , gold would be the sole form of repayment after that . you must buy I off with honour , my boy . ' 
 I be compel to receive a dab from the child 's nose , by way of a kiss , in return for buy he off with honour . 
 the child stump away on the pavement front our hotel , star at its fist that hold the treasure . 
 ' poor pet wee drab of it ! ' exclaim my father . ' one be glad , Richie , to fill a creature out of one 's emptiness . now she toddle ; she be digest it rapidly . the last performance of one 's purse be rarely so pleasant as that . I owe it to she that I make the discovery in time . ' 
 in this manner I also make the discovery that my father have no further supply of money , none whatever . how it have run out without his remark it , he could not tell ; he could only assure I that he have become aware of the fact while search vainly for a coin to bestow on the beggar - girl . I despatch a letter attest by a notary of the city , apply for money to the banker to whom Colonel Goodwin have introduce I on my arrival on the continent . the money come , and in the meantime we have form acquaintance and entertain they ; they be chiefly half - pay english military officer , dash man . one , a major Dykes , my father establish in our hotel , and we carry he on to Paris , where , consequent upon our hospitality , the purse be again deficient . 
 two reason for not regret it be adduce by my father ; firstly , that it teach I not to despise the importance of possess money ; secondly , that we have serve our country by assist dyke , who be on the scent of a new and terrible weapon of destruction , which he believe to be in the hand of the french Government . major Dykes disappear on the scent , but we have the satisfaction of know that we have do our good toward save the Navy of Great Britain from be blow out of water . Temple and I laugh over major dyke , and he become our puppet for by - play , on account of his enormous whisker , his passion for strong drink , and his air of secresy . my father 's faith in his patriotic devotedness be sufficient to withhold I from suspicion of his character . whenever my instinct , or common sense , would have lead I to differ with my father in opinion fun supervene ; I be willing that everything in the world should be as he would have it be , and take up with a spirit of laughter , too happy in have win he , in have fish he out of the deep sea at one fling of the net , as he say , to care for accuracy of sentiment in any other particular . 
 our purse be at its low ebb ; he suggest no mean of replenish it , and I think of none . he have hear that it be possible to live in Paris upon next to nothing with very great luxury , so we try it ; we stroll through the lilac aisle among bonne and baby , attend military spectacle , ride on omnibus , din on the country height , go to theatre , and have a most pleasurable time , gain everywhere front place , friendly smile , kind little service , in a way that would have be incomprehensible to I but for my consciousness of the magical influence of my father 's address , a mixture of the ceremonious and the affable such as the people could not withstand . 
 ' the poet be perhaps , on the whole , more exhilarating than the alderman , ' he say . 
 these be the respective name give by he to the empty purse and the full purse . we vow we prefer the poet . 
 ' ay , ' say he , ' but for all that the alderman be light on his foot : I back he to be across the Channel first . the object of my instruction to you will be lose , Richie , if I find you despise the Alderman 's Pegasus . on money you mount . we be literally chain here , you know , there be no doubt about it ; and we be add a nail to our fetter daily . true , you be accomplish the parisian accent . Paris have also this immense advantage over all other city : ' tis the central hotel on the high - road of civilization . in Paris you meet your friend to a certainty ; it catch they every one in turn ; so now we must abroad early and late , and cut for trump . ' a meeting with a friend of my father , Mr. Monterez Williams , be the result of our resolute adoption of this system . he help we on to Boulogne , where my father meet another friend , to whom he give so sumptuous a dinner that we have not money enough to pay the hotel bill . 
 ' now observe the inconvenience of leave Paris , ' say he . ' ten to one we shall have to return . we will try a week 's whistling on the jetty ; and if no luck come , and you will admit , Richie Mr. Temple , I call your attention to it that luck will scarcely come in profuse expedition through the narrow neck of a solitary seaport , why , we must return to Paris . ' 
 I propose to write to my aunt Dorothy for money , but he would not hear of that . after two or three day of whistle , I see my old friend , Mr. Bannerbridge , step out of the packetboat . on condition of my writing to my aunt to say that I be come home , he advance I the sum we be in need of , grudgingly though , and with the prediction that we should break down again , which be verify . it occur only a stage from Riversley , where my grandfather 's name be good as coin of the realm . besides , my father remain at the inn to guarantee the payment of the bill , while Temple and I push on in a fly with the two dozen of Hock . it could hardly be call a break - down , but my father be not unwilling for I to regard it in that light . among his parting remark be an impressive adjuration to I to cultivate the squire 's attachment at all cost . 
 ' do this , ' he say , ' and I shall know that the lesson I have teach you on your journey homeward have not be throw away . my darling boy ! my curse through life have be that the sense of weight in money be a sense I be and be bear utterly a stranger to . the consequence be , my grand edifice fall ; there be no foundation for they . not that I be bad , understand I , than under a temporary cloud , and the blessing of heaven have endow I with a magnificent constitution . Heaven forefend that I should groan for myself , or you for I ! but digest what you have learn , Richie ; press nothing on the squire ; be guide by the advice of that esteemed and admirable woman , your aunt Dorothy . and , by the way , you may tell she confidentially of the progress of your friendship with the Princess Ottilia . here I shall employ my hour in a tranquil study of nature until I see you . ' thus he speed I forward . 
 we sight Riversley about mid - day on a sunny June morning . compare with the view from Bella Vista , our fir look scanty , our heath - tract dull , as place have no page of history write on they , our fresh green meadow not more than commonly homely . I be so full of my sense of triumph in my adventurous journey and the recovery of my father , that I gaze on the old Grange from a tower height . the squire be on the lawn , surround by a full company : the Ilchesters , the Ambroses , the Wilfords , Captain and Squire Gregory Bulsted , the Rubreys , and other , all bend to rose , to admire , smell , or pluck . charming group of lady be here and there ; and Temple whisper as we pass they : 
 ' we beat foreigner in our woman , Richie . ' 
 I , make it my business to talk with perfect unconcern , reply 
 ' do you think so ? perhaps . not in all case ' ; all the while I be exult at the sweet beam of England radiate from these dear early - morning - look woman . 
 my aunt Dorothy swim up to I , and , kiss I , murmur : 
 ' take no rebuff from your grandpapa , darling . ' 
 my answer be : ' I have find he ! ' 
 Captain Bulsted sing out our name ; I catch sight of Julia Rippenger 's face ; the squire have his back turn to I , which remind I of my first speech with Captain Jasper Welsh , and I think to myself , I know something of the world now , and the thing be to keep a good temper . here there be no wire - coil to intercept we , so I front he quickly . 
 ' Hulloa ! ' he cry , and give I his shoulder . 
 ' Temple be your guest , sir , ' say I. 
 he be oblige to stretch out his hand to Temple . 
 a prompt instinct warn I that I must show he as much Beltham as I could summon . 
 ' dog and horse all right , sir ? ' I ask . 
 Captain Bulsted saunter near . 
 ' here , William , ' say the squire , ' tell this fellow about my stable . ' 
 ' in excellent condition , Harry Richmond , ' return the captain . 
 ' oh ! he be get a new name , I will swear , ' say the squire . 
 ' not I ! ' 
 ' then what have you get of your trip , eh ? ' 
 ' a sharp eye than I have , sir . ' 
 ' you have   be sharpen it in London , have you ? ' 
 ' I have   be a little farth than London , squire . ' 
 ' well , you be not a liar . ' 
 ' there , you see the lad can stand fire ! ' Captain Bulsted break in . ' Harry Richmond , I be proud to shake your hand , but I will wait till you be through the ceremony with your grandad . ' 
 the squire 's hand be cross behind he . I smile boldly in his face . 
 ' shall I make the tour of you to get hold of one of they , sir ? ' he frown and blink . 
 ' shuffle in among the lady ; you seem to know how to make friend among they , ' he say , and pretend to disengage his right hand for the purpose of wave it toward one of the group . 
 I seize it , say heartily , ' Grandfather , upon my honour , I love you , and I be glad to be home again . ' 
 ' mind you , you be not at home till you have   beg Uberly 's pardon in public , you know what for , ' he rejoin . 
 ' leave the horse at that inn be on my conscience , ' say I. 
 the squire grumble a bit . 
 ' suppose he kick ? ' say I ; and the captain laugh , and the squire too , and I be in such high spirit I think of a dozen witty suggestion relative to the seat of the conscience , and grieve for a minute at go to the lady . 
 all the well ; keep he there Captain Bulsted convoy I to pretty irish - eyed Julia Rippenger . Temple have previously make discovery of Janet Ilchester . relate our adventure on different part of the lawn , we both hear that Colonel Goodwin and his daughter have journey down to Riversley to smooth the way for my return ; so my easy conquest of the squire be not at all wonderful ; nevertheless , I maintain my sense of triumph , and be assure in my secret heart that I have a singular masterfulness , and could , when I choose to put it forth , compel my grandfather to hold out his hand to my father as he have do to I . 
 Julia Rippenger be a guest at Riversley through a visit pay to she by my aunt Dorothy in alarm at my absence . the intention be to cause the squire a distraction . it succeed ; for the old man need lively prattle of a less childish sort than Janet Ilchester 's at his elbow , and that young lady , though true enough in her fashion , be the ardent friend of none but flourish head ; whereas Julia , find my name under a cloud at Riversley , speak of I , I be lead to imagine by Captain Bulsted , as a ballad hero , a gloriful fellow , a darling whose deed be all pardonable a mere puff of smoke in the splendour of his nature . 
 ' to hear the young lady allude to I in that style ! ' he confide to my ear , with an ineffable heave of his big chest . 
 certain good influence , at any rate , preserve the squire from threaten to disinherit I . I fancy all be well . before I try the second step , Captain Bulsted ride over to my father , who himself generously enjoin the prudent course , in accordance with his aforegone precept . he be float off , as he term it , from the inn where he lie strand , to London , by I know not what heaven - send gift of money , bid I keep in view the grand career I be to commence at Dipwell on arrive at my majority . I would have go with he have he beckon a finger . the four - and - twenty bottle of Hock be range in a line for the stable - boy to cock - shy at they under the squire 's supervision and my enforced attendance , just as revolutionary criminal be execute . I feel like the survivor of friend , who have see their blood flow . 
 he hand I a cheque for the payment of debt incur in my recent adventure . who could help be grateful for it ? and yet his remorseless spilling of the kindly wine full of mellow recollection of my father and the little princess , drive the sense of gratitude out of I . 




 CHAPTER XX . news of a fresh conquest of MY FATHER 'S 

 Temple go to sea . the wonder be that I do not go with he : we be both in agreement that adventure be the only thing worth live for , and we despise English fellow who have see no place but England . I could not bear the long separation from my father that be my reason for not insist on the squire 's consent to my become a midshipman . after pass a brilliant examination , Temple have the good fortune to join Captain Bulsted 's ship , and there my honest - hearted friend dismally compose his letter of confession , let I know that he have be untrue to friendship , and have propose to Janet Ilchester , and interchange vow with she . he beg my forgiveness , but he do love she so ! he hop I would not mind . I send he a reproachful answer ; I never care for he more warmly than when I see the letter shoot the slope of the postoffice mouth . Aunt Dorothy undertake to communicate assurance of my undying affection for he . as for Janet Temple 's letter , in which he speak of her avow preference for oriental present , and declare his intention of accumulate they on his voyage , be a harpoon in her side . by mean of it I worry and terrify she until she be glad to have it all out before the squire . what do he do ? he say that Margery , her mother , be niggardly ; a girl want present , and I do not act up to my duty ; I ought to buy Turkey and Tunis to please she , if she have a mind for they . 
 the further she be flatter the fast she cry ; she have the face of an old setter with these hideous tear . the squire promise she fifty pound per annum in quarterly payment , that she might buy what present she like , and so tie herself to constancy . he say aside to I , as if he have a knowledge of the sex ' young lady must have lot of knickknack , or their eye will be catch right and leave , remember that . ' I should have be delighted to see she catch . she talk of love in a ludicrous second - hand way , send I into fit of disgusted laughter . on other occasion her lip be not hypocritical , and her figure anything but awkward . she be a bold , plump girl , fond of male society . Heriot enrapture she . I believe at the time she would have appoint a year to marry he in , have he put the question . but too many woman be in love with Heriot . he and I meet Kiomi on the road to the race - course on the southdown ; the prettiest racecourse in England , shut against gipsy . a bare - footed swarthy girl run beside our carriage and toss we flower . he and a friend of his , young Lord Destrier , son of the Marquis of Edbury , who know my father well , talk and laugh with she , and think she so very handsome that I likewise begin to stare , and I suddenly call ' Kiomi ! ' she bound back into the hedge . this be our second meeting . it would have be a pleasant one have not Heriot and Destrier pretend all sort of thing about our previous acquaintance . neither of we , they say , have make a bad choice , but why have we separate ? she snatch her hand out of mine with a grin of anger like puss in a fury . we have wonderful fun with she . they take she to a great house near the race - course , and there , assist by one of the young lady , dress she in flow silk , and so pass she through the gate of the enclosure interdict to bare foot . there they lead she to group of fashionable lady , and get themselves into pretty scrape . they say she be an Indian . Heriot lose his wager and call she a witch . she reply , ' you will find I be one , young man , ' and that be the only true thing she speak of the day to come . owe to the hubbub around the two who be guilty of this unmeasured joke upon consequential lady , I have to conduct she to the gate . instantly , and without a good - bye , she scramble up her skirt and run at stride across the road and through the wood , out of sight . she win her dress and a piece of jewelry . 
 with Heriot I go on a sad expedition , the same I have set out upon with Temple . this time I see my father behind those high red wall , once so mysterious and terrible to I . Heriot make light of prison for debt . he insist , for my consolation , that they have but a temporary dishonourable signification ; very estimable gentleman , as well as scamp , inhabit they , he say . the impression produce by my visit the feasting among ruined man who believe in good luck the more the low they fall from it , and their fearful admiration of my imprisoned father be as if I have drink a stupefy liquor . I be unable clearly to reflect on it . daily afterwards , until I release he , I make journey to usurer to get a loan on the faith of the reversion of my mother 's estate . Heriot , like the real friend he be , help I with his name to the bond . when my father stand free , I have the proud heart alive ; and as soon as we have part , the most amazed . for a long while , for year , the thought of he be haunt by racketball and bearded man in their shirtsleeve ; a scene sicken to one 's pride . yet it have grow impossible for I to think of he without pride . I delight to hear he . we be happy when we be together . and , moreover , he swear to I on his honour , in Mrs. Waddy 's presence , that he and the constable would henceforth keep an even pace . his exuberant cheerfulness and charming playfulness be always fascinating . his vision of our glorious future enchain I . how it be that something precious have go out of my life , I could not comprehend . 
 Julia Rippenger 's marriage with Captain Bulsted be , an agreeable distraction . unfortunately for my peace of mind , she go to the altar poignantly pale . my aunt Dorothy settle the match . she have scheme it , her silence and half - downcast look seem to confess , for the sake of her own repose , but neither to she nor to other do that come of it . I write a plain warning of the approach catastrophe to Heriot , and receive his reply after it be over , to this effect : 
 ' in my regiment we have a tolerable knowledge of woman . they like change , old Richie , and we must be content to let they take their twenty shilling for a sovereign . I myself prefer the Navy to the Army ; I have no right to complain . once she swear one thing , now she have swear another . we will hope the lady will stick to her choice , and not seek small change . " I could not forgive copper " ; that be quote your dad . I have no wish to see the uxorious object , though you praise he . his habit of fall under the table be middle old - fashioned ; but she may like he the well , or she may cure he . whatever she be as a woman , she be a very nice girl to enliven the atmosphere of the switch . I sometimes look at a portrait I have of J. R. , which , I fancy , Mrs. William Bulsted have no right to demand of I ; but suppose her husband think he have , why then I must consult my brother officer . we want a war , old Richie , and I wish you be sit at our mess , and not moon about girl and woman . ' 
 I presume from this that Heriot 's passion for Julia be extinct . Aunt Dorothy disapprove of his tone , which I think admirably philosophical and coxcombi - cally imitable , an expression of the sort of thing I should feel on hearing of Janet Ilchester 's nuptial . 
 the daring and success of that foreign adventure of mine have , with the aid of Colonel and Clara Goodwin , convince the squire of the folly of stand between I and he I love . it be consider the good sign possible that he should take I down on an inspection of his various estate and his great coal - mine , and introduce I as the heir who would soon relieve he of the task . 
 perhaps he think the smell of wealth a promising cure for such fit of insubordination as I have exhibit . my occasional absence on my own account be wink at . on my return the squire be sour and snappish , I cheerful and complaisant ; I grow cold , and he solicitous ; he would drink my health with a challenge to heartiness , and I drink to he heartily and he relapse to a fit of sulk , inform I , that in his time young man know when they be well off , and ask I whether I be up to any young man 's villanie , have any conceal debt perchance , because , if so oh ! he know the way of youngster , especially when they fall into bad hand : the list of bad title rumble on in an underbreath like cowardly thunder : well , to cut the matter short , because , if so , his cheque - book be at my service ; do not   I know that , eh ? not be immediately distress by debt , I do not exhibit the gush of gratitude , and my sedate ' thank you , sir , ' confuse his appeal for some sentimental show of affection . 
 I be sure the poor old man suffer pang of jealousy ; I could even at time see into his breast and pity he . he want little more than to be manage ; but a youth when he perceive absurdity in opposition to he chafe at it as much as if he be unaware that it be laughable . have the squire talk to I in those day seriously and fairly of my father 's character , I should have abandon my system of defence to plead for he as before a judge . by that time I have gain the knowledge that my father be totally of a different construction from other man . I wish the squire to own simply to his loveable nature . I could have tell he woman do . without cite my dear aunt Dorothy , or so humble a creature as the devoted Mrs. Waddy , he have sincere friend among woman , who esteem he , and be staunch adherent to his cause ; and if the widow of the City knight , Lady Sampleman , aim openly at be something more , she be not the less his friend . nor be it only his powerful animation , generosity , and grace that win they . 
 there occur when I be a little past twenty , already much in his confidence , one of those strange crucial event which try a man publicly , and bring out whatever can be say for and against he . a young Welsh heiress fall in love with he . she be , I think , seven or eight month young than myself , a handsome , intelligent , high - spirited girl , rather want in polish , and perhaps in the protect sense of decorum . she be well - bear , of course she be Welsh . she be really well - breed too , though somewhat brusque . the young lady fall hopelessly in love with my father at Bath . she give out that he be not to be for one moment accuse of have encourage she by secret address . it be her unsolicited avowal think by my aunt Dorothy immodest , not by I that she prefer he to all live man . her name be Anna Penrhys . the squire one morning receive a letter from her family , request he to furnish they with information as to the antecedent of a gentleman call himself Augustus Fitz - George Frederick William Richmond Guelph Roy , for purpose which would , they assure he , warrant the inquiry . he be for throw the letter aside , shout that he thank his God he be unacquainted with anybody on earth with such an infernal list of name as that . Roy ! who know anything of Roy ? 
 ' it happen to be my father 's present name , ' say I. 
 ' it sound to I like the name of one of those blackguard adventurer who creep into family to catch the fool , ' pursue the squire , not hear I with his eye . 
 ' the letter at least must be answer , ' my aunt Dorothy say . 
 ' it shall be answer ! ' the squire work himself up to roar . he write a reply , the content of which I could guess at from my aunt 's refusal to let I be present at the discussion of it . the letter despatch be write by she , with his signature . her eye glitter for a whole day . 
 then come a statement of the young lady 's case from Bath . 
 ' look at that ! look at that ! ' cry the squire , and go on , ' look at that ! ' in a muffled way . there be a touch of dignity in his unforced anger . 
 my aunt wince displeasingly to my sight : ' I see nothing to astonish one . ' 
 ' nothing to astonish one ! ' the squire set his mouth in imitation of she . 
 ' you see nothing to astonish one ? well , ma'am , when a man grow old enough to be a grandfather , I do see something astonishing in a child of nineteen by George ! it be out o ' nature . but you woman like monstrosity . oh ! I understand . here be an heiress to fifteen thousand a year . it be not astonishing if every ruin gambler and scapegrace in the kingdom 's hunt her hot ! no , no ! that be not astonishing . I suppose she have her money in a coal mine . ' 
 the squire have some of his in a coal - mine ; my mother once have ; it be the delivery of a blow at my father , signify that he have the scent for this description of wealth . I leave the room . the squire then affect that my presence have constrain he , by bellow out epithet easy for I to hear in the hall and out on the terrace . he vow by solemn oath he be determined to save this girl from ruin . my aunt 's speech be brief . 
 I be summon to Bath by my father in a curious peremptory tone imply the utmost urgent need of I . 
 I hand the letter to the squire at breakfast , say , ' you must spare I for a week or so , sir . ' 
 he spread the letter flat with his knife , and turn it over with his fork . 
 ' Harry , ' say he , half - kindly , and choke , ' you be well out of it . ' 
 ' I be the good friend he could have by he , sir . ' 
 ' you be the good tool he could have handy , for you be a gentleman . ' 
 ' I hope I shall not   offend you , grandfather , but I must go . ' 
 ' do not   you see , Harry Richmond , you be in for an infernal marriage ceremony there ! ' 
 ' the young lady be not of age , ' interpose my aunt . 
 ' eh ? an infernal elopement , then . it be clear the girl 's mad - head 's crack as a cocoa - nut bowl by a monkey , brain nowhere . Harry , you be not a greenhorn ; you do not   suspect you be call down there to stop it , do you ? you jump plump into a furious lot of the girl 's relative ; you might as well take a header into a leech - pond . come ! you be a man ; think for yourself . do not   have this affair on your conscience , boy . I tell you , Harry Richmond , I be against your going . you go against my will ; you offend I , sir ; you drag my name and blood into the mire . she be Welsh , be she ? those Welsh be addle - pat , every one . poor girl ! ' 
 he throw a horrible tremour into his accent of pity . 
 my aunt express her view mildly , that I be send for to help cure the young lady of her delusion . 
 ' and take she himself ! ' cry the squire . ' Harry , you would not   go and do that ? why , the law , man , the law the whole country would be up about it . you will be stick in a coloured caricature ! ' 
 he be really alarmed lest this should be one of the consequence of my going , and describe some of the scourging caricature of his day with an intense appreciation of their awfulness as engine of the moral sense of the public . I go nevertheless . 




 CHAPTER XXI . a promenade in BATH 

 I find my father at his hotel , sit with his friend Jorian DeWitt , whom I have meet once before , and think clever . he be an ex - captain of dragoon , a martyr to gout , and addict to Burgundy , which necessitate his resort to the water , cause he , as he say , between his appetite and the penance he pay for they , to lead the life of a pendulum . my father be in a temper gay mood , examine a couple of the county newspaper . one abuse he virulently ; he be support by the other . after embrace I , he desire I to listen while he read out oppose sentence from the column of these eminent journal : 
 ' the person call himself " Roy , " whose monstrously absurd pretension be suppose to be embody in this self - dub surname ... ' 
 ' the celebrated and courtly Mr. Richmond Roy , know no less by the fascination of his manner than by his romantic history ... ' 
 ' have very soon succeed in make himself the talk of the town ... ' 
 ' have latterly become the theme of our tea - table ... ' 
 ' which be always the adventurer 's privilege ... ' 
 ' through no fault of his own ... ' 
 ' that we may throw light on the blushing aspiration of a crow - sconce Cupid , it will be as well to recall the antecedent of this   if no bad   preposterous imitation buck of the old school ... ' 
 ' suffice it , without seek to draw the veil from those affect chapter of his early career which kindle for he the enthusiastic sympathy of all class of his countryman , that he be not yet free from a tender form of persecution ... ' 
 ' we think we be justify in entitle he the Perkin Warbeck of society ... ' 
 ' reference might be make to mythological hero ... ' 
 Hereat I cry out mercy . 
 Captain DeWitt   stretch nursing a leg   remove his silk handkerchief from his face to murmur , 
 ' the bass stedfastly drown the treble , if this be mean for harmony . ' 
 my father ring up the landlord , and say to he , 
 ' the choicest of your cellar at dinner to - day , Mr. Lumley ; and , mind you , I be your guest , and I exercise my right of compel you to sit down with we and assist in consume a doubtful quality of wine . we dine four . Lay for five , if your conscience be bad , and I excuse you . ' 
 the man smirk . he venture to say he have never be so tempt to supply an inferior article . 
 my father smile on he . 
 ' you invite our editorial advocate ? ' say Captain DeWitt . 
 ' our adversary , ' say my father . 
 I protest I would not sit at table with he . but he assure I he believe his advocate and his adversary to be one and the same , and refer I to the collated sentence . 
 ' the man must earn his bread , Richie , boy ! to tell truth , it be the advocate I wish to rebuke , and to praise the adversary . it will confound he . ' 
 ' it do I , ' say DeWitt . 
 ' you perceive , Jorian , a policy in din these man of the Press now and occasionally , consider their grow power , do you not ? ' 
 ' ay , ay ! it be a great gossiping machine , mon Roy . I prefer to let it spout . ' 
 ' I crave your permission to invite he in complimentary term , cousin Jorian . he be in the town ; remember , it be for the good of the nation that he and his like should have the opportunity of study good society . as to myself personally , I give he carte blanche to fire his shot at I . ' 
 near the fashionable hour of the afternoon my father take my arm , Captain DeWitt a stick , and we walk into the throng and buzz . 
 ' whenever you be , to quote our advocate , the theme of tea - table , Richie , ' say my father , ' walk through the crowd : it will wash you . it be do we the honour to observe we . we in turn discover an interest in its general countenance . ' 
 he be receive , as we pass , with much staring ; here and there a lifting of hat , and some blunt nodding that incense I , but he , feel I bristle , squeeze my hand and talk of the scene , and ever and anon gather a line of head and shed an indulgent bow along them- ; so on to the Casino . not once do he offend my taste and make my acute sense of self - respect shiver by appear grateful for a recognition , or anxious to court it , though the curtest salute meet his acknowledgement . 
 the interior of the Casino seem more hostile . I remark it to he . ' a trifle more eye - glassy , ' he murmur . he be quite at his easy there . 
 ' we walk up and down , my son , ' he say , in answer to a question of mine , ' because there be very few who can ; even walk be an art ; and if nobody do , the place be dull . ' 
 ' the place be pretty well supply with newspaper , ' say Captain DeWitt . 
 ' and dowager , friend Jorian . they be cousin . ' tis the fashion to have our tattle do by machinery . they have their opportunity to compare the portrait with the original . come , invent some scandal for we ; let we make this place our social Exchange . I warrant a good bold piece of invention will fit they , too , some of they . Madam , ' my father bow low to the beckoning of a fan , ' I trust your ladyship do not chance to overhear that last remark I make ? ' 
 the lady reply : ' I should have shut my eye if I have . I call you to tell I , who be the young man ? ' 
 ' for twenty year I have live in the proud belief that he be my son ! ' 
 ' I would not disturb it for the world . ' she do I the honour to inspect I from the low waistcoat button to the eyebrow . ' bring he to I to - night . Captain DeWitt , you have forsake my whist - table . ' 
 ' purely temporary fit of unworthiness , my lady . ' 
 ' in English , gout ? ' 
 ' not gout in the conscience , I trust , ' say my father . 
 ' oh ! that be curable , ' laugh the captain . 
 ' you man of repartee would be nothing without your wickedness , ' the lady observe . 
 ' man be suppose to be incomplete ' Captain DeWitt affect a murmur . 
 she nod ' yes , yes , ' and lift eye on my father . ' so you have not give up go to church ? ' 
 he bend and speak low . 
 she humphe her lip . ' very well , I will see . it must be a night in the early part of the week after next , then : I really do not   know why I should serve you ; but I like your courage . ' 
 ' I can not consent to accept your ladyship 's favour on account of one single virtue , ' say he , droop . 
 she wave he to move forward . 
 during this frothy dialogue , I could see that the ear of the assembly have be catch by the sound of it . 
 ' that , ' my father inform I , ' be the great Lady Wilts . now you will notice a curious thing . Lady Wilts be not so old but that , as our Jorian here say of she , she be marriageable . hence , Richie , she be a queen to make the masculine knee knock the ground . I fear the same be not to be say of her rival , Lady Denewdney , whom our good Jorian compare to an antiquated fledgeling emerge with effort from a nest of ill construction and bad cement . she be rich , she be sharp , she use she quill ; she be emphatically not marriageable . bath might still accept she as a rival queen , only she be always behindhand in seize an occasion . now you will catch sight of her fan work in a minute . she be envious and imitative . it would be undoubtedly well policy on her part to continue to cut I : she can not , she be begin to rustle like December 's oak . if Lady Wilts have I , why , she must . we refrain from notice she until we have turn twice . ay , Richie , there be this use in adversity ; it teach one to play sword and target with etiquette and retenue well than any crown king in Europe . for I now to cross to her summon immediately would be a gross breach of homage to Lady Wilts , who be inspire to be the first to break through the fence of scandal environ I . but I must still show that I be independent . these people must not suppose that I have to cling to a party . let they take side ; I be on fair term with both the rival . I show just such a nuance of a distinction in my treatment of they just such enough , I mean , to make the flattered one warm to I , and t ' other be jealous of she . ay , Richie , these thing be trivial thing beyond the grave ; but here be we , my boy ; and , by the way , I suspect the great campaign of my life be open . ' 
 Captain DeWitt say that if so it would be the tenth , to his certain knowledge . 
 ' not great campaign ! ' my father insist : ' mere skirmish before this . '

' and rise to the upper note of his groan : ' ignorance , density , total imbecility , be well ; I would rather any day of my life sit and carve for guest the gross of human trial a detestable dinner , than be doom to hear some wretched fellow and you hear the old as well as the young excruciate feeling which , where they exist , can not but be exquisitely delicate . goodness gracious I ! to see the man pump up his wit ! for I , my visage be of an unalterable gravity whenever I be present at one of these exhibition .  CHAPTER XXII . conclusion of the BATH episode 

 the friend of Miss Penrhys be ill advise in try to cry down a man like my father . active persecution be the breath of life to he . when untroubled he be apt to let both his ambition and his dignity slumber . the squib and scandal set afloat concern he arm his wit , nerve his temper , touch he with the spirit of enterprise ; he become a new creature . I lose sight of certain characteristic which I have begin to ponder over critically . I believe with all my heart that circumstance be blameable for much that do not quite please I . upon the question of his magnanimity , as well as of his courage , there could not be two opinion . he would neither retort nor defend himself .  my father borrow these letter , and return they to I , say , ' a good soul ! the good of woman ! there there be a treasure lose !  she seem to I preternaturally anxious about Miss Penrhys .

 he lose his voice .

People smile and bow , and forget their curiosity , forget even to be critical , while he be in sight . I can say this , for I be acutely critical of their bearing ; the atmosphere of the place be never perfectly pleasing to I . 
 my attitude of watchful reserve , and my reputation as the heir of immense wealth , tend possibly to constrain a certain number of the inimical party to be ostensibly civil .  at a dancing party where we meet , she be throw on my hand by her ungovernable vehemence , and I , as I have tell Lady Wilts , not be able to understand the liking of twenty for forty   fifty would have be near the actual mark , or sixty , offer she no lively sympathy . I see more than I grasp . love for my father be to my mind a natural thing , a proof of taste and goodness ; woman might love he ; but the love of a young girl with the morning 's mystery about she ! and for my progenitor ! a girl   as I reflect in the midst of my interjection   well - build , clear - eye , animate , clever , with soft white hand and pretty foot ; how could it be ? she be sombre as a sunken fire until he at last come round to she , and then her sudden vivacity be surprising . 
 affair be no far advanced when I have to obey the squire 's command and return to Riversley , miss the night of the grand ball with no profound regret , except for my father 's sake . he write soon after one of his characteristic letter , to tell I that the ball have , be a success . immediately upon this announcement , he indulge luxurious reflection , as his manner be :

 out of his circle of attraction book be my resource . 




 CHAPTER XXIII . MY TWENTY - first birthday 

 book and dream , like the two river cite by my father , flow side by side in I without mix ; and which the bright Rhone be , which the brown Arve , need not to be tell to those who know anything of youth ; they be destine to intermingle soon enough . I read well , for I feel ground and have mount view ; the real world , and the mind and passion of the world , grow visible to I . my tutor please the squire immensely by call I matter - of - fact . in philosophy and history I hat speculation ; but nothing be too fantastic for my idea of possible occurrence . once away from book , I carry a head that shoot rocket to the farth hill .

Harry ' ; then they shadow : ' she be worth ten of I . ' such pathetic humility tempt I to exalt she supremely .

 we see they together on my twenty - first birthday . to my shame I awake in the early morning at Riversley , forgetful of my father 's old appointment for the great Dipwell feast . not long after sunrise , when blackbird peck the lawn , and swallow be out from under eave to the flood 's face , I be hail by Janet Ilchester beneath my open window .
