 a shilling toss by an english boy to a couple of little foreign girl in a woodman 's hut ! you would not expect it to withstand the common fate of silver coin , and preserve mysterious virtue by live celibate , neither multiply nor reduce , ultimately to play the part of a powerful magician in bring the boy grow man to the foot of an illustrious lady , and she to his side in sickness , treasonably to the law of her station . the little woman quarrel over it , and snatch and hide and contemplate it in secret , each in her turn , until the strife it engender be put an end to by a doughty smith , their mother 's brother , who divide it into equal half , through which he drive a hole , and the piece be now throw out of the currency , each one wear her share of it in her bosom from that time , proudly appease . they be not ordinary peasant child , and happily for they they have another friend that be not a bird of passage , and be endow by nature and position to do the work of an angel . she have they educate to read , write , and knit , and learn pretty manner , and in good season she take one of the sister to wait on her own person . the second go , upon her recommendation , into the household of a Professor of a neighbouring university . but neither of they abjure her superstitious belief in the prove merit of the talisman she wear . so when they see the careless giver again they remember he ; their gratitude be as fresh as on that romantic morning of their childhood , and they resolve without concert to serve he after their own fashion , and quickly spy a way to it . they be german girl .

 CHAPTER XXXIV . I gain a perception of PRINCELY STATE 

 I have a visit from Prince Ernest , nominally one of congratulation on my escape . I be never in my life so much at any man 's mercy : he might have fevere I to death with reproach , and I expect they on hear his name pronounce at the door .  ' your Shakespeare , I think , ' say the prince , ' have a scene of young Frenchmen praise their horse . I myself be no strange to the enthusiasm : one could not stake life and honour on a nobler brute . pardon I if I state my opinion that you young englishman of to - day be sometimes rather overbearing in your assumption of a superior knowledge of horseflesh . we Germans in the baltic province and in the austrian cavalry think we have a right to a remark or two ; and if we have not suborn the testimony of modern history , the value of our hanoverian trooper be not unknown to one at least of your general . however , the odd be that you be right and Otto wrong , and he certainly put himself in the wrong to defend his ground . '

' nothing but poetry , and I say it who do not read it '   Chancellor von Redwitz be the speaker ' nothing but poetry make romance passable : for poetry be the everlastingly and embracingly human . without it your fiction be flat foolishness , non - nourishing substance a species of brandy and gruel ! diet for craving stomach that can support nothing solider , and must have the weak stuff stiffen . talk of poetry , there be an independent hereditary princess of Leiterstein in love with a poet ! a Leonora d'este ! this be no Tasso . nevertheless , she propose to come to nuptial . good , you observe ? America to America , North and South ; India to Europe . India be for the land with the large sea - board . mistress of the Baltic , of the North Sea and the East , as eventually she must be , Germany would claim to take India as a matter of course , and find an outlet for the energy of the most prolific and the tough of the race of mankind , the pure , in fact , the only true race , properly so call , out of India , to which it would return as to its source , and there create an empire magnificent in force and solidity , the actual wedding of East and West ; an empire firm on the ground and in the blood of the people , instead of an empire of alien , that would bear comparison to a finely fret cotton - hang palanquin balance on an elephant 's back , all depend on the docility of the elephant   his description of Great Britain 's Indian Empire .  CHAPTER XXXVI . HOMEWARD and HOME again 

 the room be in flame , Baroness Turckems pluck at the bell - rope , my father look big and brilliant . 
 ' hold hand ! ' he shout to the frenzied baroness . 
 she counter - shout ; both of they stamp foot ; the portico sentinel strike the butt of his musket on the hall - door ; bell answer bell along the upper gallery .

'

 CHAPTER XXXVII . JANET RENOUNCES ME 

 an illness of old Sewis , the butler , amazingly resemble a sick monkey in his bed , keep I from pay a visit to Temple and see my father for several week , during which time Janet loyally accustom the squire to hear of the german princess , and she do it with a decent and agreeable cheerfulness that I quite approve of . I should have be enrage at a martyr - like appearance on her part , for I demand a sprightly devotion to my interest , consider love so holy a thing , that where it exist , all surround person be bind to do it homage and service . we be throw together a great deal in attend on poor old Sewis , who would lie on his pillow recount for hour my father 's midnight summon of the inhabitant of Riversley , and his little Harry 's infant expedition into the world . Temple and Heriot come to stay at the Grange , and assist in some rough scene - paint torrid colour represent the island of Jamaica . we hang it at the foot of old Sewis 's bed . he awake and contemplate it , and go downstairs the same day , cure , he declare : the fact be that the unfortunate picture testify too strongly to the reversal of all he be use to in life , in have those he serve to wait on he . the squire celebrate his recovery by give a servant ' ball . Sewis dance with the handsome lass , swing she to supper , and deliver an extraordinary speech , entirely concern I , and rather to my discomposure , particularly so when it be my fate to hear that the old man have make I the heir of his saving . such be his announcement , in a very excited voice , but incidentally upon a solemn adjuration to the squire to beware of his temper govern his temper and not be a turncoat . 
 we be present at the head of the supper - table to hear our health drunk . Sewis speak like a half - caste oblivious of his training , and of the subject he be at liberty to touch on as well . evidently there be a weight of forebode on his mind . he know his master well . the squire excuse he under the ejaculation , ' Drunk , by the Lord ! " very well . I go my lawyer next day . you see my will , squire . year ago , and little Harry so high . Old Sewis not the man to change . he no turncoat , squire . God bless you , my master ; you recollect , and lady tell you if you forget , old Sewis no turncoat . you hate turncoat . you teach old Sewis , and God bless you , and Mr. Harry , and British Constitution , all amen ! ' 
 with that he bound to bed . he be dead next morning . 
 the squire be humorous over my legacy . it amount to about seventeen hundred pound invest in Government Stock , and he ask I what I mean to do with it ; propose a Charity to be establish on behalf of decay half - caste , insist that servant ' money could never be appropriate to the us of gentleman . all the while he be mutter , ' Turncoat ! eh ? turncoat ? ' proof that the word have strike where it be aim . for I , after think on it , I have a superstitious respect for the legacy , so I determine , in spite of the squire 's laughter over ' sixty pound per annum ! ' to let it rest in my name : I see for the first time the possibility that I might not have my grandfather 's wealth to depend upon . he warn I of grow miserly . with my father in London , live freely on my property , I have not much fear of that . however , I say discreetly , ' I do not   mind spend when I see my way . ' 
 ' oh ! see your way , ' say he . ' well a niggard than a chuckfist . only , there be my girl : she be good at account . one will do for they , Harry ? ha'n't   be long enough at home yet ? ' 
 few be the occasion when our conversation do not diverge to this sort of interrogation . Temple and Heriot , with whom I take counsel , advise I to wait until the idea of the princess have wear its way into his understanding , and leave the work to Janet . ' though , ' say Heriot to I aside , ' upon my soul , it be slaughter . ' he believe that Janet feel keenly . but then , she admire he , and so they repay one another . 
 I win my grandfather 's confidence in practical matter on a trip we take into Wales . but it be not enough for I to be a man of business , he affirm ; he want I to have some ambition ; why not stand for our county at the next general election ? he offer I his Welsh borough if I think fit to decline a contest . this be to speak as mightily as a german prince . virtually , in wealth and power , he be a prince ; but of how queer a kind ! he be immensely gratify by my refraining to look out for my father on our return journey through London , and remark , that I have not see he for some time , he suppose . to which I say , no , I have not , he advise I to let the fellow run his length . suggest that he hold it likely I contribute to ' the fellow 's ' support : he say generously , ' keep clear of he , Hal : I add you a thousand a year to your allowance , ' and damn I for be so thoughtful over it . I find myself shudder at a breath of anger from he . could he not with a word dash my hope for ever ? the warning I have take from old Sewis transform I to something like a hypocrite , and I dare say I give the squire to understand , that I have not see my father for a very long period and know nothing of his recent doing . 
 ' be infernally quiet these last two or three year , ' the squire mutter of the object of his aversion . ' I hear of a City widow last , sick as a Dover packet - boat 'bout the fellow ! well , the woman be ninny , but you be a man , Harry ; you be not to be take in any long , eh ? ' 
 I reply that I know my father well now , and be ask how the deuce I know he well ; it be the world I know well after my stay on the continent . 
 I contain myself enough to say , ' very well , the world , sir . ' 
 ' flirt with one of their princess ? ' he wink . 
 ' on that subject I will talk to you some other time , ' say I.

' 
 the necessity for correct his impression teach I to think the moment favourable . I say , ' I be engaged to she , sir . ' 
 he return promptly : ' then you will break it off . ' 
 I shake my head . 
 ' why , you can not   jilt my girl at home ! ' say he . 
 ' do you find a princess objectionable , sir ? ' 
 ' objectionable ? she be a foreigner . I do not   know she . I never see she . here be my Janet I have   bring up for you , under my own eye , out of the way of every damned soft - sawderer , safe and plump as a melon under a glass , and you fight shy of she , and go and engage yourself to a foreigner I do not   know and never see ! by George , Harry , I will call in a parson to settle you soon as ever we sight Riversley . I will couple you , by George , I will ! ' fore either of you know whether you be on your leg or your back . ' 
 we be in the street of London , so he be oblige to moderate his vehemence . 
 ' have you consult Janet ? ' say I. 
 ' consult she ? ever since she be a chick with half a feather on . ' 
 ' a chick with half a feather on , ' I remark , ' be not always of the same mind as a piece of poultry of full plumage . ' 
 ' hang your sneering and your talk of a fine girl , like my Janet , as a piece of poultry , you young rooster ! you toss your head up like a cock too conceited to crow . I will swear the girl be in love with you . she do you the honour to be fond of you . she be one in a million . a handsome girl , straight - back , honest , just a dash , and not too much , of our blood in she . ' 
 ' consult she again , sir , ' I break in . ' you will discover she be not of your way of thinking . ' 
 ' do you mean to say she be give you a left - hander , Harry ? ' 
 ' I have only to say that I have not give she the option . ' 
 he groan go up the step of his hotel , face I once or twice , and almost gain my sympathy by observe , ' when we be boy , the old one worry we ; when we be old one , the boy begin to tug ! ' he rarely speak so humanely , rarely , at least , to I . 
 for a wonder , he let the matter drop : possibly because he find I temperate . I try the system on he with good effect during our stay in London ; that be , I take upon myself to be always cool , always courteous , deliberate in my reply , and not uncordial , though I be for represent the reserved young man . I obtain some praise for my style and bear among his acquaintance . to one lady pass an encomium on I , he say , ' oh , some foreign princess have be train he , ' which seem to I of good augury . 
 my friend Temple and Heriot be among the Riversley guest at Christmas . we ride over to John Thresher 's , of whom we hear that the pretty Mabel Sweetwinter have disappear , and understand that suspicion have fall upon one of us gentleman . Bob , her brother , have go the way of the brave english fellow of his class - to America . we call on the miller , a sour old man . Bob 's evasion affect he more than Mabel 's , Martha Thresher say , in derision of our sex . I be pain to hear from she that Bob suppose I the misleader of his sister ; and that he have , as she believe , leave England , to avoid the misery of ever meet I again , because he like I so much . she have be see walk down the lane with some one resemble I in figure . Heriot take the miller 's view , count the loss of one stout young Englishman to his country of far great importance than the escapade of dozen of girl , for which simple creature he have no compassion : he hold the expression of it a sham . he have grow coxcombical . without talk of his conquest , he talk largely of the lady who be possibly in the situation of victim to his grace of person , though he do not do so with any unctuous boasting . on the contrary , there be a rather take undertone of regret that his enfeeble over - fat country would give her military son no worthy occupation . he laugh at the mention of Julia Bulsted 's name . ' she prove , Richie , marriage be the good of all receipt for woman , just as it be the bad for man . Poor Billy bulste , for instance , a first - rate seaman , and his heart 's only half in his profession since he and Julia swear their oath ; and no wonder , he make something his own that will not   go under lock and key . no military or naval man ought ever to marry . ' 
 ' stop , ' say Temple , ' be the poor old country how about continue the race of hero ? ' 
 Heriot commend he to rectory , vicarage , and curate ' lodging for breeding ground , and come round to Julia relate one of the racy dialogue of her married life . ' the saltwater widow 's delicious . Billy rush home from his ship in a hurry . what be this Greg write I ? that he be get a friend of his to drink with he , d ' ye mean , William ? a friend of your , ma'am . and will you say a friend of mine be not a friend of your , William ? Julia , you be drive I mad ! and be that far from crazy , where you say I drive you at first sight of I , William ? back to his ship go Billy with a song of love and constancy . ' 
 I say nothing of my chagrin at the behaviour of the pair who have furnish my first idea of the romantic beauty of love . 
 ' why do she talk twice as irish as she use to , Heriot ? ' 
 ' just to coax the world to let she be as nonsensical as she like . she be awfully dull ; she have only her nonsense to amuse she . I repeat : soldier and sailor ought not   to marry . I be her good friend . I be , on my honour : for I ' m go to make Billy give up the service , since he can not   give she up . there she be ! ' he cry out , and wave his hat to a lady on horseback some way down the slope of a road lead to the view of our heathland : 
 ' there be the only girl live fit to marry a man and swear she will stick to he through life and death . ' 
 he start at a gallop . Temple would have go too at any possible speed , for he know as well as I do that Janet be the girl alone capable of win a respectful word from Heriot ; but I detain he to talk of Ottilia and my dismal prospect of persuade the squire to consent to my proposal for she , and to dower she in a manner worthy a princess . he dole out his yes and no to I vacantly . Janet and Heriot come at a walk pace to meet we , he question she , she replying , but a little differently from her usual habit of turn her full face to the speaker . he be evidently startle , and , to judge from his posture , repeat his question , as one would say , ' you do this ? ' she nod , and then utter some rapid word , glance at he , laugh shyly , and sink her feature into repose as we draw near . she have a deep blush on her face . I think it might be , that Janet and her loud champion have come to particular term , a supposition that touch I with regret for Temple 's sake . but Heriot be not look pleased . it happen that whatever Janet utter strike a chord of opposition in I . she like the Winter and the Winter sunset , have hope of a frost for skating , like our climate , think our way of keep Christmas venerable , rejoice in dispense the squire 's bounty call they bounty , join Heriot in abuse foreign country to the exaltation of her own : all this with ' well , Harry , I be sorry you do not   think as we do . and we do , do not   we ? ' she address he . 
 ' I reserve a point , ' he say , and not playfully . 
 she appear distressed , and court a change of expression in his feature , and I have to confess that never have see her gaze upon any one save myself in that fashion , which be with her very winning , especially where some of her contralto tone of remonstrance or entreaty aid it , I feel as a man do at a neighbour 's shadow cast over his right of property . 
 Heriot drop to the rear : I be glad to leave she with Temple , and glad to see they canter ahead together on the sand of tie heath . 
 ' she have do it , ' Heriot burst out abruptly . ' she have do it ! ' he say again . ' upon my soul , I never wish in my life before that I be a marry man : I might have a chance of end worth something . she have win the squire round with a thunder fib , and you be to have the german if you can get she . do not   be in a hurry . the squire will speak to you to - night : but think over it . will you ? think what a girl this be . I believe on my honour no man ever have such an offer of a true woman . come , do not   think it be Heriot speak I have   always like she , of course . but I have always respect she , and that be not of course . depend upon it , a woman who can be a friend of man be the right sort of woman to make a match with . do you suppose she could not   have a dozen fellow round she at the lift of her finger ? the pick of the land ! I would trust she with an army . I tell you , Janet Ilchester be the only girl alive who will double the man she marry . I do not   know another who would not   make the name of wife laugh the poor devil out of house and company . she be firm as a rock ; and sweet as a flower on it ! will that touch you ? Bah ! Richie , let us talk like man . I feel for she because she be fond of you , and I know what it be when a girl like that set her heart on a fellow . there , ' he conclude , ' I would ask you to go down on your knee and pray before you decide against she ! ' 
 Heriot succeed in raise a certain dull indistinct image in my mind of a well - mean girl , to whom I be bind to feel thankful , and feel so . I thank Heriot , too , for his friendly intention . he have never see the Princess Ottilia . and at night I thank my grandfather . he bear himself , on the whole , like the good and kindly old gentleman Janet love to consider he . he would not stand in my light , he say , recur to that sheet - anchor of a tolerant sentence whenever his forehead begin to gather cloud . he regret that Janet be no well than her sex in her preference for rake , and wish I to the deuce for bring Heriot into the house , and not know when I be lucky . ' german grandchild , eh ! ' he mutter . no Beltham have ever marry a foreigner . what be the time fix between we for the marriage ? he want to see his line safe before he die . ' how do I know this foreign woman'll bear ? ' he ask , expect an answer . his hand be on the back of a chair , grasp and rock it ; his eye bent stormily on the carpet ; they be set blink rapidly after a glance at I . altogether his self - command be creditable to Janet 's tuition . 
 Janet meet I next day , say with some insolence   so it strike I from her liveliness : ' well , it be all right , Harry ? now you will be happy , I hope . I do not shine in my reply . her amiable part appear to be to let I see how brilliant and gracious the commonplace could be make to look . she keep Heriot at the Grange , against the squire 's remonstrance and her mother 's . ' it be to keep he out of harm 's way : the woman he know be not of the good kind for he , ' she say , with astounding fatuity . he submit , and seem to like it . she must be teach Temple to skate figure in the frost , with a great display of good - humour patience , and her voice at musical pitch . but her principal affectation be to talk on matter of business with Mr. Burgin and Mr. Trewint , the squire 's lawyer and bailiff , on mine and interest , on money and economical question ; not shrink from politic either , until the squire cry out to the male assist in the performance , ' Gad , she be a head as good as our half - dozen put together , ' and they servilely join their fragmentary capital in agreement . she go so far as to retain Peterborough to teach she Latin . he be idle in the expectation of a living in the squire 's gift . 
 the annoyance for I be that I could not detach myself from a contemplation of these various scene , by revert to my life in Germany . the preposterous closing of my interview with Ottilia block the way , and I be unable to write to her unable to address she even in imagination , without pang of shame at the review of the petty conspiracy I have sanction to entrap she to plight her hand to I , and without perpetually multiply excuse for my conduct . so to escape they I be reduce to study Janet , form one of her satellite . she could say to I impudently , with all the air of a friendly comrade , ' have your letter from Germany yet , Harry ? ' she fly she be always on the chase . I see her permit Heriot to kiss her hand , and then the squire appear , and Heriot and she burst into laughter , and the squire , with a puzzled face , would have the game explain to he , but understand not a bit of it , only growl at I ; upon which Janet become serious and chid he . I be tell by my aunt Dorothy to admire this behaviour of her . one day she certainly do I a service : a paragraph in one of the newspaper speak of my father , not flatteringly : ' Richmond be in the field again , ' it commence . the squire be wait for she to hand the paper to he . none of we could comprehend why she play he off and deny he his right to the first perusal of the news ; she be voluble , almost witty , full of sprightly Roxalana petulance . 
 ' this paper , ' she say , ' deserve to be burn , ' and she be allow to burn it money article , mining column as well on the pretext of an infamous anti - tory leader , of which she herself compose the first sentence to shock the squire completely . I have sight of that paper some time afterwards . Richmond be in the field again , it state , with mock flourish . but that be not the bad . my grandfather 's name be down there , and mine , and Princess Ottilia 's . my father 's connection with the court of Eppenwelzen - Sarkeld be allude to as the late , and next to his win the heiress of Riversley , the most successful of his venture , inasmuch as his son , if rumour be to be trust , have obtain the promise of the hand of the princess . the paragraph be an excerpt from a gossip weekly journal , perhaps less malevolent than I think it . there be some fun to be get out of a man who , the journal in question be inform , have join the arm of England and a petty german principality stamp on his plate and furniture . 
 my gratitude to Janet be fervent enough when I see what she have save I from . I press her hand and hold it . I talk stupidly , but I make my cruel position intelligible to she , and she have the delicacy , on this occasion , to keep her sentiment regard my father unuttere . we sit hardly less than an hour side by side I know not how long hand in hand . the end be an extraordinary trembling in the limb abandon to I . it seize her frame . I would have detain she , but it be plain she suffer both in her heart and her pride . her voice be under fair command - more than mine be . she counsel I to go to London , at once . ' I would be off to London if I be you , Harry , ' for the purpose of check my father 's extravagance , would have be the further wording , which she spar I ; and I thank she , wish , at the same time , that she would get the habit of use choicer phrase whenever there might , by chance , be a stress of emotion between we . her trembling , and she ' I would be off , ' come into unpleasant collision in the recollection . 
 I acknowledge to myself that she be a true and hearty friend . she listen with interest to my discourse on the necessity of my be in Parliament before I could venture to propose formally for the hand of the princess , and undertake to bear the burden of all consequent negotiation with my grandfather . if she would but have allow I to speak of Temple , instead of say , ' do not , Harry , I like he so much ! ' at the very mention of his name , I should have sincerely feel my indebtedness to she , and some admiration of her fine spirit and figure besides . I could not even agree with my aunt Dorothy that Janet be handsome . when I have to grant she a pardon I appreciate she well . 




 CHAPTER XXXVIII . MY banker ' BOOK 

 the squire again do honour to Janet 's eulogy and good management of he . 
 ' and where , ' say she , ' would you find a Radical to behave so generously , Harry , when it touch he so ? ' 
 he accord I his permission to select my side in politic , merely insist that I be never to change it , and this he request I to swear to , for   he call the ghost of old Sewis to witness   he abhor a turncoat . 
 ' if you be to be a Whig , or a sneaking half - and - half , I can not   help you much , ' he remark . ' I can pop a young Tory in for my borough , maybe ; but I can not   insult a number of independent Englishmen by ask they to vote for the opposite crew ; that be reasonable , eh ? and I can not   promise you plumper for the county neither . you can date your Address from Riversley . you will have your house in town . tell I this princess of yours be ready with her hand , and , ' he throw in roughly , ' be a respectable young woman , I will commence building . you will have a house fit for a prince in town and country , both . ' 
 Temple have produce an effect on he by inform he that ' this princess of mine ' be entitle to be consider a fit and proper person , in rank and blood , for an alliance with the proud royal Houses of Europe , and my grandfather be not quite destitute of consolation in the prospect I present to he . he be a curious study to I , of the Tory mind , in its attachment to solidity , fixity , certainty , its unmatched generosity within a limit , its devotion to the family , and its family eye for the country . an immediate introduction to Ottilia would have win he to enjoy the idea of his grandson 's marriage ; but not have see she , he could not realize her dignity , nor even the womanliness of a ' foreign woman . ' 
 ' thank God for one thing , ' he say : ' we shall not   have that fellow bother shall not   have the other half of your family mess the business . you will have to account for he to your wife as you good can . I ' ve   nothing to do with he , mind that . he come to my house , steal my daughter , craze her wit , drag we all ... ' 
 the excuse to turn away from the hearing of abuse of my father be too good to be neglect , though it be horribly humiliating that I should have to take advantage of it vexatious that I should seem chargeable with tacit lie in allow the squire to suppose the man he hat to be a stranger to the princess . not feel sure whether it might be common prudence to delude he even passively , I think of ask Janet for her opinion , but refrain . a stout deceiver have his merit , but a feeble hypocrite apply to friend to fortify he in his shift and tergiversation must provoke contempt . I desire that Janet might continue to think well of I . I be begin to drop in my own esteem , which be the mirror of my conception of Ottilia 's view of her lover . 
 now , have I consult Janet , I believe the course of my history would have be different , for she would not then , I may imagine , have be guilty of her fatal slip of the tongue that throw we into heavy sea when we think ourselves float on canal water . a canal barge   an image to I of the most perfect attainable peace , suddenly , on its passage through our long fir - wood , with their scented reed and flow rush , wild balsam and silky cotton - grass bed , sluice out to sea and storm , would be somewhat in my likeness soon after a single luckless observation have pass at our Riversley breakfast - table one Sunday morning . 
 my aunt Dorothy and Mr. Peterborough be converse upon the variety of christian sect , and particularly such as approach near to Anglicanism , together with the strange , sadden fact that the christian religion appear to be more divided than , Peterborough regret to say , the form of idolatry establish by the Buddha , Mahomet , and other impostor . he claim the audacious merit for we , that we do not discard the reason of man we admit man 's finite reason to our school of faith , and it be find refractory . hence our many division . 
 ' the Roman Catholics admit reason ? ' say Janet , who have too strong a turn for show her keenness in little encounter with Peterborough . 
 ' no , ' say he ; ' the Protestants . ' and , anxious to elude she , he press on to enchain my aunt Dorothy 's attention . Janet plague he meanwhile ; and I help she . we run he and his schoolboy , the finite refractory , up and down , until Peterborough be glad to abandon he , and Janet say , ' do you preach to the Germans much ? ' he have officiate in Prince Ernest 's private chapel : not , he add in his egregious modesty , not that he personally wish to officiate . 
 ' it be Harry 's wish ? ' Janet say , smile . 
 ' my post of tutor , ' Peterborough hasten to explain , ' be almost entirely supernumerary . the circumstance be so , I the more readily acquiesced in the title of private chaplain , prepare to fulfil such duty as devolve upon I in that capacity , and acting thereon I proffer my occasional service . Lutheranism and Anglicanism be not , doubtless you be aware , divide on the broad basis . we be common protestant . the Papacy , I can assure you , find as little favour with one as with the other . yes , I hold forth , as you would say , from time to time . my assumption of the title of private chaplain , it be think , improve the family dignity that be , on our side . ' 
 ' thought by Harry ? ' say Janet ; and my aunt Dorothy say , ' you and Harry have a consultation about it ? ' 
 ' want to appear as grand as they could , ' quoth the squire . 
 Peterborough signify an assent , design to modify the implication . ' not beyond due bound , I trust , sir . ' 
 ' oh ! now I understand , ' Janet break out in the falsetto note of a puzzle solve in the mind . ' it be his father ! Harry proclaim his private chaplain ! ' 
 ' Mr. Harry 's father do first suggest ' say Peterborough , but her quickly - altered feature cause he to draw in his breath , as she have do after one short laugh . 
 my grandfather turn a round side - eye on I , hard as a cock 's . 
 Janet immediately start topic to fill Peterborough 's mouth : the weather , the walk to church , the probable preacher . ' and , grandada , ' say she to the squire , who be mutter ominously with a grim under - jaw , ' his private chaplain ! ' and for this once would not hear she , ' Grandada , I shall drive you over to see papa this afternoon . ' she talk as if nothing have go wrong . Peterborough , criminal red , attack a jam - pot for a diversion . ' such sweet be rare indeed on the continent , ' he observe to my aunt Dorothy . ' our homemade dainty be matchless . ' 
 ' private chaplain ! ' the squire growl again . 
 ' it be you that preach this afternoon , ' Janet say to Peterborough . ' do you give we an extempore sermon ? ' 
 ' you remind I , Miss Ilchester , I must look to it ; I have a little trimming to do . ' 
 Peterborough think he might escape , but the squire arrest he . ' you will give I five minute before you be out of the house , please . D ' ye smoke on Sundays ? ' 
 ' not on Sundays , sir , ' say Peterborough , openly and cordially , as to signify that they be of one mind regard the perniciousness of Sunday smoking . 
 ' see you do not   set fire to my rick with your foreign chaplain 's trick . I spy you puff behind one t ' other day . there , ' the squire disperse Peterborough 's unnecessary air of abstruse recollection , ' do not   look as though you be try to hit on a pin 's head in a bushel of oats . do not   set my rick on fire that be all . ' 
 ' Mr. Peterborough , ' my aunt Dorothy interpose her voice to soften this rough treatment of he with the offer of some hot - house flower for his sitting - room . 
 ' oh , I thank you ! ' I hear the garlanded victim lowing as I leave he to the squire 's mercy . 
 Janet follow I out . ' it be my fault , Harry . you will not   blame he , I know . but will he fib ? I do not   think he be capable of it , and I be sure he can not   run and double . Grandada will have he fast before a minute be over . ' 
 I tell she to lose no time in go and extract the squire 's promise that Peterborough should have his living , so much it seem possible to save . 
 she fly back , and in Peterborough 's momentary absence , do her work . nothing could save the unhappy gentleman from a distracting scene and much archaic English . the squire 's power of vituperation be notorious : he could be more than a match for roadside navvy and predatory tramp in cogency of epithet . Peterborough come to I drench , and wail that he have never hear such language , never dream of it . and to find himself the object of it ! and , bad , to be unable to conscientiously defend himself ! the pain to he be in the conscience , which be , like the spleen , a function whose us be only to be understand in its derangement . he have ease his conscience to every question right out , and he rejoice to I at the immense relief it give he . conscientiously , he could not deny that he know the squire 's objection to my being in my father 's society ; and he have connive at it ' for reason , my dear Harry , I can justify to God and man , but not I have to confess as much not , I grieve to say , to your grandfather . I attempt to do justice to the amiable quality of the absent . in a moment I be assail with epithet that ... and not a word be to be get in when he be so violent . one have to make up one 's mind to act Andromeda , and let he be the sea - monster , as somebody have say ; I forget the exact origin of the remark . ' 
 the squire certainly have a whole ocean at command . I string myself to pass through the same performance . to my astonishment I go unchallenged . Janet vehemently assert that she have mollify the angry old man , who , however , be dark of visage , though his tongue keep silence . he be gruff over his wine - glass the blandishment of his favourite do not brighten he . from his point of view he have be treat vilely , and he be apparently inclined to nurse his rancour and keep my fortune tremble in the balance . under these circumstance it be impossible for I to despatch a letter to Ottilia , though I find that I could write one now , and I sit in my room write all day , most eloquent stuff it be . the shadow of misfortune restore the sense of my heroical situation , which my father have extinguish , and this unlock the power of speech . I write so admirably that my wretchedness could enjoy the fine millinery I decorate it in . then to tear the noble composition to piece be a bitter gratification . Ottilia 's station repel and attract I mysteriously . I could not separate she from it , nor keep my love of she from the contention into which it throw I . in vain I rave , ' what be rank ? ' there be a magnet in it that could at least set I quiver and twisting , behave like a man spellbound , as madly as any hero of the ballad under a wizard 's charm . 
 at last the squire relieve we . he fix that side - cast cock 's eye of his on I , and say , ' where be your banker ' book , sir ? ' 
 I presume that it be with my banker , but do not suggest the possibility that my father might have it in his custody ; for he have a cheque - book of his own , and regulate our account . why not ? I think , and flush somewhat defiantly . the money be mine . 
 ' any objection to my seeing that book ? ' say the squire . 
 ' none whatever , sir . ' 
 he nod . I make it a point of honour to write for the book to be send down to I immediately . 
 the book arrive , and the squire hand it to I to break the cover , insist , ' you be sure you would not   rather not have I look at it ? ' 
 ' quite , ' I reply . the question of money be to I perfectly unimportant . I do not see a glimpse of danger in his peruse the list of my expense . 
 " cause I give you my word I know nothing about it now , ' he say . 
 I compliment he on his frank method of deal , and tell he to look at the book if he please , but with prudence sufficiently awake to check the declaration that I have not once look at it myself . 
 he open it . we have just assemble in the hall , where breakfast be lay during Winter , before a huge wood fire . Janet have her tooth on her low lip , watch the old man 's face . I do not condescend to be curious ; but when I turn my head to he he be puff through thin lip , and then his mouth crumple in a knob . he have see sight . 
 ' by George , I must have breakfast ' fore I go into this ! ' he exclaim , and star as if he have come out of an oven . 
 Dorothy Beltham remind he that Prayers have not be read . 
 ' Prayers ! ' he be about to objurgate , but affirmative her motion to ring the bell for the servant , and address Peterborough : ' you read them abroad every morning ? ' 
 Peterborough 's conscience start off on its inevitable jog - trot at a touch of the whip . ' a - yes ; that be oh , it be my office . ' he have to recollect with exactitude : 
 ' I should specify exception ; there be interval ... ' 
 ' please , open your Bible , ' the squire cut he short ; ' I do not   want a damned fine edge on everything . ' 
 partly for an admonition to he , or in pure nervousness , Peterborough blow his nose monstrously : an unlucky note ; nothing go well after it . ' a slight cold , ' he murmur and resume the note , and throw himself maniacally into it . the unexpected figure of Captain bulste on tiptoe , wear the ceremonial depressed air of intruder on these occasion , distract our attention for a moment . 
 ' fresh from ship , William ? ' the squire call out . 
 the captain ejaculate a big word , to judge of it from the aperture , but it be mute as his footing on the carpet , and he sit and gaze devoutly toward Peterborough , who have wait to see he take his seat , and must now , in his hurry to perform his duty , sweep the peccant little redbound book to the floor . ' here , I will have that , ' say the squire . ' allow I , sir , ' say Peterborough ; and they spring into a collision . 
 ' would you jump out of your pulpit to pick up an old woman 's umbrella ? ' the squire ask he in wrath , and mutter of require none of his clerical legerdemain with book of business . tear be in Peterborough 's eye . my aunt Dorothy 's eye dwelt kindly on he to encourage he , but the man 's irritable nose be again his enemy . 
 Captain Bulsted chance to say in the musical voice of inquiry : ' prayer be not yet over , be they ? ' 
 ' no , nor never will be with a parson blow his horn at this rate , ' the squire rejoin . ' and mind you , ' he say to Peterborough , after dismiss the servant , to whom my aunt Dorothy read the morning lesson apart , ' I would not have have this happen , sir , for money in lump . I have   always know I should hang the day when my house be not   bless in the morning by prayer . so do my father , and his before he . fiddle ! sir , you can not   expect young people to wear decent face when the parson 's hopping over the floor like a flea , and trumpet as if the organ - pipe would not   have the sermon at any price . you try to juggle I out of this book here . ' 
 ' on my ! indeed , sir , no ! ' Peterborough proclaim his innocence , and it be unlikely that the squire should have suspect he . 
 Captain Bulsted have come to we for his wife , whom he have not find at home on his arrival last midnight . 
 ' God bless my soul , ' say the squire , ' you do not   mean to tell I she be go off , William ? ' 
 ' oh ! dear , no , sir , ' say the captain , ' she be only cruise . ' 
 the squire recommend a draught of old ale . the captain accept it . his comportment be cheerful in a sober fashion , notwithstanding the transparent perturbation of his spirit . he answer my aunt Dorothy 's question relate to Julia simply and manfully , as become a gallant seaman , cordially excuse his wife for not have be at home to welcome he , with the singular plea , base on his knowledge of the sex , that the nearer she know he to be the less able be she to sit on her chair wait like Patience . he drink his ale from the hand of Sillabin , our impassive new butler , who have succeed Sewis , the squire tell he , like a Whig Ministry the Tory ; proof that thing be not improve . 
 ' I think , sir , thing be get well , ' say the captain . 
 ' the damnedest mistake ever make , William . how about the Fall of Man , then ? eh ? you talk like a heathen Radical . it be scripture say we be go from well to bad , and that be Tory doctrine . and stick to the good as long as you can ! why , William , you be a jolly bachelor once . ' 
 ' Sir , and ma'am , ' the captain bow to Dorothy Beltham , ' I have , thank to you , never know happiness but in marriage , and all I want be my wife . ' 
 the squire fret for Janet to depart . ' I ' m go , grandada , ' she say . ' you will oblige I by not attend to any matter of business to - day . give I that book of Harry 's to keep for you . ' 
 ' how d ' ye mean , my dear ? ' 
 ' it be bad work do on a Sunday , you know . ' 
 ' so it be . I will lock up the book . ' 
 ' I have your word for that , grandada , ' say Janet . 
 the lady retire , take Peterborough with they . 
 ' good - bye to the frock ! and now , William , out with your trouble , ' say the squire . 
 the captain 's eye be turn to the door my aunt Dorothy have pass through . 
 ' you remember the old custom , sir ! ' 
 ' ay , do I , William . sorry for you then ; infernally sorry for you now , that I be ! but you have   run your head into the halter . ' 
 ' I love she , sir ; I love she to distraction . let any man on earth say she be not an angel , I flatten he dead as his lie . by the way , sir , I be bind in duty to inform you I be speak of my wife . ' 
 ' to be sure you be , William , and a trim schooner - yacht she be . ' 
 ' she be off , sir ; she be off ! '

 CHAPTER XXXIX . I see my FATHER take the TIDE and AM carry on IT MYSELF 

 my father stand in the lobby of the Opera , hold a sort of open court , it appear to I , for a cluster of gentleman hang round he ; and I have presently to bow to greeting which be rather of a kind to flatter I , lead I to presume that he be respect as well as marvel at . the name of Mr. Serjeant Wedderburn , Mr. Jennings , Lord Alton , Sir Weeton Slater , Mr. Monterez Williams , Admiral Loftus , the Earl of Witlington , be among those which strike my ear , and strike I as good one .  CHAPTER XL . MY FATHER 's meeting with MY GRANDFATHER 

 my father 's pleasure on the day of our journey to Bulsted be to drive I out of London on a lofty open chariot , with which he make the circuit of the fashionable district , and cause innumerable head to turn . I would have prefer to go the way of other man , to be unnoticed , but I be subject to an occasional glowing of undefined satisfaction in the observance of the universally acknowledge harmony exist between his pretension , his taste and habit , and his person . he contrive by I know not what persuasiveness and simplicity of manner and speech to banish from I the idea that he be engage in play a high stake ; and though I know it , and he more than once admit it , there be an ease and mastery about he that afford I some degree of positive comfort still . I be still most securely attach to his fortune . suppose the ghost of dead Hector to have hang over his body when the inflame son of Peleus whirl he at his chariot wheel round Troy , he would , with his natural passion sober by Erebus , have have some of my reflection upon force and fate , and my partial sense of exhilaration in the tremendous speed of the course during the whole of the period my father term his Grand Parade .  we strike a station at a point half - way down to Bulsted , and find little Kiomi there , thunder in her brow , carry a bundle , and purchase a railway - ticket , not to travel in our direction . she give I the singular answer that she could not tell I where her people be ; nor would she tell I whither she be go , alone , and by rail .  the squire direct Uberly , act as his groom , to walk his horse up and down the turf front young Tom Eckerthy 's cottage , and I to remain where I be ; then hobble up to the door , follow at a leisurely march by my father . the door open . my father sweep the old man in before he , with a bow and flourish that admit of no contradiction , and the door close on they .  the cottage - door continue shut . the heath be darken . I hear a noise of wheel , and presently the unmistakable voice of Janet say , ' that must be Harry .  ' he will want money even to do that , ' I remark . 
 she murmur , ' be there not some annual income pay to he ? ' 
 her quick delicacy make her redden in allude so closely to his personal affair , and I love she for the nice feeling . ' it be not much , ' I say . the miserable attempt to repair the wrong do to he with this small annuity anger I and I remember , little please , the foolish expectation he found on this secret acknowledgement of the justice of his claim . ' we will not   talk of it , ' I pursue . ' I wish he have never touch it . I shall interdict he . ' 
 ' you would let he pay his debt with it , Harry ? ' 
 ' I be not sure , aunty , that he do not incur a great debt by accept it . ' 
 ' one 's wish would be , that he might not ever be in need of it . ' 
 ' ay , or never be care to find the key of it . ' 
 ' that must be waste of time , ' she say . 
 I mean something else , but it be useless to tell she so . 




 CHAPTER XLI . commencement of the SPLENDOURS and perplexitie of MY FATHER 's GRAND 

 PARADE 
 Janet , in reply to our inquiry as to the condition of the squire 's temper , point out in the newspaper a notification of a grand public Ball to be give by my father , the first of a series of three , and say that the squire have see it and shrug . she think there be no positive cause for alarm , even though my father should fail of his word ; but express her view decidedly , that it be an unfortunate move to bring he between the squire and I , and so she blame Captain Bulsted . this be partly for the reason that the captain and his wife , charm by my father , be for advocate his merit at the squire 's table : our ingenuity be ludicrously tax to mystify he on the subject of their extravagant eulogy . they tell he they have be invite , and be go to the great London Balls . 
 ' Subscription Balls ? ' ask the squire . 
 ' no , sir , ' rejoin the captain . 
 ' Tradesmen 's Balls , d ' ye call them , then ? ' 
 ' no , sir ; they be Balls give by a distinguished gentleman . ' 
 ' take care it be not another name for tradesman 's Balls , William . ' 
 ' I do not attend tradesman 's Balls , sir . ' 
 ' take care o ' that , William . ' 
 the captain be very angry . ' what , ' say he , turn to we , ' what do the squire mean by tell an officer of the Royal Navy that he be conduct his wife to a tradesman 's ball ? ' 
 Julia threaten malicious doing for the insult . she and the squire have a controversy upon the explication of the word gentleman , she describe my father 's appearance and manner to the life . ' now listen to I , squire . a gentleman , I say , be one you would say , if he be not   bear a duke , he ought to have be , and more shame to the title ! he turn the key of a lady 's heart with a twinkle of his eye . he be never mean what he have be yours . he be a true friend ; and if he do not   keep his word , you know in a jiffy it be the fault of affair ; and stand about five foot eleven : he be a full - blow man ' : and so forth . 
 the squire listen , and perspire at find the object of his abhorrence crown thus in the unassailable realm of the abstract . ' 
 ' three - quarter , William , ' say the squire ; ' there be about the computation for your gentleman 's creditor , I suspect . ' 
 ' ay , sir ; well , ' return the captain , to whom this kind of fencing in the dark be an affliction , ' we make it up in quality in quality . '

 Captain Bulsted bow . ' the last word to you , squire . ' 
 the squire nod . ' I will hand it to your wife , William . ' 
 Julia take it graciously . ' a perfect gentleman ! perfect ! confound his enemy ! ' 
 ' why , ma'am , you might keep from swearing , ' the squire bawl . 
 ' La ! squire , ' say she , ' why , do not   you know the National Anthem ? '

 the old man fondle she . I could have do the same , she bend over he with such homely sweetness . ' one comfort , you will not   go to these gingerbread Balls , ' he say .

 ' dance away , my dear ; I have   no objection . '

 the squire point at I .

 so say , I follow the lady . it be not the wise of speech , and happen , Captain Bulsted inform I , to be deliver in my father 's manner , for the squire pronounce emphatically that he see very little Beltham in I . the right course would have be for I to ask he then and there whether I have his consent to start for Germany . but I be the sport of resentment and apprehension ; and , indeed , I should not have go .  Janet keep out of my sight . I protest , it be an aim with the mark in sight , and not out of range . ' 
 he whip himself up to one of his oratorical frenzy , of which a cheque be the common fruit . the power of his persuasiveness in speech , back by the spectacle of his social accomplishment , continue to subdue I , and I protest only inwardly even when I know that he be gamble with fortune .  outside tattle and scandal declare , that the Club be originate to serve as a club for Lady Edbury , but I choose to have no opinion upon what I know nothing of .

Arabian Nights ! '

 I remember , when back in the London vortex , the curious soft beauty she win from cast up her eye to watch the descend feather , and the brilliant direct beam of those thick - browed , firm , clear eye , with her frown , and her set lip and brave figure , when she be in the act of strike to keep up a regular quick fusilade .  CHAPTER XLII . the MARQUIS of EDBURY and his PUPPET 

 I pass from man to man , hear hint and hesitation , alarm half - remark , presume to be address to one who could supply the remainder , and deduce consequence . there be a clear atmosphere in the street of Clubs . Jennings be the first of my father 's more intimate acquaintance to meet I frankly . he speak , though not with great seriousness , of the rumour of a possible prosecution . Sir Weeton Slater trip up to we with a mixed air of solicitude and restraint , ask whether I be well , and whether I have see the newspaper that morning ; and on my inform he that I have just come up from Riversley , on account of certain rumour , advise I to remain in town strictly for the present . he also hint at rumour of prosecution . ' the fact be ' he begin several time , render discreet , I suppose , by my juvenility , fierte , and repute wealth . 
 we be join by Admiral Loftus and Lord Alton . they query and counterquerie as to passage between my father and the newspaper , my father and the committee of his Club , preserve sufficient consideration for I to avoid the serious matter in all but distant allusion ; a point upon which the breeding of Mr. Serjeant Wedderburn be not so accurate a guide to he . an exciting public scandal soon gather knot of gossip in Clubland . we see Wedderburn break from a group some way down the pavement and pick up a fresh crumb of amusement at one of the doorstep . ' Roy Richmond be have his benefit to - day ! ' he say , and repeat this and that , half audible to I . for the rest , he pooh - poohe the idea of the Law intervene . his ' how d ' ye do , Mr. Richmond , how d ' ye do ? ' be almost congratulatory . ' I think we meet at your father 's table to - night ? it will not   be in the Tower , take my word for it . oh ! the paper ! there be no Act to compel a man to deny what appear in the paper . no such luck as the Tower ! though Littlepitt   Mr. Wedderburn 's nickname for our Premier   would be fool enough for that . he would . if he could turn attention from his Bill , he would do it . we should have to dine off Boleyn 's block : coquite horum obsonia he would say , eh ? " 
 Jennings espy my father 's carriage , and step to speak a word to the footman . he return , say , with a puff of his cheek : ' the Grand Monarque have be send his state equipage to give the old backbiting cripple Brisby an airing . he be for horse exercise to - day they have   drop he in Courtenay Square . there go Brisby . he would take the good Samaritan 's shilling to buy a flask of poison for he . he will use Roy 's carriage to fetch and carry for that venomous old woman Kane , I will swear . ' 
 ' she be a male in scripture , ' say Wedderburn , and this remind I of an anecdote that remind he of another , and after tell they , he hand round his hat for the laugh , as my father would have phrase it . 
 ' have her ladyship declare war ? ' Sir Weeton Slater inquire . 
 ' no , that be not her preliminary to wage it , ' Wedderburn reply . these high - pressure smart talker have a moment of dulness , and he bethink he that he must run into the Club for letter , and be busy at Westminster , where , if anything fresh occur between meridian and six o'clock , he should be glad , he say , to have word of it by messenger , that he might not be behind his Age . 
 the form of humour to express the speed of the world be common , but it strike I as a terrible illustration of my father 's . I have still a sense of pleasure in the thought that these intimate of his be gentleman who relish and , perhaps , really like he . they be not parasite ; not the kind of man find hang about vulgar profligate . 
 I quit they . Sir Weeton Slater walk half - a - dozen step beside I . ' may I presume on a friendly acquaintance with your father , Mr. Richmond ? ' he say . ' the fact be you will not be offend ? he be apt to lose his head , unless the Committee of Supply limit he very precisely . I be aware that there be no material necessity for any restriction .
